title: SWEEP-AP: evictAESGCMEncrypt in cmd/evict.go duplicates aesGCMEncrypt in pkg/transport/http/crypto.go
description:
Pattern: Copy-paste / violated DRY.

Location: cmd/cf/cmd/evict.go lines 615-634 (evictAESGCMEncrypt) and pkg/transport/http/crypto.go lines 54-74 (aesGCMEncrypt).

The cmd layer implements its own AES-256-GCM encrypt function (evictAESGCMEncrypt) that is byte-for-byte identical in behavior to aesGCMEncrypt in the http transport package. Both: accept a 32-byte key, generate a random nonce, return nonce||ciphertext, use gcm.Seal. The only difference is the function name and slightly different error messages.

The reason the cmd layer cannot call the pkg function directly is that aesGCMEncrypt is unexported. But HkdfSHA256 is exported and is already called from evict.go — the same export-level inconsistency that forced the copy.

Why it matters: Two places to maintain the same crypto primitive. A bug fix to one will not apply to the other. The cmd layer is supposed to be a thin CLI wrapper — it should not contain cryptographic primitives at all.

Better pattern: Export aesGCMEncrypt (rename to AESGCMEncrypt) from pkg/transport/http/crypto.go and remove evictAESGCMEncrypt from evict.go. The cmd layer should call the package function, not reimplement it.
close_reason: Implemented: exported AESGCMEncrypt in pkg/transport/http/crypto.go, replaced duplicate evictAESGCMEncrypt in cmd/evict.go. -20 lines.

title: SWEEP-TEST: handleDeliver — malformed CBOR body after valid signature returns 400 but untested
description: handler_message.go lines 49-53: after signature verification and membership check pass, the body is decoded as CBOR. If the body is valid CBOR for the signature but not a valid message.Message, the handler returns 400. No test sends a valid-signature, valid-CBOR, but wrong-type payload (e.g., a CBOR map that doesn't decode into message.Message). This path could mask bugs in cfencoding.Unmarshal. Done condition: a test that sends a validly-signed but structurally-invalid CBOR body to /deliver and verifies 400.

title: SWEEP-TEST: handleJoin - no test for invite-only campfire rejection
description:
File: pkg/transport/http/handler.go handleJoin()

handleJoin serves any join request without checking whether the campfire join_protocol is open or invite-only. The join_protocol field exists in the JoinResponse but is not enforced server-side. Any agent with a valid identity can call POST /campfire/{id}/join and receive the encrypted private key if the key provider is set.

Missing tests:
- invite-only campfire: handleJoin should reject joiners not pre-approved (currently no enforcement exists)
- No key provider set: returns 501 (tested indirectly but no explicit test)
- Invalid EphemeralX25519Pub hex: returns 400 (not tested)
- campfire membership not found in store: returns 404 (not tested)
- Join request with no EphemeralX25519Pub: JoinResponse.EncryptedPrivKey should be nil (not tested)

The invite-only gap is P1: it means the p2p-http transport ignores the join_protocol and distributes the campfire private key to any requester who can sign an HTTP request.

Done: tests for each missing path; join_protocol enforcement added or explicitly documented as out-of-scope with a tracking bead.
close_reason: Fixed by workspace-972 — test added for invite-only rejection (TestJoinInviteOnlyForbidden)

title: SWEEP-BUG: GetMessageByPrefix stops collecting after second match but rows.Close() is still deferred — cursor leaks until GC
description:
In store.go GetMessageByPrefix(), the query fetches rows via a SQL LIKE prefix match. When a second match is found (len(matches) > 1), the function returns an 'ambiguous prefix' error immediately. The defer rows.Close() will eventually clean this up, but the underlying SQLite statement is not closed until the GC finalizes the rows object. Under high throughput, this causes SQLite to hold open cursors/read transactions longer than necessary.

More critically, when more than 2 rows match the prefix, the function fetches ALL of them via rows.Next() until the 'ambiguous' branch fires on the second one. After the first append, it keeps calling rows.Next() in the loop body even though it already has > 1 match, due to the if len(matches) > 1 check being inside the loop. The query reads all matching rows from SQLite even though only 2 are needed to declare ambiguity.

Trigger: any GetMessageByPrefix call where the prefix matches 3+ messages. 
Impact: P2 - unnecessary DB reads; open cursor leak per call until GC.
Fix: break the rows.Next() loop immediately after detecting len(matches) > 1, before calling return.

title: SWEEP-SEC: handleDeliver and handleSync accept messages from any valid Ed25519 signer (no membership check)
description:
Severity: CRITICAL

handleDeliver (POST /campfire/{id}/deliver) and handleSync (GET /campfire/{id}/sync) only verify that the sender has a valid Ed25519 keypair — they do NOT check whether the sender is a member of the campfire.

Anyone who generates a valid Ed25519 keypair can:
- Deliver arbitrary messages to any campfire they know the ID of
- Read (sync) all messages from any campfire they know the ID of

Only handlePoll performs a membership check (lines 267-283 in handler.go).

Relevant code: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go
- handleDeliver: lines 121-177 — signature verified but no membership check
- handleSync: lines 181-233 — signature verified but no membership check
- handlePoll: lines 246-377 — DOES perform membership check (only endpoint that does)

Impact: Broken access control. Any actor knowing the campfire ID can inject messages and read all history. The campfire ID (Ed25519 public key hex) is discoverable via beacons and membership events.

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.
close_reason: Fixed: added checkMembership() helper and applied it to handleDeliver and handleSync. Non-members now get 403. 2 new tests added (TestDeliverNonMemberForbidden, TestSyncNonMemberForbidden).

title: SWEEP-BUG: GetMessageByPrefix reads extra rows after finding ambiguity
description:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/store/store.go, GetMessageByPrefix (line 266-295)

The query fetches all matching rows from the DB (no LIMIT clause), but the ambiguity check reads up to len(matches) > 1 and returns an error. If there are 1000 matches, it reads all 1000 rows before returning the error. 

Worse, when 2+ matches are found, the error is returned before rows.Close() can be called via defer. Actually, rows.Close() IS deferred, so that part is OK. But the real issue is: the query uses `ORDER BY id` with no `LIMIT 2`, so for a short prefix matching many messages, the DB scans and returns all matches. The correct fix is to use `LIMIT 2` in the query so the DB stops after finding 2 matches.

Impact: performance degradation under high message volumes when a user provides a very short prefix that matches many messages. Not a correctness bug but a resource waste issue.
close_reason: Fixed: GetMessageByPrefix LIMIT 2 added (befb820)

title: SWEEP-BUG: PutFile in github client reads then writes without atomic check - TOCTOU race
description:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/github/client.go, PutFile (line 279-328)

PutFile first GETs the file to retrieve its SHA (line 285-297), then PUTs the file with the SHA (line 305-327). Between the GET and the PUT, another process could update the file on GitHub. The PUT would then fail with a 409 Conflict (SHA mismatch).

The code does not handle this case:
  if resp.StatusCode != http.StatusCreated && resp.StatusCode != http.StatusOK {
      b, _ := io.ReadAll(resp.Body)
      return fmt.Errorf("put file: HTTP %d: %s", resp.StatusCode, b)
  }

A 409 from GitHub when two agents simultaneously publish beacons would surface as an opaque error. There is no retry logic.

Additionally, the getResp.Body is read only when StatusCode == 200 (line 292-295), but for other status codes (e.g. 404 meaning file does not exist yet, which is valid for new files), the body is not read. This is fine since existingSHA stays empty ("") in that case.

However, getResp.Body.Close() on line 297 is called unconditionally, which is correct.

The TOCTOU window is inherent in GitHub's API and is typically handled with retry-on-conflict logic. Missing retry means concurrent beacon updates silently fail.
close_reason: Fixed: PutFile now retries once on HTTP 409 Conflict to handle TOCTOU races

title: SWEEP-TEST: checkMembership — store.ListPeerEndpoints error path silently passes as non-member
description: handler.go lines 132-151: checkMembership calls store.ListPeerEndpoints and if it returns an error, the loop is skipped — the sender is then rejected as not a member (403). This means a transient store error causes a legitimate member to be locked out. This behavior is correct for security (fail-closed) but is untested. More importantly, no test verifies what happens when checkMembership is called for a campfire_id that has no membership record (the store returns an empty list, not an error). The function should return 403 in this case — it does, but no test covers it. Done condition: a test where ListPeerEndpoints returns an error (e.g., closed DB) and verifies the handler returns 403, not 500 or 200.

title: No mechanism to change an existing member's role
description:
FINDING from workspace-o3l.2.2 review.

admit.go rejects members who are already present ("already a member"). There is no cf admit --role for existing members and no cf member role-set command. To change a member role you must directly edit the CBOR file in the transport directory.

This is a critical UX gap for the observer/writer/full role system: you can assign a role at admit time, but you cannot promote or demote a member afterward.

Done condition: A new command (or --force flag on admit) that updates the Role field of an existing member record in the transport directory. Tests verify role upgrade/downgrade persists through CBOR round-trip.

Parent: workspace-o3l.2
close_reason: Deferred: role change mechanism is a UX enhancement, not a bug.

title: Evictions decrypt successfully — deliverRekey applies HKDF to match handler
description:
**Fixes:** workspace-a4j (bug), workspace-z3b (sec)

**Problem:** `deliverRekey()` in `cmd/cf/cmd/evict.go:577` passes the raw X25519 ECDH shared secret directly to `evictAESGCMEncrypt()`. The receiver side in `pkg/transport/http/handler_rekey.go:166` applies `hkdfSHA256(rawShared, "campfire-rekey-v1")` before decryption. The derived keys never match — **every eviction rekey delivery silently fails**.

**Done condition:** A test sends a rekey from evict→handler with ECDH+HKDF on both sides and decryption succeeds. The existing `TestRekeyIntegration` (if any) passes. `go test ./...` green.

**Files to modify:**
- `cmd/cf/cmd/evict.go` — line ~577: after `senderPriv.ECDH(receiverPub)`, apply `cfhttp.HkdfSHA256(sharedSecret, "campfire-rekey-v1")` (or import the function). The `evictAESGCMEncrypt` call on lines 592/599 then uses the derived key.
- May need to export `hkdfSHA256` from `pkg/transport/http/crypto.go` or duplicate the 5-line HKDF call.

**Test approach:** Integration test — evict command delivers rekey to a test HTTP handler, handler decrypts successfully.

**Constraints:**
- Do NOT change the handler side — it already does HKDF correctly.
- Do NOT change the HKDF info string — it must be "campfire-rekey-v1" on both sides.
- Write failing test first, then fix.

**Agent type:** implementer
**Model tier:** sonnet

title: Auth preamble centralized as middleware — no copy-paste across handlers
description:
**Fixes:** workspace-t27 (antipattern P1)

**Problem:** Every HTTP handler (deliver, sync, poll, membership, sign, rekey) copy-pastes the same auth preamble: read body, get X-Campfire-Sender/Signature headers, call verifyRequestSignature, call checkMembership. 5 files, ~15 lines each. Adding any auth concern (rate limiting, nonce checking, logging) requires touching all handlers.

**Done condition:**
1. Auth logic (signature verification + membership check) is in a middleware or shared function called by the router.
2. Each handler receives a pre-authenticated context (verified senderHex available without re-checking).
3. All existing handler tests still pass.
4. `go test ./...` green.

**Files to modify:**
- `pkg/transport/http/handler.go` — add auth middleware that wraps handler functions
- `pkg/transport/http/handler_message.go` — remove auth preamble from handleDeliver, handleSync, handlePoll, handleMembership
- `pkg/transport/http/handler_join.go` — remove auth preamble (join has special auth — no membership check, just signature)
- `pkg/transport/http/handler_sign.go` — remove auth preamble
- `pkg/transport/http/handler_rekey.go` — remove auth preamble

**Design:**
- Middleware pattern: `func authMiddleware(next func(w, r, campfireID, senderHex string)) http.HandlerFunc`
- handleJoin is special: it needs signature verification but NOT membership check (joiners aren't members yet). Use a separate `signatureOnly` middleware, or pass a flag.
- Store verified `senderHex` in request context.

**Constraints:**
- This is a refactor — no behavioral changes except where security fixes (workspace-17qu.2, .4, .6) are already applied.
- Depends on all security implementation beads being complete first.
- Do NOT change the wire protocol or HTTP API.

**Agent type:** implementer
**Model tier:** sonnet
close_reason: Auth middleware centralized. 4 files refactored, auth preamble eliminated from handlers. Commit 2fe6b7c.

title: Only campfire creator can trigger rekey; rekey signature verified against stored key
description:
**Fixes:** workspace-x07 (sec), workspace-7sr (sec)

**Problem 1 (workspace-x07):** `handleRekey` in `pkg/transport/http/handler_rekey.go` checks membership but NOT creator role. Any member can rename the campfire ID, evict peers, and rewrite state by sending a rekey request.

**Problem 2 (workspace-7sr):** Line 262-263 verifies the rekey message signature using `rekeyMsg.VerifySignature()`, which checks `rekeyMsg.Sender` — an attacker-controlled CBOR field. The verification should use the **stored** campfire public key (the old campfire ID is a hex Ed25519 public key), not a field from the request body.

**Done condition:**
1. A non-creator member sending a rekey request receives 403 Forbidden.
2. A rekey message with a forged Sender field (different from the campfire public key) is rejected.
3. `go test ./...` green.

**Files to modify:**
- `pkg/transport/http/handler_rekey.go`:
  - After membership check (~line 85): query `store.GetMembership(oldCampfireID)` for the sender's role, reject if not creator. Or: check if `senderHex` matches the campfire's creator pubkey stored in membership.
  - Lines 260-265: Replace `rekeyMsg.VerifySignature()` with explicit verification: decode `oldCampfireID` hex to get the expected Ed25519 public key, verify `rekeyMsg.Signature` over the canonical sign input using that key.
- `pkg/store/store.go` — may need a method to look up creator pubkey for a campfire, or use existing `GetMembership` which returns role info.

**Test approach:**
- Test 1: Non-creator member sends rekey → 403.
- Test 2: Rekey message with forged Sender passes `VerifySignature()` but is rejected by the handler.
- Test 3: Legitimate creator rekey still works.

**Constraints:**
- Do NOT change the evict CLI command — it already checks creator role client-side. This is server-side enforcement.
- The creator check must use the stored creator identity, not just "is this member the first one who joined."
- Write failing tests first.

**Agent type:** implementer
**Model tier:** sonnet
close_reason: Fixed: creator-only rekey + sig verified against stored campfire key. Commit 40643ad.

title: Rekey state update is atomic — DB failure cannot orphan campfire
description:
**Fixes:** workspace-fn8 (bug)

**Problem:** In `pkg/transport/http/handler_rekey.go` phase 2 (lines 201-233), the handler:
1. Writes new state file (line 223)
2. Removes old state file (line 225)
3. THEN calls `h.store.UpdateCampfireID()` (line 229)

If the DB update fails at step 3, the peer has a new state file, no old state file, and an outdated DB — the campfire is unrecoverable.

**Done condition:** If `UpdateCampfireID` fails, the old state file still exists and the new state file is cleaned up. The campfire remains in its pre-rekey state. Test verifies this by injecting a store error.

**Files to modify:**
- `pkg/transport/http/handler_rekey.go` lines 201-233: Reorder to:
  1. Update DB first (`UpdateCampfireID`)
  2. On success: write new state file, remove old state file
  3. On DB failure: return error, leave files untouched
  - Also: the `os.WriteFile` on line 223 and `os.Remove` on line 225 should check errors — if new file write fails, roll back the DB change (or at minimum, don't remove the old file).

**Test approach:** Integration test with a mock/failing store that errors on `UpdateCampfireID`. Verify old state file still exists, new state file does not.

**Constraints:**
- Keep the fix minimal — reorder operations, add error checks.
- Do NOT introduce a transaction abstraction — just reorder.
- Write failing test first.

**Agent type:** implementer
**Model tier:** sonnet
close_reason: Fixed: DB update before file ops in handleRekey. Commit 7d4d1b1.

title: Join handler persists verified sender identity, not unverified body field
description:
**Fixes:** workspace-t24 (sec)

**Problem:** `handleJoin` in `pkg/transport/http/handler_join.go` line 264-270 persists `req.JoinerPubkey` (unverified JSON body field) instead of `senderHex` (verified from `X-Campfire-Sender` header, signature-checked). An attacker can inject any pubkey into the peer list by signing the request with their own key but putting a different pubkey in the body.

**Done condition:**
1. `UpsertPeerEndpoint` and `AddPeer` calls use `senderHex` instead of `req.JoinerPubkey`.
2. A test where `req.JoinerPubkey != senderHex` results in `senderHex` being stored, not the body field.
3. `go test ./...` green.

**Files to modify:**
- `pkg/transport/http/handler_join.go` lines 264-270:
  ```go
  // Before (vulnerable):
  MemberPubkey: req.JoinerPubkey,
  h.transport.AddPeer(campfireID, req.JoinerPubkey, req.JoinerEndpoint)
  
  // After (fixed):
  MemberPubkey: senderHex,
  h.transport.AddPeer(campfireID, senderHex, req.JoinerEndpoint)
  ```
- Consider: should `req.JoinerPubkey` be validated to match `senderHex`? If they differ, that's suspicious — could return 400. But the minimal fix is to just use the verified value.

**Test approach:**
- Test: Send join request signed by key A, but with `JoinerPubkey` set to key B. Verify key A is stored, not key B.
- Test: Normal join where they match still works.

**Constraints:**
- Minimal change — just swap the field used for storage.
- Do NOT remove the `JoinerPubkey` field from the request struct (backward compat).
- Write failing test first.

**Agent type:** implementer
**Model tier:** sonnet
close_reason: Fixed: handleJoin uses verified senderHex for peer storage. Commit c8e68ba.

title: FROST signing sessions are thread-safe — Start/Deliver under lock in round 1
description:
**Fixes:** workspace-zid (bug), workspace-pi9 (bug)

**Problem:** In `pkg/transport/http/handler_sign.go` round 1 (lines 86-114):
1. `getOrCreateSignSession()` is called under write lock (line 86-88)
2. Lock is released (line 88)
3. `ss.Start()` called WITHOUT lock (line 96)
4. `ss.Deliver()` called WITHOUT lock (lines 100-104)

Concurrent round-1 requests for the same session_id call `Start()` and `Deliver()` concurrently on the same `SigningSession`, which is not goroutine-safe. This can corrupt FROST state machine state.

**Done condition:**
1. Round 1 holds the lock (or a per-session lock) across `Start()` and `Deliver()`.
2. A race detector test (`go test -race`) with concurrent round-1 requests passes.
3. `go test ./...` green.

**Files to modify:**
- `pkg/transport/http/handler_sign.go` lines 84-114: Extend the lock scope to cover `Start()` and `Deliver()` calls. Two options:
  - Option A: Hold `t.mu` across the entire round-1 block (simple but coarse — blocks all other sign sessions).
  - Option B: Add a per-session `sync.Mutex` to `signingSessionState` and lock it for the duration of Start/Deliver.
  - Option B is preferred — it allows concurrent sessions for different session_ids.

**Test approach:**
- Race test: Launch 3 goroutines sending round-1 to the same session_id concurrently. Run with `-race`. Must not panic or corrupt state.
- Correctness test: Single round-1 request still produces valid commitments.

**Constraints:**
- Do NOT change the round-2 path — it already holds the write lock correctly.
- The per-session lock must be created atomically with the session in `getOrCreateSignSession`.
- Write failing test (race detector) first.

**Agent type:** implementer
**Model tier:** sonnet
close_reason: Fixed: per-session mutex for FROST round-1. Commit b0b6c0a.

title: Membership join events use verified sender identity, not body field
description:
**Fixes:** workspace-99w (sec, P2 — elevated because it enables identity injection)

**Problem:** `handleMembership` in `pkg/transport/http/handler_message.go` lines 300-305 accepts `event.Member` from the request body for join events. This is an attacker-controlled field — the sender is verified via `X-Campfire-Sender`, but the member being added to the peer list comes from the unverified body. A member can inject arbitrary pubkeys into peer lists and set arbitrary endpoints (SSRF vector).

**Done condition:**
1. For "join" events, `event.Member` is validated: either it must equal `senderHex`, or the handler uses `senderHex` as the member identity.
2. For "leave"/"evict" events from a creator, the body field is acceptable (creator evicts others).
3. Test: join event with mismatched member field is rejected.
4. `go test ./...` green.

**Files to modify:**
- `pkg/transport/http/handler_message.go` lines 300-305:
  - For "join": validate `event.Member == senderHex` or use `senderHex`.
  - For "leave": validate `event.Member == senderHex` (you can only leave yourself).
  - For "evict": more nuanced — the sender must be the creator. Check against stored role.

**Test approach:**
- Test: Join event with `event.Member != senderHex` → 400 or uses senderHex.
- Test: Normal join event works.

**Constraints:**
- Minimal change — add validation, don't restructure.
- Write failing test first.

**Agent type:** implementer
**Model tier:** sonnet
close_reason: Fixed: join/leave require member==sender. Commit e62d3c5.

title: FROST signing has e2e test over HTTP and sign handler edge cases covered
description:
**Fixes:** workspace-ir0 (test), workspace-cnw (test)

**Gaps:**
1. `RunFROSTSign` has no end-to-end test over real HTTP transports — only auth rejection is tested.
2. `handleSign` round-2-without-round-1 returns 400 but no test covers it.

**Done condition:**
1. Integration test: 2-of-3 FROST signing over real HTTP transport instances. Starts sign session, completes rounds 1+2, produces valid group signature.
2. Test: round-2 request without prior round-1 → 400 "signing session not found".

title: SWEEP-SEC: evict command role check is local-only; any agent can claim creator role by manipulating local store
description:
Severity: MEDIUM

The evict command (evict.go line 65) checks m.Role == "creator" against the local SQLite store. The role field is set locally when the user creates or joins a campfire — it is not cryptographically bound to any campfire-signed credential.

Attack:
1. Agent A joins campfire C (gets role "full")
2. Agent A directly edits ~/.campfire/store.db: UPDATE campfire_memberships SET role = "creator" WHERE campfire_id = ?
3. Agent A runs `cf evict C <victimPubkey>` — eviction proceeds
4. Agent A sends rekey notifications to all remaining peers, replacing their campfire key

This is feasible because:
- SQLite DB is at a known path (~/.campfire/store.db)
- No integrity protection on the store
- No server-side authorization check in handleRekey (see workspace-j4j)

More broadly: the "creator" role is self-asserted via local state — there is no mechanism for other nodes to verify that an eviction request came from the legitimate creator.

Relevant code: /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/evict.go lines 57-66

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.
close_reason: Documented as known limitation: evict role check is client-side only; server-side verification requires protocol changes tracked separately

title: SWEEP-BUG: handlePoll allows up to 120s wait but server WriteTimeout is 60s — poll killed mid-wait
description:
File: pkg/transport/http/handler_message.go + pkg/transport/http/transport.go

In transport.go, the HTTP server is created with WriteTimeout: 60 * time.Second (line 95).

In handler_message.go, handlePoll caps the poll timeout at 120 seconds (if timeoutSec > 120 { timeoutSec = 120 }). The default is 30s. The handler blocks on a select for up to timeoutSec seconds (line 186), THEN writes the response.

In Go net/http, WriteTimeout is measured from when the server finishes reading the request headers and starts handling — it includes the time the handler runs before writing anything. For a long-poll with timeoutSec > 55s (60s WriteTimeout minus ~5s for response writing), the server kills the connection with a deadline exceeded error before the handler can write the 204/200 response.

Impact:
- Any poll with timeout 61-120s (or even ~56-120s with margin) is killed mid-wait.
- The client sees a broken connection error, not a clean 204 timeout.
- runNATPoll in read.go calls Poll() with timeoutSecs up to 30s (default), so the default path is safe. But a client requesting ?timeout=90 will always fail.
- The cap at 120 is lying: it implies the server supports 120s polls, but WriteTimeout kills them at 60s.

Conditions: any poll request with timeout > ~55 seconds.

Fix: Either lower the max poll timeout cap to 50s (safe below WriteTimeout), or increase WriteTimeout to 130s+ for the polling server, or implement http.Hijacker/streaming to bypass the deadline.
close_reason: Implemented: cap poll timeout at 50s (under 60s WriteTimeout), add explicit ReadTimeout/WriteTimeout/IdleTimeout to http.Server.

title: SWEEP-SEC: handleSign has no authorization check — any valid signer can participate in FROST signing sessions
description:
Severity: HIGH

POST /campfire/{id}/sign verifies the request signature (valid Ed25519 keypair) but does NOT verify that the sender is a member of the campfire or has permission to initiate/participate in threshold signing sessions.

Attack:
1. Attacker learns a threshold campfire ID
2. Attacker sends a round-1 sign request — creates a signing session on the co-signer
3. Attacker can attempt to complete threshold signatures on arbitrary messages
4. Session state accumulates in memory (map keyed by sessionID) — no limit on concurrent sessions, DoS via session exhaustion

Additionally, the sessionID is attacker-supplied (arbitrary string). A malicious co-signer could collide session IDs with legitimate sessions, potentially corrupting signing state.

Relevant code: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go handleSign() lines 610-746

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.
close_reason: Fixed: handleSign now verifies sender is a campfire member before allowing participation in FROST signing sessions. Non-members get 403. Test: TestSignNonMemberForbidden.

title: Bug: one-shot read/dag cursor advances on filtered messages, causing re-display
description:
In cmd/cf/cmd/read.go one-shot mode (lines 421-496), allMessages is reassigned to the filtered result on line 422 before cursor advancement on lines 486-496. This means when using --tag or --sender filters, filtered-out messages do not advance the cursor, so they re-appear on the next cf read invocation. The --follow mode correctly separates pre-filter (newMessages) and post-filter (filteredMessages) and advances cursors based on pre-filter data (lines 361-389).

Same bug exists in cmd/cf/cmd/dag.go: line 68 filters, then lines 77-85 advance cursor on filtered result.

Fix: In one-shot read, save the pre-filter messages, apply filterMessages for display, but advance cursor based on the pre-filter set. Same for dag.go.
close_reason: Fixed in c25edc6. Regression test added in 1e97f93.

title: SWEEP-TEST: handleRekey phase-2 with corrupt AES-GCM ciphertext returns 400 but untested
description: handler_rekey.go lines 184-199: if the AES-GCM decryption of EncryptedPrivKey fails (tampered, wrong key, truncated), the handler returns 400. No test sends a phase-2 request with a ciphertext that was not encrypted with the derived HKDF key. The aesGCMDecrypt function is unit-tested in crypto_test.go, but the handler integration path (wrong shared secret → decryption failure → 400) is not. Done condition: a test where phase-1 completes but phase-2 is sent with a ciphertext encrypted under a different key → 400 Bad Request.

title: SWEEP-BUG: handleMembership evict silently skips creator check when GetMembership fails or returns nil
description:
File: pkg/transport/http/handler_message.go, lines 236-244

The evict case in handleMembership reads:

    membership, err := h.store.GetMembership(campfireID)
    if err == nil && membership != nil && membership.CreatorPubkey != "" && senderHex != membership.CreatorPubkey {
        http.Error(w, "only the campfire creator may evict members", http.StatusForbidden)
        return
    }
    h.transport.RemovePeer(campfireID, event.Member)

The creator check is skipped entirely when:
1. GetMembership returns an error (DB failure) — err != nil → condition false → no check
2. GetMembership returns nil membership (campfire not found on this node) — membership == nil → condition false → no check
3. membership.CreatorPubkey is empty (legacy/migrated records) — condition false → no check

In cases 1 and 2, any authenticated member (who passes the authMiddleware membership check via the peer list) can send an evict event and remove any other member from the in-memory peer routing table, without being the creator.

Impact:
- Any member of a campfire can remove any other member from the in-memory peer list without being the creator.
- The evicted peer loses ability to receive messages routed through this node until it reconnects.
- In case 3 (empty CreatorPubkey), this is explicitly allowed for backward compat, which is a design decision. Cases 1 and 2 are bugs.

Conditions: DB error on GetMembership (transient or corrupt), or campfire record not in this node's store (a peer receiving a membership event for a campfire it just joined via rekey may not yet have the membership record).

Fix: Change the condition to enforce the check whenever the membership record is available:
    if err != nil {
        http.Error(w, "internal error verifying membership", http.StatusInternalServerError)
        return
    }
    if membership == nil {
        http.Error(w, "not a member of this campfire", http.StatusNotFound)
        return
    }
    if membership.CreatorPubkey != "" && senderHex != membership.CreatorPubkey {
        http.Error(w, "only the campfire creator may evict members", http.StatusForbidden)
        return
    }
close_reason: Duplicate of workspace-vf9u (same finding)

title: SWEEP-AP: inline migration ALTER TABLE statements bypass schema versioning
description:
pkg/store/store.go Open() function contains inline ALTER TABLE migrations at lines 113-116:

```go
db.Exec("ALTER TABLE messages ADD COLUMN instance TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT ''") //nolint:errcheck
db.Exec("ALTER TABLE campfire_memberships ADD COLUMN description TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT ''") //nolint:errcheck
```

These are silently ignored migrations (errors suppressed with nolint). Problems:
1. There is no schema version tracking - if a migration is needed in the future, there is no record of which migrations have run.
2. The nolint comments hide the fact that these migrations will fail on every subsequent open (SQLite returns an error when a column already exists). The failure is expected but is invisible.
3. New migrations added to this list will silently fail if they conflict, with no developer feedback.

A proper migration strategy (even a simple version integer in a metadata table) or use of a migrations library would make this explicit and safe.

Severity: MEDIUM - fragile migration path; first sign of trouble is silent data corruption when a new migration conflicts with existing columns.
close_reason: Fixed: schema migration versioning added (befb820)

title: SWEEP-SEC: SSRF via DNS rebinding — joiner endpoint validation races DNS resolution
description: validateJoinerEndpoint() in pkg/transport/http/handler.go resolves the hostname at validation time (line 45: net.LookupHost(host)) and checks the resolved IPs against private ranges. However, the validation result is not used for the actual outbound connection — the raw URL string is stored in the peer_endpoints table and later used to make outbound HTTP requests (e.g., NotifyMembership, Deliver). An attacker controlling a DNS record with a very short TTL can pass validation by resolving to a public IP, then change the DNS record to a private IP before the server makes the actual outbound connection (DNS rebinding / TOCTOU). This allows a campfire member to redirect server-initiated HTTP requests to internal services (SSRF). Impact: P2 because (1) it requires DNS control, (2) the attacker must already be a campfire member, and (3) the attacker only controls the initial membership notification payload. Mitigation: resolve the hostname at connection time and validate again, or use a DNS resolver that pins the result.
close_reason: Duplicate of workspace-lm43 (same finding)

title: SWEEP-TEST: handleJoin threshold>1 — pending share claim path has no test
description: handler_join.go lines 207-225: when membership.Threshold > 1, the handler calls store.ClaimPendingThresholdShare to distribute a FROST DKG share to the joiner. This entire code path is untested. Specifically missing: (a) a test that a joiner receives ThresholdShareData in the JoinResponse when threshold>1 and a pending share is available, (b) a test that the joiner's participant ID is correctly persisted in peer_endpoints, (c) a test that joining when no pending share is available does not panic or return an error to the joiner (the code returns 500 on ClaimPendingThresholdShare error — behavior when shareData==nil is to silently skip, but this is not tested). This is P1 because it affects the FROST threshold join flow — the primary mechanism for distributing key material to new members in threshold campfires.

title: SWEEP-AP: pkg/campfire imports pkg/identity creating upward coupling from core domain to infrastructure
description:
Pattern: Leaky abstraction / package coupling direction.

Location: pkg/campfire/campfire.go line 10 — imports 'github.com/campfire-net/campfire/pkg/identity'.

pkg/campfire is the core domain package (campfire identity, member management, roles). pkg/identity is an infrastructure package (Ed25519 keypair generation, file persistence, X25519 conversion).

The Campfire struct embeds *identity.Identity directly (line 38). This means:
1. pkg/campfire cannot be used without pkg/identity — a lower-level package depends on a higher-level one.
2. Consumers of pkg/campfire (the cmd layer, tests) must transitively take on pkg/identity's filesystem and crypto dependencies.
3. The Identity field is tagged json:"-" (excluded from JSON) but is used for campfire key operations. This suggests the campfire domain concept (a keypair owner) is being tangled with the identity infrastructure (how to generate/persist/load a keypair).

Ideally the dependency arrow should point the other way: identity knows nothing about campfire, and campfire takes raw keys ([]byte) rather than *identity.Identity.

Why it's problematic: Any refactor of the Identity type (new fields, different persistence format) forces changes in the campfire package. Testing campfire logic requires constructing identity.Identity values.

Suggested direction: Replace the *identity.Identity field in Campfire with raw PublicKey/PrivateKey []byte fields. Move identity generation to the call sites (create.go already calls identity.Generate via campfire.New). The campfire package becomes infrastructure-independent.

title: SWEEP-BUG: isCompactionMsg uses strings.Contains vs HasTag in store - inconsistent tag detection
description:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/compact.go line 239-241 vs pkg/store/store.go line 732

Two different implementations detect campfire:compact tags:
- compact.go isCompactionMsg: strings.Contains(m.Tags, "\"campfire:compact\"")  
- store.go isCompactionEvent: HasTag(rec.Tags, "campfire:compact") via JSON parse

These are used in different paths: isCompactionMsg is the WRITE path (determining what to supersede when creating a compaction event), isCompactionEvent is the READ path (filtering superseded messages).

If a future tag like "campfire:compact-v2" is introduced, strings.Contains would falsely match it (the JSON "campfire:compact-v2" does not contain the literal "campfire:compact" with trailing quote, so actually safe for v2 case). The real danger is a tag value ending in campfire:compact such as "new-campfire:compact" stored as JSON gives ["new-campfire:compact"] which does contain the substring "campfire:compact" but NOT the search string "\"campfire:compact\"" (the leading quote differs).

The critical actual bug: these two implementations will diverge if they are ever updated independently, and since they serve the two halves of the same invariant (what gets superseded vs. what gets hidden), a split would cause read/write inconsistency where compact creates a supersedes list excluding a compaction event that the read path WOULD have included (or vice versa). This is a latent maintenance bug that should be unified to a single source of truth.
close_reason: Fixed: isCompactionMsg in compact.go now uses store.HasTag (exact JSON element match) instead of strings.Contains, unified with isCompactionEvent in store.go. Committed in befb820.

title: SWEEP-TEST: handleSync and handlePoll invalid query parameters not tested

Timeout capping at 120s is also untested. Done condition: tests for invalid 'since' and 'timeout' params returning 400, and timeout>120 being capped.

description:
The swarm.go help text documents 'cf swarm status' as available, but the subcommand is not implemented:

title: SWEEP-BUG: compact command stores compaction event twice for P2P-HTTP campfires (duplicate insert)
description:
In compact.go execCompact(), line 217 calls s.AddMessage(...) unconditionally after sendP2PHTTP/sendFilesystem/sendGitHub. However, sendP2PHTTP() already calls s.AddMessage() at line 355 of send.go. For P2P-HTTP campfires, the compaction message is therefore inserted into the SQLite store twice.

The second insert is protected by INSERT OR IGNORE (AddMessage returns (false, nil) for a duplicate), so no data is corrupted. However, the supersededCache is invalidated based on maxCompactionTimestamp — since the second insert is a no-op, the cache logic remains correct.

Still, the duplicate call path is a latent bug waiting for the INSERT OR IGNORE protection to be removed, and it produces redundant DB write attempts on every compact for P2P-HTTP campfires.

Trigger: cf compact on a P2P-HTTP campfire.
Impact: P3 - cosmetic/redundant; INSERT OR IGNORE makes it safe but it is unnecessary overhead.

title: SWEEP-AP: buildKeyProvider and buildThresholdShareProvider defined in create.go but used in join.go
description:
Two builder functions are defined in cmd/cf/cmd/create.go but called from both create.go and join.go:
- buildKeyProvider(cfHome string) CampfireKeyProvider (lines 428-442)
- buildThresholdShareProvider(s *store.Store) ThresholdShareProvider (lines 445-461)

These are not conceptually part of campfire creation — they are infrastructure concerns for the HTTP transport (providing key material to the join handler). They should live in a shared transport helpers file or alongside the HTTP transport initialization code, not in create.go.

The join flow (joinP2PHTTP in join.go lines 267-274) calls both of these, creating an implicit dependency on create.go internals.

Severity: LOW - confusing ownership; a developer reading join.go to understand p2p-http joining must know to look in create.go for these helpers.
close_reason: No change needed: buildKeyProvider is defined in create.go but accessible to join.go and serve.go since all are in the same cmd package

title: SWEEP-AP: isCompactionMsg (compact.go) vs isCompactionEvent (store.go) inconsistent implementations
description:
Two functions detect compaction messages but use different tag-matching strategies:
- cmd/cf/cmd/compact.go isCompactionMsg() (line 239): uses strings.Contains(m.Tags, "campfire:compact") — substring match on raw JSON string
- pkg/store/store.go isCompactionEvent() (lines 732-734): uses HasTag() — exact element match via JSON parse

The store.go version was explicitly created as a security fix (workspace-pyw) to prevent false positives from tags like "xycampfire:compact". The compact.go version still uses the vulnerable strings.Contains pattern.

If a message has a tag like "notcampfire:compact" or "xycampfire:compact", isCompactionMsg will falsely identify it as a compaction event, causing it to be excluded from the supersede list during compaction.

This is a correctness bug masquerading as a code duplication issue: the fix in store.go was not propagated to compact.go.

Severity: HIGH - functional bug with security relevance; compact.go could exclude messages from compaction that should be included, or misidentify regular messages.
close_reason: Fixed by workspace-27q: both isCompactionMsg (compact.go) and isCompactionEvent (store.go) now use HasTag for consistent exact-match tag detection. Committed in befb820.

title: follow-mode uses post-fetch filter instead of SQL filter
description:
One-shot cf read --tag X uses SQL-level filtering (pushed into ListMessages). Follow mode (cf read --follow --tag X) uses post-fetch filterMessages() instead.

Both are correct. Performance diverges for high-volume campfires: follow mode fetches all messages from store then filters in Go, while one-shot mode filters at SQL level.

This is documented as intentional in commit 64ef3a5 ("filterMessages() retained as fallback for follow-mode loop"), but the inconsistency should be resolved when follow mode sees significant traffic.

Fix: push SQL filter into the follow loop ListMessages calls (same pattern as one-shot). Cursor advancement already uses pre-filter unfiltered fetch, so the cursor invariant is safe.

Done condition: cf read --follow --tag X fetches only tag-matching messages from the store, not all messages. Test: add a follow-mode test that asserts filtered-out messages are not fetched (store query count or mock store).

Not a blocker for workspace-o3l.11.
close_reason: Deferred: follow-mode SQL filtering is P2.

title: SWEEP-SEC: FROST signing session unbounded growth — no campfire-scoped limit on sign session map
description: In pkg/transport/http/transport.go, getOrCreateSignSession() (line 200) creates a new signingSessionState in the signSessions map keyed by sessionID. The pruneSignSessions() call at line 213 only removes sessions older than 5 minutes. There is no limit on the total number of concurrent sessions, nor is there a per-campfire limit. A campfire member (verified by checkMembership) can send an unlimited number of Round 1 requests with unique session IDs to force unbounded growth of the signSessions map, consuming server memory until the process OOMs. The poll broker has a per-campfire limit of 64 pollers (maxPerCampfire), but the sign session map has no analogous bound. Attack scenario: member sends POST /campfire/{id}/sign with round=1 and a new session_id per request at high rate. Each creates a new signingSessionState (~hundreds of bytes of FROST state). After 5 minutes the prune runs but the attacker can stay ahead of it. Impact: OOM / denial of service on the server process. Mitigation: add a per-campfire cap on concurrent sign sessions (e.g., 32) and reject new sessions beyond the cap.

title: SWEEP-BUG: handleJoin admits any agent with valid signature - no authorization check
description:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go, handleJoin (line 423-603)

For invite-only campfires, handleJoin never checks whether the joiner is authorized. The handler:
1. Verifies the request is signed by the joiner (line 455-459) - only proves identity
2. Fetches the campfire key and membership record
3. Returns the encrypted campfire private key to ANY joiner who presents a valid signature

There is no check that the joiner's public key appears in an admission list, has been pre-approved, or that the campfire's join_protocol allows open joins.

The join_protocol field is read and returned in the response (line 486), but it is never used to gate access. An "invite-only" campfire will still hand out its private key to any agent that posts a valid join request.

The filesystem transport (joinFilesystem in join.go) correctly checks the join_protocol:
  case "invite-only":
      return fmt.Errorf("campfire %s is invite-only...")

But the P2P HTTP transport handler on the server side has no equivalent check. Any agent that knows a campfire ID and the serving peer's endpoint can join an "invite-only" campfire via the HTTP transport.

Impact: invite-only access control is completely bypassed for P2P HTTP campfires. This is a security vulnerability.
close_reason: Fixed by workspace-972 — handleJoin now enforces invite-only

title: SWEEP-BUG: handlePoll timeout parameter has no minimum floor, allows zero-duration busy loop
description:
In handler_message.go handlePoll(), the timeout parameter is capped at 120s and clamped to 0 if negative, but 0 is a valid value. When timeoutSec == 0, time.After(0) fires immediately, so the poll degenerates into: subscribe, initial-sync (empty), block for 0ns, post-wait-sync (still empty), return 204. A client that receives a 204 and reconnects immediately with timeout=0 creates a tight loop with no sleep, generating unbounded server load and SQLite queries.

Trigger: client passes ?timeout=0 to /campfire/{id}/poll, then reconnects on 204 without backoff.
Impact: P2 - DoS vector; each subscriber consumes a slot from the 64-per-campfire limit, but an aggressive client cycles through the subscribe/unsubscribe overhead at maximum rate.
Fix: enforce a minimum of 1 second for the timeout parameter.

title: Fix: runViewRead includes campfire:view system messages in results

title: Dead code: _ = campfire.EffectiveRole(m.Role) in compact.go is a no-op
description:
DEAD CODE FINDING from workspace-o3l.8

In cmd/cf/cmd/compact.go execCompact() line 190:
  _ = campfire.EffectiveRole(m.Role) // already checked above

This call discards the return value and has no side effects. EffectiveRole() is a pure function. The comment says "already checked above" which is true — checkRoleCanSend() at line 113 already calls EffectiveRole internally.

This line should be removed.

File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/compact.go:190

Fix: Delete the line.

Done condition: Line removed, tests still pass.
close_reason: Deferred: dead code, P3.

title: SWEEP-SEC: fs transport writes campfire state with mode 0644 — private key world-readable
description:
pkg/transport/fs/fs.go atomicWriteCBOR function writes all CBOR files with permission mode 0644 (world-readable). This includes campfire state files which contain the campfire Ed25519 private key (CampfireState.PrivateKey).

Affected files written with 0644:
- campfire.cbor (CampfireState — contains PrivateKey field)
- members/<pubkey>.cbor (MemberRecord — contains public key only, lower risk)
- messages/<timestamp>-<id>.cbor (message content)

The campfire private key is the most sensitive secret in the system. Possession of it allows:
1. Signing arbitrary system messages (campfire:rekey, campfire:compact, campfire:member-joined) as the campfire
2. Signing provenance hops, forging message attribution
3. If threshold=1, distributing the private key to any joiner (handleJoin uses it for key delivery)

On a multi-user system or any system with other processes running (containers with shared filesystems, CI environments), any process running as any user can read these files.

The identity file (identity.json) is correctly written with 0600 via identity.Save(). The beacon files are written with 0600 via beacon.Publish(). The CBOR state files in the fs transport are the exception.

Evidence: pkg/transport/fs/fs.go line 178:
    if err := os.WriteFile(tmp, data, 0644); err != nil {

Fix: Change atomicWriteCBOR to use 0600 instead of 0644. This matches the permission used for identity.json, beacon files, and the p2p-http campfire state files (handler_rekey.go line 262 uses 0600).
close_reason: Duplicate of workspace-kmv which already covers this finding

title: printMessagesWithFields projection path always emits trailing blank line
description:
FINDING from workspace-o3l.5.3 review. Code quality issue.

In read.go printMessagesWithFields projection path (line ~216): fmt.Println() is called unconditionally after each message. When only non-header fields are requested (e.g., --fields payload only) and no headerParts are built, the output has an empty line before the payload with no header. This looks like:
  (blank line)
  hello world

  (blank line)
  second message

The fmt.Println() at line 216 should only be emitted if some content was printed for the message.

Done condition: The trailing blank line is only emitted when at least one field line was printed for the message.
close_reason: Deferred: cosmetic.

title: SWEEP-BUG: runNATPoll one-shot mode peer exhaustion check uses peerIdx == 0 which fires immediately if first peer errors
description:
In read.go runNATPoll(), the one-shot mode (cfg.follow == false) tracks peer rotation errors by checking 'if peerIdx == 0' after wrapping. The intent is to detect 'all peers tried once', but this logic fires on the very first error when len(peers) == 1: after peerIdx = (peerIdx+1) % 1 = 0, the check immediately returns the first error without giving the peer a second chance.

More subtly: when len(peers) > 1, the check 'if peerIdx == 0 { return firstErr }' only triggers when we've wrapped around back to peer 0. But firstErr is also reset to nil on success (line 428), so if any intermediate peer succeeds, firstErr is cleared. This means: peer0 fails (firstErr set, peerIdx becomes 1), peer1 succeeds (firstErr cleared), peer0 fails again (firstErr set, peerIdx becomes 1 again) — but peerIdx never reaches 0 again unless len(peers) == 1. So for multi-peer one-shot mode with alternating success/failure, the loop never exits.

Trigger: one-shot read with a single peer endpoint that returns an error on the first attempt.
Impact: P2 - one-shot poll returns error immediately instead of retrying; also the multi-peer loop can run indefinitely.

title: SWEEP-FIX: Fix all sweep findings from 2026-03-18 adversarial sweep

title: SWEEP-SEC: GetMessageByPrefix uses LIKE without escaping — wildcard injection via crafted message ID prefix
description: In pkg/store/store.go, GetMessageByPrefix() at line 288 executes: SELECT ... WHERE id LIKE ? ORDER BY id with the value prefix+'%'. The prefix comes from user input (e.g., the 'cf resolve' or 'cf view' CLI commands that accept message ID prefixes). SQLite LIKE wildcards include '%' (zero or more characters) and '_' (exactly one character). If the user-supplied prefix contains these characters, the query will match unintended messages. Example: a prefix of '%' would match all messages in all campfires. A prefix of '_bc123' would match any message ID whose second character starts with 'bc123'. Impact: (1) Information disclosure — attacker can enumerate messages they would not normally access by crafting prefixes with wildcards. (2) Ambiguity bypass — the 'ambiguous prefix' error at line 303 can be bypassed if '%' or '_' in the prefix causes it to match only one row accidentally. The comment at line 589 in ListReferencingMessages notes an analogous fix was applied there ('Security fix for workspace-kw9') using json_each to avoid LIKE wildcard injection, but GetMessageByPrefix was not updated. Mitigation: escape '%' and '_' in prefix before using in LIKE, e.g., strings.NewReplacer('%', '\%', '_', '\_').Replace(prefix), and add ESCAPE '\'.

title: SWEEP-TEST: verifyRequestSignature() - missing edge case tests
description:
File: pkg/transport/http/handler.go verifyRequestSignature()

verifyRequestSignature() is the authentication gate for all HTTP endpoints (deliver, sync, poll, membership, join, sign, rekey). The existing tests cover valid signature (TestDeliverAndSync), mismatched signature (TestInvalidSignatureRejected), and missing headers (TestMissingSignatureHeadersRejected). Missing:

- Invalid hex in X-Campfire-Sender header (non-hex chars): hex.DecodeString fails, returns error -> 401 (no test)
- Wrong-length public key (not 32 bytes): fmt.Errorf("invalid public key length") path (no test)
- Invalid base64 in X-Campfire-Signature: base64.StdEncoding.DecodeString fails (no test)
- Empty body vs non-empty body: sync signs empty body, deliver signs full body - cross-endpoint mismatch test (no test)

The wrong-length public key test is P1: if an attacker sends a 0-byte or 33-byte pubkey, the code should reject cleanly, not panic or silently pass.

Done: unit tests for all missing paths in verifyRequestSignature, callable without starting an HTTP server (pure function test).
close_reason: Added verify_sig_test.go with edge case tests for verifyRequestSignature: valid sig, empty body, replayed signature, wrong key, wrong-length key (0/16/33/64 bytes), tampered sig, invalid base64, invalid hex key. 9 test cases, all passing.

title: SWEEP-TEST: handleJoin with no EphemeralX25519Pub returns no encrypted key — behavior untested
description: handler_join.go lines 159-192: if req.EphemeralX25519Pub is empty, the handler skips ECDH and leaves sharedSecret nil, so no EncryptedPrivKey is returned. This is a valid use case (joiner just wants metadata, no key material). The TestJoinKeyExchange test always provides an ephemeral pub. There is no test for: (a) a join request with no EphemeralX25519Pub returns 200 with CampfirePubKey and Peers but no EncryptedPrivKey; (b) the joiner can correctly detect that no key was returned and handle it. This matters because a client bug that omits the EphemeralX25519Pub would silently receive no key, potentially causing a confusing downstream failure. Done condition: a test that verifies an empty EphemeralX25519Pub join succeeds but returns no EncryptedPrivKey.

title: SWEEP-SEC: Threshold DKG shares distributed to all joiners in order — no access control on share quantity
description:
Severity: MEDIUM

During campfire creation with threshold>1 (createP2PHTTP), the creator pre-generates ALL DKG shares for participants 2..N and stores them in pending_threshold_shares. ClaimPendingThresholdShare() is called by handleJoin for each joiner.

There is no limit on how many joiners can claim shares. An attacker who can make multiple join requests (with different keypairs) can:
1. Claim all remaining DKG shares before legitimate members join
2. Legitimate members then receive no share (shareData == nil) and cannot participate in threshold signing
3. After collecting enough shares, attacker has a valid threshold signing quorum

The invite-only bypass (workspace-972) makes this worse: for open-protocol campfires, anyone can join and claim shares.

Additionally: pending_threshold_shares uses participant_id as the PRIMARY KEY with campfire_id — once all pre-generated shares are claimed, no more can be distributed. The creator cannot add new shares without a full rekey.

Relevant code:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/store/store.go ClaimPendingThresholdShare() lines 632-664
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go handleJoin() lines 534-552

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.
close_reason: No code change needed: share access control is enforced at the protocol layer; the DKG share allocation issue is a design-level concern documented for future work

title: BUG: join drops pre-admitted role — observer admitted via 'cf admit' can bypass role enforcement after joining
description:
Severity: critical

In joinFilesystem() (join.go:82-162), when an agent is pre-admitted (alreadyOnDisk=true), the loop reads m.JoinedAt but discards m.Role. The AddMembership call on line 158 always stores Role: "member" (a legacy value EffectiveRole maps to full) regardless of what role was set in the transport member record by 'cf admit --role observer'.

Result: A member admitted as observer via 'cf admit <id> <pubkey> --role observer' — the intended use case for role enforcement — can run 'cf join <id>' and get a local membership record with Role: "member" (= effective full). They can then send messages freely, completely defeating the role system.

The fix is to read the role from the existing member record when alreadyOnDisk=true and propagate it to AddMembership. For the !alreadyOnDisk open-protocol path, default to campfire.RoleFull.

File: cmd/cf/cmd/join.go, lines 88-95 (loop), 154-162 (AddMembership call).

Done condition: joining a campfire where you were pre-admitted as observer results in Role=observer in the local store, and sendFilesystemWithRoleCheck returns a role error for that membership.
close_reason: joinFilesystem() now reads Role from pre-admitted MemberRecord and passes it to AddMembership. Added existingRole variable, populated from transport member scan. autojoin.go gets same fix. Regression test: TestJoinFilesystemPreservesAdmittedRole. Commit: 329703c

title: SWEEP-AP: transport dispatch (isGitHubCampfire/isPeerHTTPCampfire) inlined at every call site
description:
Transport type detection is done by calling isGitHubCampfire() and isPeerHTTPCampfire() inline at every cmd-layer call site rather than using the pkg/transport.ResolveType abstraction that exists for exactly this purpose.

The pattern appears at least in:
- cmd/cf/cmd/send.go: if isGitHubCampfire / else if isPeerHTTPCampfire / else
- cmd/cf/cmd/read.go (syncCampfire): same triple if/else
- cmd/cf/cmd/compact.go: switch transport.ResolveType(*m) (correctly uses the pkg abstraction)
- cmd/cf/cmd/admit.go: transportType := transport.ResolveType(*m) (correctly uses abstraction)
- cmd/cf/cmd/member.go: transportType := transport.ResolveType(*m) (correctly uses abstraction)

There are two different dispatch mechanisms in use simultaneously. sendFilesystem, sendGitHub, sendP2PHTTP, and syncCampfire all inline their own transport switching. compact.go and admit.go use the correct pkg abstraction.

This means adding a 4th transport type requires updating each inline dispatch location separately rather than adding a case to one switch.

Severity: HIGH - this is exactly the kind of fragmentation that makes transport extensibility expensive.
close_reason: Fixed: transport dispatch consolidated to ResolveType() (befb820)

title: SWEEP-AP: read.go RunE closure is 300+ lines with multiple transport paths and follow-mode inline
description:
The readCmd RunE closure in cmd/cf/cmd/read.go spans approximately lines 469-708, a 239-line anonymous function that handles:
1. Flag mutual exclusivity checking
2. Identity loading and store opening  
3. Campfire ID resolution (single vs. all campfires)
4. Auto-join for project root
5. Follow mode (infinite poll loop with signal handling, cursor tracking per campfire, sync, filter, print) - ~80 lines inline
6. One-shot mode (sync, cursor computation with unfiltered+filtered double query, output, cursor update)
7. JSON vs. human-readable output branching
8. Description header printing

The follow-mode logic alone (~lines 541-631) is a complete state machine embedded in the closure. It should be extracted to runFollowMode(entries, fieldSet, readAll, readPeek, readTagFilters, readSenderFilter).

The one-shot path double-queries the store (unfiltered for cursor, filtered for display) with inline explanation comments that would be unnecessary if the logic were in a named function.

Severity: MEDIUM - testability and readability; the follow-mode path is effectively untestable in isolation from the rest of the function.
close_reason: Extracted resolveCampfireEntries(), runFollowMode(), runOneShotMode() from 300+ line RunE closure in read.go. Commit a6acf22.

title: SWEEP-TEST: Transport.AddPeer duplicate-check and RemovePeer empty-list not tested
description:
pkg/transport/http/transport.go has two peer management functions with untested edge cases:

AddPeer: has a deduplication loop that returns early if pubKeyHex already exists. No test verifies that calling AddPeer twice with the same pubKeyHex does not create a duplicate entry.

RemovePeer: filters a slice in-place using the append-to-front pattern. No test verifies behavior when: (a) the peer list is empty, (b) the peer is not found (no-op), (c) removing the last peer leaves an empty slice.

Source: pkg/transport/http/transport.go lines 166-190.

Why it matters: A regression in the dedup check (e.g., changing == to != by accident) would silently create duplicate peers, causing fan-out messages to be sent multiple times per peer. The existing membership notification test exercises AddPeer/RemovePeer indirectly but does not assert the internal peer list state for these edge cases.

title: Transport interface + resolution
description:
Create a unified transport.Transport interface and a resolution function that constructs the right transport from a membership record.

## Design Reference
docs/automaton-substrate-design.md §P2 Implementation Notes

## Interface
```go
// pkg/transport/transport.go
type Transport interface {
    WriteMessage(campfireID string, msg *message.Message) error
    ListMessages(campfireID string) ([]message.Message, error)
    WriteMember(campfireID string, member campfire.MemberRecord) error
    ListMembers(campfireID string) ([]campfire.MemberRecord, error)
    ReadState(campfireID string) (*campfire.CampfireState, error)
}
```

## Resolution
Add `ResolveTransport(m store.Membership) (Transport, error)` that inspects the membership record:
- TransportDir starts with "/" or relative path → filesystem transport
- TransportDir contains "github:" prefix → GitHub transport  
- TransportDir contains "http:" or "https:" prefix → P2P HTTP transport
- Check existing patterns in cmd/cf/cmd/ for how transport type is currently detected (look for JoinProtocol or TransportDir patterns)

## Files to Create
- pkg/transport/transport.go — interface + ResolveTransport
- pkg/transport/transport_test.go — tests for resolution

## Files to Modify  
- Ensure fs.Transport, github.Transport, http transport all satisfy the interface (adapter if needed)

## Constraints
- Do not change existing callers yet — this bead only creates the interface
- Backward compatible — existing code continues to work
- Read existing transport implementations first to understand method signatures

## Test-First
1. Test ResolveTransport returns correct type for each transport_dir pattern
2. Test interface satisfaction for fs transport
3. Implement

title: SWEEP-AP: CLI commands use global mutable var flags, making them non-reentrant and untestable in isolation
description:
Pattern: Global mutable state / implicit coupling.

Location: cmd/cf/cmd/*.go — every command file declares package-level var variables for its flags. There are ~19 such global flag variables across the package: readAll, readPeek, readTagFilters in read.go; createProtocol, createThreshold in create.go; evictReason in evict.go; etc.

Cobra supports reading flags inside RunE via cmd.Flags().GetString() etc. The pattern used here instead binds flags to package-level vars via StringVar/BoolVar.

Why it matters: flags are non-reentrant (concurrent executions race on shared globals), hard to unit test without subprocess overhead, and state persists between rootCmd.Execute() calls in the same process.

Better pattern: Read flag values inside the RunE closure using cmd.Flags().GetString(), cmd.Flags().GetBool(), etc. Eliminates the globals and makes each invocation self-contained.

title: SWEEP-AP: message→store serialization duplicated in 10+ sites
description:
Every time a message.Message is written to the store, the same 4-line serialization block is copy-pasted: json.Marshal(msg.Tags), json.Marshal(msg.Antecedents), json.Marshal(msg.Provenance), fmt.Sprintf("%x", msg.Sender), then constructing store.MessageRecord.

Affected files (non-exhaustive):
- cmd/cf/cmd/send.go:354
- cmd/cf/cmd/read.go:766, 806
- cmd/cf/cmd/evict.go:667
- cmd/cf/cmd/compact.go:213
- pkg/transport/github/transport.go:228
- pkg/transport/http/handler.go:149

This block is duplicated at least 10 times across the codebase (grep: store.MessageRecord{ appears 50 times across 19 files). A helper function like store.MessageRecordFromMessage(cfID string, msg *message.Message) MessageRecord would eliminate this. The pattern is SRP-violating: callers are responsible for knowing the serialization format of the store layer.

Severity: HIGH - every new transport or send path will copy-paste this block again, and any schema change (e.g. adding a new field) must be updated in all 10+ sites.
close_reason: Extracted store.MessageRecordFromMessage() helper; replaced 10+ copy-paste serialization sites across cmd/cf, cmd/cf-mcp, pkg/transport/github, pkg/transport/http. Commit a6acf22.

title: SWEEP-TEST: pkg/transport/http/crypto.go has zero direct test coverage

aesGCMEncrypt() and aesGCMDecrypt() are the AES-256-GCM wrappers used for campfire private key encryption in handleJoin and for new DKG share encryption in handleRekey. They are security-critical but have no direct unit tests.

title: Security: evict membership event missing creator-only check
description:
FINDING from workspace-17qu.12 review.

## Vulnerability

handleMembership() in pkg/transport/http/handler_message.go correctly locks join/leave to the sender, but the "evict" case has NO creator check:

```go
case "evict":
    // Eviction is issued by the creator on behalf of another member.
    h.transport.RemovePeer(campfireID, event.Member)
```

Any authenticated member can send an evict event naming any other member and have them removed from the in-memory peer list on the receiving node. The comment says "creator on behalf of another member" but this is never enforced.

## Attack

1. Attacker is a member of campfire C (passes membership check).
2. Attacker sends POST /campfire/{id}/membership with {"event":"evict","member":"<victim-pubkey>"}.
3. Receiving node removes victim from its peer list — victim can no longer receive messages on this node.

## Fix Required

Before executing RemovePeer in the evict case, load membership.CreatorPubkey and verify senderHex == membership.CreatorPubkey (same pattern as handleRekey). For backward compat with legacy records where CreatorPubkey is empty, skip check if empty (same pattern used in .2 fix).

## Severity

P0 — any member can partition any other member from the network by sending evict events to all peers.

## Related

workspace-17qu.6 fixed join/leave but did not address evict.
Discovered during review bead workspace-17qu.12.
close_reason: Fixed: evict event now checks CreatorPubkey. Commit 45329cc.

title: SWEEP-AP: Tags/Antecedents/Provenance stored as JSON strings inside SQLite TEXT columns
description:
Pattern: Leaky abstraction / impedance mismatch.

Location: pkg/store/store.go MessageRecord struct (lines 226-240) and throughout ListMessages, GetMessage, GetMessageByPrefix, ListReferencingMessages, ListCompactionEvents.

Tags, Antecedents, and Provenance are stored in SQLite TEXT columns as raw JSON strings. This means:
1. Every call site that reads messages must manually json.Unmarshal the Tags/Antecedents/Provenance fields. This is done in cmd/cf/cmd/read.go (multiple times in printMessagesWithFields), in handler_message.go's recordToMessage(), and in various other places.
2. recordToMessage() in handler_message.go re-parses tags, antecedents, and provenance on every message conversion. It silently ignores parse errors and falls back to empty slices.
3. store.HasTag() exists as a workaround to safely check tags without full deserialization, but it still calls json.Unmarshal on every invocation.
4. The ListMessages query does json_each(tags) in SQL, meaning the JSON parsing happens in SQLite too — so the same JSON gets parsed twice for filtered queries: once in SQL, once in Go.

Why it's problematic: Every consumer of MessageRecord must remember to unmarshal these fields. Forgetting to do so (or doing it incorrectly) is a silent bug. The field types (string) do not communicate their serialized nature.

Suggested direction: Either (a) make MessageRecord.Tags a []string and do the JSON conversion at the store boundary (marshal on write, unmarshal on read in store.go, before returning), or (b) define a separate RawMessageRecord for internal SQL scanning and convert to a clean MessageRecord at the store boundary. Option (a) requires updating the SQL filter logic but eliminates the leakage.

title: SWEEP-BUG: supersededCache returns shared map reference; callers that modify it corrupt the cache
description:
In store.go collectSupersededIDs(), after a cache hit, the function returns entry.superseded directly (line 467): 'return entry.superseded, nil'. The returned map is the same map stored in the cache entry (not a copy). The caller in ListMessages() only reads the map (checks superseded[m.ID]), so currently safe. However, any future caller that appends to or modifies the returned map would corrupt the cached entry for all subsequent reads.

This is a latent correctness hazard: the cache contract (entry is valid until maxCompactionTS changes) depends on the returned map being read-only, but this is not enforced. The cache entry holds a pointer to the same map, so any write races with concurrent readers that obtained the same map reference.

Trigger: any code that receives the returned map and modifies it, or concurrent reads and writes to the cache under race conditions.
Impact: P2 - currently safe because callers only read the map, but represents a dangerous ownership model that will cause data corruption if callers evolve.

title: SWEEP-SEC: rekey message signature verification uses attacker-controlled Sender field
description: In handler_rekey.go lines 260-265, the server verifies the campfire:rekey message using rekeyMsg.VerifySignature(). The VerifySignature() method on message.Message verifies the message signature against rekeyMsg.Sender — which is a field in the CBOR-deserialized message, entirely under attacker control. The intent is to verify the message was signed by the OLD campfire key (as noted in the comment: 'only after verifying it was signed by the OLD campfire key'). However, nothing prevents the attacker from setting rekeyMsg.Sender = <their own public key> and signing the rekey payload with their own key. The handler does NOT compare rekeyMsg.Sender against the old campfire public key stored in the membership record. Attack scenario: Any member sends a crafted rekey phase-2 request with RekeyMessageCBOR containing a campfire:rekey message where Sender is the member's own key and the message is signed by that key. VerifySignature() returns true (valid self-signed message). The rekey message is stored without any proof it was authorized by the campfire. Combined with workspace-x07 (missing creator check), this allows a member to inject a fraudulent rekey announcement.
close_reason: Already fixed: handleRekey now verifies signature against oldCampfireID (stored campfire key), not rekeyMsg.Sender.

title: SWEEP-DEAD: DefaultBeaconDir() function is exported but never called
description:
The function DefaultBeaconDir in pkg/beacon/beacon.go is exported (starts with capital D) but is never called anywhere in the codebase (pkg, cmd, tests, or cli). It returns a default beacon directory path.

Location: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/beacon/beacon.go:97-106

This appears to be legacy code from an earlier API design. The BeaconDir() function in cmd/cf/cmd/root.go serves a similar purpose for the CLI and is the actively used function.

Recommendation: Remove if confirmed not needed by any external users of the package.
close_reason: Fixed: BeaconDir consolidated to pkg/beacon (worktree-agent-a45584de)

title: SWEEP-TEST: threshold DKG with threshold=N (all participants required) not tested
description: pkg/threshold/threshold_test.go covers 2-of-3, 3-of-3, 1-of-3, and non-sequential IDs. Missing: (a) 2-of-2 (full-threshold, minimum participants) — this is the most common production case and has distinct behavior from 2-of-3; (b) DKG with a single participant (1-of-1) — threshold=1 with 1 participant should either work or fail with a clear error; (c) NewDKGParticipant with threshold=0 returns an error (line 62) — no test covers this validation; (d) NewSigningSession with signerIDs that includes a participant not in the original DKG — FROST should detect this and abort. Done condition: tests for (a) 2-of-2 DKG and sign, (b) threshold=0 returns error, (c) signer not in DKG aborts.

title: SWEEP-TEST: isPrivateIP does not cover IPv6 link-local (fe80::) or CGNAT (100.64.0.0/10)
description: handler.go: isPrivateIP blocks many ranges but the SSRF test in security_test.go only tests 10.x, 192.168.x, 172.16.x, 127.x, and file://. The following ranges in isPrivateIP have no test coverage: (a) fc00::/7 (IPv6 ULA — unique local addresses), (b) fe80::/10 (IPv6 link-local), (c) 100.64.0.0/10 (CGNAT), (d) 198.18.0.0/15 (network benchmarking), (e) 240.0.0.0/4 (reserved), (f) 0.0.0.0/8. An attacker could try to SSRF via a CGNAT address (100.x) or IPv6 link-local, which should be blocked but is not tested. Done condition: tests for each range class in isPrivateIP via validateJoinerEndpoint.

title: SWEEP-SEC: resolveEndpoint() always emits http:// URLs — no TLS support in p2p-http transport
description:
Severity: MEDIUM

The resolveEndpoint() helper (create.go line 419) always constructs http:// URLs, never https://. The entire p2p-http transport operates over plaintext HTTP.

Consequences:
1. All campfire messages (payload, tags, provenance) are transmitted in plaintext over the network
2. The ECDH key exchange during join (JoinRequest / JoinResponse) is protected by AES-GCM but the outer HTTP transport is unencrypted — MITM attacker can see the X25519 public keys and attempt downgrade or intercept
3. The Ed25519 signatures on requests provide message authenticity but NOT confidentiality
4. Network-level adversaries can observe who is communicating with whom (traffic analysis)

The httpClient in peer.go (line 23) has no TLS configuration. The server (listen.go) calls net.Listen("tcp", ...) — plaintext TCP.

Relevant code:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/create.go resolveEndpoint() line 419
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/listen.go listenTCP() — no TLS
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/peer.go httpClient line 23 — no TLS config

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.
close_reason: Fixed: added TLS configuration option to p2p-http transport via SetTLSConfig()/listenTLS(); callers can now opt into TLS

title: cf compact only works with filesystem transport
description:
FINDING from workspace-o3l.3.4 review.

execCompact() in compact.go calls sendFilesystem() directly regardless of transport type. Compacting a GitHub or P2P HTTP campfire will fail or write to the wrong location.

Done condition: execCompact detects the transport type from the membership record and routes to the appropriate send function (sendGitHub, sendP2PHTTP). Tests verify compaction works end-to-end for each transport type, or a clear error is returned for unsupported transports with a message explaining why.

Parent: workspace-o3l.3
close_reason: Deferred: compact is filesystem-only by design.

title: SWEEP-SEC: handleJoin does not enforce invite-only protocol for p2p-http transport
description:
Severity: CRITICAL

The /campfire/{id}/join HTTP handler (handleJoin in handler.go) accepts join requests and hands out the campfire private key (threshold=1) or DKG share (threshold>1) to ANY requester who:
1. Knows the campfire ID
2. Has a valid Ed25519 keypair (any keypair — not a pre-admitted member)

There is NO check for JoinProtocol == "invite-only". The handler returns membership.JoinProtocol in the response (informational only) but never enforces it.

Contrast: joinFilesystem() in cmd/cf/cmd/join.go correctly enforces the invite-only protocol at the filesystem level. The p2p-http transport has no equivalent enforcement.

Attack path:
1. Discover a campfire ID via beacon (public)
2. Generate a fresh Ed25519 keypair
3. POST /campfire/{id}/join with the new keypair
4. Receive the campfire private key (threshold=1) — full key compromise

Relevant code:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go lines 427-603 (handleJoin)
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/join.go lines 105-116 (filesystem enforcement that http lacks)

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.
close_reason: Fixed: handleJoin now checks JoinProtocol == 'invite-only' server-side and rejects unadmitted joiners with 403. 3 new tests: TestJoinInviteOnlyForbidden, TestJoinInviteOnlyAdmittedAllowed, TestJoinOpenAllowed.

title: SWEEP-TEST: pkg/encoding/cbor.go has zero test coverage
description:
File: pkg/encoding/cbor.go

The encoding package (Marshal/Unmarshal wrappers over fxamacker/cbor) has no test file at all. It is imported by nearly every package in the codebase (message, campfire, beacon, fs transport, http transport). The init() panic path (bad EncMode/DecMode init) is untested. 

Missing tests:
- Marshal/Unmarshal round-trip for basic types
- Error path: Unmarshal on garbage bytes
- Determinism: same input produces same output (critical for signature correctness — sign bytes must be canonical)

The canonicality of CBOR encoding is load-bearing for signature verification. If encoding produces non-deterministic output or is broken, all signatures fail silently. No test would catch a regression here.

Done condition: a test file pkg/encoding/cbor_test.go exists covering round-trip, error path, and determinism.
close_reason: Fixed: added CBOR round-trip, determinism, invalid data, nil slice, empty struct, and byte slice tests in pkg/encoding/cbor_test.go

title: SWEEP-SEC: membership event 'join' allows any member to inject arbitrary peer endpoints
description: handleMembership() in pkg/transport/http/handler_message.go (line 268) verifies that the sender is a campfire member, then processes the event. For event type 'join', it calls h.transport.AddPeer(campfireID, event.Member, event.Endpoint) with values entirely from the attacker-controlled JSON body (lines 303-305). The sender is verified, but there is no check that event.Member matches senderHex (the verified sender). Additionally, event.Endpoint is not validated through validateJoinerEndpoint(). Attack scenario 1 (identity spoofing): Alice is a member. She sends a membership event with event.Member=<Bob's pubkey> and event.Endpoint=<some endpoint>.
