All other members now believe Bob is at that endpoint and will send messages there. Attack scenario 2 (SSRF via endpoint): Alice sends event.Endpoint='http://169.254.169.254/' — the endpoint is stored in the in-memory peer map without SSRF validation. Future Deliver() calls to that peer will hit the metadata service. Mitigation: (1) enforce event.Member == senderHex, (2) call validateJoinerEndpoint() on event.Endpoint.

title: Follow mode uses post-fetch client-side filtering, one-shot uses SQL push
description:
FINDING from workspace-o3l.1.4 review.

In read.go --follow loop (line ~558): calls s.ListMessages(e.id, afterTS) without sqlFilter, then filterMessages() post-fetch.
In one-shot mode (line ~624): calls s.ListMessages(cfID, afterTS, sqlFilter) with SQL-level tag/sender filtering.

This dual code path is inconsistent: follow mode always fetches ALL messages and filters in Go; one-shot mode pushes filtering to SQL. For high-volume campfires, follow mode will be much less efficient.

The cursor advance comment explains the intent (pre-filter cursors), but the follow mode never uses SQL filtering at all.

Done condition: Follow mode uses the same SQL filter as one-shot mode. The cursor-advance invariant is preserved by tracking pre-filter cursors separately (as one-shot already does).

Parent: workspace-o3l.1
close_reason: Known: duplicate of workspace-2j0.

title: SWEEP-BUG: handleSign round 2 reads signSessions without write lock, races with pruning
description:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go, handleSign round 2 (line 703-706) and transport.go pruneSignSessions (line 196-203)

In handleSign for round 2:
  h.transport.mu.RLock()
  sessionState, ok := h.transport.signSessions[req.SessionID]
  h.transport.mu.RUnlock()

Then the code uses sessionState.session (ss) WITHOUT holding any lock.

Meanwhile pruneSignSessions (called from getOrCreateSignSession which holds a write lock) and removeSignSession (called at the end of round 2) both modify signSessions under write lock.

The issue: after the RLock is released (line 705), another goroutine could call removeSignSession (from another round-2 handler completing a different session ID) which acquires the write lock and deletes entries from signSessions. The sessionState pointer obtained from the read is still valid (the map entry was a pointer), but ss.session could be used concurrently across multiple HTTP requests for the same sessionID.

If two simultaneous requests hit round 2 for the same sessionID, both will obtain the same session pointer and call ss.ProcessAll() and ss.Deliver() concurrently without any per-session locking. The FROST state machine (state.State) is not documented as thread-safe.

Impact: concurrent round-2 calls for the same session ID (possible in a retry scenario or test parallelism) could corrupt signing state.
close_reason: Fixed: handleSign round 2 now uses Lock() for entire operation (worktree-agent-a699bc1e)

title: SWEEP-AP: Transport type detection heuristic is fragile filesystem probe, not explicit type field
description:
Pattern: Implicit coupling / fragile convention.

Location: pkg/transport/transport.go — ResolveType() and its helpers.

Transport type is detected by:
1. GitHub: string prefix 'github:' on TransportDir
2. PeerHTTP: os.Stat for <campfire-id>.cbor file in TransportDir
3. Filesystem: default fallback

Problems:
1. The GitHub detection depends on a string encoding convention ('github:' prefix) that is implicit — nothing in the store schema enforces it. If someone writes a TransportDir without the prefix (e.g. from a different tool or manual DB edit), it silently falls through to filesystem behavior.
2. The PeerHTTP detection makes a filesystem call (os.Stat) on every transport type resolution. Every call to transport.ResolveType for a p2p-http campfire hits the disk. This is called in send.go, read.go, and other hot paths.
3. The JoinProtocol field in the store.Membership record already distinguishes transport types conceptually, but it's not used for routing — a separate convention-based heuristic is used instead.
4. The 'filesystem' transport has no explicit marker — it's the else branch, meaning any corrupted or unknown TransportDir falls silently into filesystem mode.

Suggested direction: Add an explicit TransportType TEXT column to the campfire_memberships table. ResolveType becomes a direct field lookup with no filesystem I/O. Migrate existing rows during store Open().

title: SWEEP-AP: MembershipHash uses hand-rolled sort + SHA-256 instead of standard bytes.Compare
SWEEP-BUG: evict rekey uses raw ECDH secret without HKDF
description:
In evict.go deliverRekey(), the raw X25519 ECDH shared secret (sharedSecret) is passed directly to evictAESGCMEncrypt() without going through HKDF. The join and rekey server-side handlers both derive a proper 32-byte key via hkdfSHA256(rawShared, 'campfire-join-v1' / 'campfire-rekey-v1'), but the client-side eviction path in cmd/cf/cmd/evict.go line 577 does raw ECDH output = senderPriv.ECDH(receiverPub) and then passes that directly to evictAESGCMEncrypt as the key. Raw X25519 output is 32 bytes but is NOT a properly derived key. Furthermore, the server-side handler_rekey.go applies hkdfSHA256(rawShared, 'campfire-rekey-v1') while the client uses raw output, meaning the derived key on each side will NEVER MATCH, causing every eviction to fail with a decryption error on the receiver side.

Trigger: any cf evict invocation that has remaining peers with endpoints.
Impact: eviction rekey delivery always fails with decryption error on peers; evicted members are not properly notified of new campfire ID.
Fix: apply hkdfSHA256(rawShared, 'campfire-rekey-v1') in deliverRekey() before calling evictAESGCMEncrypt.
close_reason: Already fixed: evict.go now calls cfhttp.HkdfSHA256(rawShared, 'campfire-rekey-v1') before AESGCMEncrypt.

title: SWEEP-BUG: compact --before flag accepts prefix but execCompact requires exact ID
description:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/compact.go

The compactCmd flag description says:
  compactCmd.Flags().StringVar(&compactBefore, "before", "", "compact messages before the given message ID prefix")

The flag is documented as accepting a "message ID prefix". However, execCompact (line 127-137) does a direct equality comparison:
  if msg.ID == beforeMsgID { beforeTS = msg.Timestamp; break }

If the user provides a prefix (e.g., "abc123" matching a full UUID like "abc123xx-..."), the comparison msg.ID == beforeMsgID will never match, beforeTS remains 0, and the function returns:
  fmt.Errorf("message not found: %s", beforeMsgID)

The documentation says prefix but the code requires exact match. Either:
1. The flag description is wrong (should say "exact message ID")
2. The code is wrong (should use prefix matching like GetMessageByPrefix)

This is a usability bug: users following the documented behavior of providing a short prefix will always get "message not found" errors.
close_reason: Fixed: compact --before now supports prefix matching; ambiguous prefix returns error; flag description updated

title: SWEEP-AP: store.Store mixes message storage, membership, peer endpoints, threshold shares, and compaction into one type
description:
Pattern: God Object.

Location: pkg/store/store.go — the Store struct and its 834 lines.

The Store type provides:
1. Campfire membership CRUD (AddMembership, GetMembership, ListMemberships, RemoveMembership, UpdateMembershipRole)
2. Message CRUD with dynamic SQL query building (AddMessage, GetMessage, ListMessages, ListCompactionEvents, collectSupersededIDs, etc.)
3. Read cursor management (GetReadCursor, SetReadCursor)
4. Filter config (implied by schema)
5. Peer endpoint management (UpsertPeerEndpoint, DeletePeerEndpoint, ListPeerEndpoints)
6. Threshold DKG share management (UpsertThresholdShare, GetThresholdShare, StorePendingThresholdShare, ClaimPendingThresholdShare)
7. Cross-campfire ID migration (UpdateCampfireID)
8. In-memory superseded ID cache with its own mutex (supersededCache/supersededMu)

Why it's problematic: The supersededCache lock (supersededMu) is separate from the db, creating two independent concurrency surfaces. Testing peer endpoint logic requires a full Store with all migrations. Any schema change requires understanding the whole type. The compaction logic (collectSupersededIDs, isCompactionEvent) is a domain concern embedded in a storage layer.

Suggested direction: Not a full rewrite — the SQLite backend is intentional. But group related methods with interface boundaries: a MessageStore, MembershipStore, PeerStore, ThresholdStore. Even if they share the same sql.DB, the interface separation clarifies responsibilities and makes testing easier.

title: SWEEP-DEAD: NotifyEviction stub never called in PollBroker
description: PollBroker.NotifyEviction is a no-op stub method (pkg/transport/http/poll_broker.go:76) that was created as a placeholder for future forced-eviction support. The method is never called anywhere in the codebase (only defined). The comment says it exists so handleMembership can call it without requiring a file change, but handleMembership does not actually call it. Since evicted members already receive 403 on reconnect, this stub can be removed when proper eviction notification is implemented.

title: SWEEP-AP: BeaconDir duplicated between cmd/root.go and pkg/beacon/beacon.go
description:
The BeaconDir resolution logic is defined twice with identical behavior:
- cmd/cf/cmd/root.go BeaconDir() (lines 68-77): checks CF_BEACON_DIR env, falls back to ~/.campfire/beacons, falls back to /tmp/campfire/beacons
- pkg/beacon/beacon.go DefaultBeaconDir() (lines 97-106): identical logic, identical fallbacks

The cmd layer defines its own BeaconDir() instead of calling beacon.DefaultBeaconDir(). If the resolution order or fallback path changes in one, it will not change in the other.

Additionally, CFHome() in root.go and the ~/.campfire directory derivation in identity.go both independently compute the home-based default path.

Severity: MEDIUM - config resolution divergence risk.
close_reason: BeaconDir() in cmd/root.go now delegates to beacon.DefaultBeaconDir(). Commit a6acf22.

title: SWEEP-SEC: Role enforcement is client-side only — server accepts campfire:* system messages from any member
description:
Severity: HIGH

role.go comments explicitly acknowledge: "Phase 1: client enforces only. Transport-level enforcement is future work."

The role system (observer/writer/full) is enforced only in the CLI client before sending. The server-side handlers (handleDeliver, syncFromFilesystem, GitHub Poll) store all messages including campfire:* system messages without checking whether the sender has "full" role.

Consequences:
- A "writer" role member can bypass the client check (e.g., direct HTTP POST to /deliver) and inject campfire:compact, campfire:rekey, campfire:member-joined messages
- An "observer" member can send arbitrary messages by bypassing the CLI
- Role is stored in the local SQLite of the admitting node — a different node has no way to verify the sender's role assignment

This is a known limitation but worth tracking as a security bead because the role system is documented as providing access control in the CLI help and docs.

Relevant code:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/role.go (client-side only)
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go handleDeliver() — no role check
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/github/transport.go Poll() — no role check

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.
close_reason: Fixed: server-side role enforcement added in handler auth (worktree-agent-a9d8fd59)

title: SWEEP-TEST: rekey phase-1 replay attack — same sender X25519 pub reused not tested
description: handler_rekey.go: phase-1 caches the receiver's ephemeral key by senderEphemeralPubHex. Phase-2 calls claimRekeySession which deletes the entry on retrieval (single-use). However, there is no test for: (a) a replay where the same senderX25519Pub is used for two sequential phase-1 requests — the second overwrites the first, silently discarding the first receiver key, which could be a DoS vector; (b) a phase-2 call with a senderX25519Pub that was never phase-1'd returns 400 (code path exists, untested); (c) a phase-2 call after the rekey session has been pruned (5-minute timeout) — returns 400 (same path, untested). Done condition: tests for all three cases.

title: SWEEP-SEC: GitHub transport sends messages in plaintext as GitHub Issue comments — public by default
description:
Severity: MEDIUM

The GitHub transport encodes messages as base64(cbor(msg)) posted as public GitHub Issue comments. The comment encoding is:
  campfire-msg-v1:<base64>

This means:
1. All message payloads, tags, antecedents, sender public keys, and provenance chains are visible to anyone who can see the GitHub repository (public repos = world-readable)
2. The ErrEncryptAtRestNotSupported error in transport.go confirms encryption is not implemented
3. Message metadata (who talks to whom, when, what campfire they are in) is permanently stored in GitHub\s infrastructure

The Config struct has EncryptAtRest bool field but it explicitly returns an error if set to true.

Even for private repos: GitHub tokens have broad scope, repo contents are retained by GitHub, and GitHub staff can read them.

Relevant code:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/github/transport.go lines 18-20 (ErrEncryptAtRestNotSupported)
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/github/message.go EncodeComment() — raw message in comment

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.
close_reason: Documented as known limitation: GitHub transport sends messages as plaintext public Issue comments; already documented via ErrEncryptAtRestNotSupported

title: SWEEP-TEST: campfire.EffectiveRole has no dedicated test
description:
File: pkg/campfire/campfire.go EffectiveRole()

EffectiveRole() is the canonical role normalization function (empty/legacy roles -> full). It is used in checkRoleCanSend (cmd layer) and indirectly tested there, but has no direct unit test in the campfire package.

Missing: direct unit test for each input (observer, writer, full, empty, "member", "creator", unknown).

Done: campfire_test.go adds TestEffectiveRole covering all cases.
close_reason: Fixed: added TestEffectiveRole covering observer, writer, full, empty, creator, member, and unknown inputs in campfire_test.go

title: SWEEP-SEC: rekey phase-1 session cache keyed by attacker-controlled SenderX25519Pub — denial of service and key confusion
description: handleRekey() stores the receiver's ephemeral X25519 key in rekeySessions, keyed by req.SenderX25519Pub (the sender's claimed X25519 public key from the JSON body). In phase 1, any authenticated member can send an arbitrary SenderX25519Pub value — including a valid key already in use by the legitimate evict initiator. If two concurrent rekey operations use the same SenderX25519Pub value (or an attacker sends phase 1 before the legitimate sender does), the storeRekeySession() call at line 123 will overwrite the cached key with a new one. When the legitimate sender sends phase 2, claimRekeySession() at line 137 retrieves the WRONG key (one generated from the attacker's phase-1 request). The ECDH will succeed (since both sides have valid X25519 keys) but will derive a different shared secret, causing AES-GCM decryption to fail at lines 185-198. Effectively: any campfire member can block an eviction by racing phase 1 with the same SenderX25519Pub. DoS against eviction/rekey operations. Mitigation: scope the rekey session key by (campfireID, senderX25519Pub, senderEdPubKey) to prevent collisions.

title: SWEEP-SEC: handleRekey path traversal via TransportDir field — arbitrary file read/write via membership record
description:
Severity: HIGH

handleRekey (handler.go line 938) constructs a file path by concatenating membership.TransportDir with the old campfire ID:

  stateFile := membership.TransportDir + "/" + oldCampfireID + ".cbor"

The TransportDir field comes from the campfire_memberships SQLite table. This field is populated by the joiner during the join process and stored without sanitization.

Attack:
1. Attacker joins a campfire and manipulates their local TransportDir value in the store to contain path traversal sequences: "../../../etc" or similar
2. Attacker triggers a rekey (as the node receiving the rekey)
3. The handler reads from and writes to attacker-controlled paths

Additionally, os.WriteFile is called on a path derived from newCampfireID (line 952):
  newStateFile := membership.TransportDir + "/" + newCampfireID + ".cbor"

Since newCampfireID comes from the attacker-supplied RekeyRequest.NewCampfireID (after hex decode), and TransportDir is locally stored, an attacker with local store access can write arbitrary CBOR to arbitrary paths.

Note: The oldCampfireID in the URL path is controlled by the HTTP client, but the TransportDir comes from the local store (attacker would need local access to manipulate it). Still a defense-in-depth gap.

Relevant code: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go lines 938-956

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.
close_reason: Fixed: sanitizeTransportDir() added to handleRekey; rejects paths with '..' or non-absolute paths before any file operations. filepath.Join used instead of string concatenation. Test TestRekeyPathTraversalRejected and TestRekeyPathTraversalAbsoluteRelative verify path traversal attempts return 500.

title: admit command hardcodes filesystem transport
description:
FINDING from workspace-o3l.2.2 review.

In admit.go: transport := fs.New(fs.DefaultBaseDir()) — always uses filesystem transport, ignores the membership TransportDir. For GitHub or P2P HTTP campfires, there is no admit path. The command will write a member file to the wrong location (or nowhere useful).

Done condition: admit command reads the campfire membership transport type and routes to the appropriate transport handler. For GitHub: no-op or error with explanation. For P2P HTTP: writes to CBOR state file.

Parent: workspace-o3l.2
close_reason: Deferred: admit is filesystem-only by design (Phase 1).

title: SWEEP-TEST: handleSign — round 2 without round 1 returns 400 but no test covers it
description: handler_sign.go line 122-125: if a round-2 request arrives and no session exists, the handler returns 400 with 'signing session not found'. No test exercises this path. More importantly, there is no test for what happens when the round-2 session lookup races with pruneSignSessions. The pruning runs inside the write lock on every getOrCreateSignSession call; if a session is created near its 5-minute expiry, a concurrent round-2 call could find it deleted. This race condition has no test. Additionally, there is no test that a second round-1 call for the same session_id returns the existing session (getOrCreateSignSession idempotency). Done condition: tests for (a) round-2 without preceding round-1 → 400, (b) idempotent round-1 session creation, (c) stale session pruning.
close_reason: Added TestGetOrCreateSignSession_Idempotent white-box test in prune_sessions_test.go. Tests (a) round-2 without round-1 → 400 and (c) stale pruning were already covered in handler_sign_test.go and prune_sessions_test.go respectively. Added missing (b) idempotent round-1: verifies second getOrCreateSignSession call returns same pointer and map has one entry. All tests pass.

title: SWEEP-BUG: evictThresholdN DKG is run in-process but dkgResults indexing by participant ID assumes creator is always ID 1
description:
In evict.go evictThresholdN(), a new DKG is run in-process with participant IDs [1..n]. The code then does creatorResult := dkgResults[1] (line 294) and assigns participant 1 unconditionally to the creator (peerToParticipantID[agentID.PublicKeyHex()] = 1, line 326). Remaining peers are assigned IDs 2..n.

The problem: the peers slice (remainingPeers) comes from ListPeerEndpoints, which is unordered. The loop at line 328-334 assigns peer IDs 2..n in ListPeerEndpoints order. When the same campfire is later used for FROST signing, the server-side handleSign uses the stored participant IDs from peer_endpoints. If a peer disconnects and rejoins between eviction and signing, or if the ordering of ListPeerEndpoints changes across sessions, the participant IDs assigned here will not match what was stored on the peers' nodes (since the peers received their IDs from deliverRekey).

More immediately: the assignment loop at line 328 does 'for _, peer := range remainingPeers' but increments nextPID before the loop starts at 2, skipping the creator's slot correctly — however, if the creator IS in remainingPeers (selfInPeers==true), that peer entry still gets a non-creator participant ID assigned (the 'continue' check is only for delivery, not for ID assignment). This means the creator could end up with two participant ID entries: 1 from the hardcoded assignment, and nextPID from the loop iteration.

Trigger: evicting from a threshold campfire where the creator's own pubkey appears in remainingPeers (e.g., if the creator's endpoint was stored during the original join).
Impact: P2 - duplicate participant ID assignment leads to FROST signing failures post-eviction.

title: SWEEP-DEAD: campfire_trust MCP tool advertised but not implemented
description:
The campfire_trust MCP tool is defined in the tools list but has no handler implementation.

Location: cmd/cf-mcp/main.go:388

The tool specification declares campfire_trust for setting/resolving agent display names, but the tools/call dispatcher (lines 1317-1342) lacks a case handler for it.

Result: calling this tool returns error -32601 (unknown tool).

Either remove the specification or implement the handler.
close_reason: Fixed: removed campfire_trust from MCP tool list; the tool had no handler and would return an error when called

title: SWEEP-AP: FROST signing protocol duplicated in send.go (thresholdSignHop) and evict.go (buildAndSignRekeyMessage)
description:
The two-round FROST signing protocol is implemented twice, nearly identically:
- cmd/cf/cmd/send.go thresholdSignHop() (lines 382-501) 
- cmd/cf/cmd/evict.go buildAndSignRekeyMessage() (lines 429-555)

Both functions:
1. Load DKG share from store
2. Pick co-signers from peer list up to (threshold - 1)
3. Build signerIDs list
4. Call threshold.NewSigningSession
5. Round 1: ss.Start(), cfhttp.SendSignRound to each co-signer, collect all round-1 msgs, Deliver each
6. ss.ProcessAll() for round-2 messages
7. Round 2: cfhttp.SendSignRound to each co-signer, collect all round-2 msgs, Deliver each
8. ss.ProcessAll()
9. select on mySS.Done() with 10-second timeout
10. Extract signature with mySS.Signature()

The near-identical code is ~80 lines duplicated with only the sign input (hop vs. message) different.

Severity: HIGH - FROST protocol bugs must be fixed in both places; the duplication will mask asymmetric bugs.
close_reason: Fixed: FROST signing extracted to shared RunFROSTSign() (worktree-agent-a699bc1e)

title: SWEEP-AP: duplicate constant githubTransportPrefix in cmd and pkg layers
description:
The constant githubTransportPrefix = "github:" is defined twice:
- cmd/cf/cmd/github.go:29
- pkg/transport/transport.go:52

Both define the identical string literal. The pkg layer comment even says "Must stay in sync with the cmd-layer constant." This is explicit acknowledgement of a sync dependency that will diverge over time.

The pkg/transport/transport.go package exists specifically to abstract transport type detection (ResolveType function). The cmd layer should import the constant from pkg rather than redeclaring it.

Root cause: the constant was originally defined in cmd, then pkg was added without importing from cmd (possibly to avoid a circular dep). If so, the constant should be moved to pkg/transport and imported from there in cmd.

Severity: MEDIUM - duplicated constants with stated sync requirement; low blast radius for now but guaranteed to diverge.
close_reason: Exported GitHubTransportPrefix from pkg/transport; removed local const in cmd/cf/cmd/github.go. Commit a6acf22.

title: SWEEP-BUG: handlePoll cursor uses ReceivedAt of last record but since param filters by Timestamp
description:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go, handlePoll (line 337) and Poll client (pkg/transport/http/peer.go line 391)

The poll handler returns X-Campfire-Cursor = ReceivedAt of the last message record:
  cursor = strconv.FormatInt(records[len(records)-1].ReceivedAt, 10)

The Poll client stores this cursor and passes it as the "since" parameter on the next poll:
  url = fmt.Sprintf("%s/campfire/%s/poll?since=%d&timeout=%d", endpoint, campfireID, cursor, timeoutSecs)

But on the server side, handleSync/handlePoll calls ListMessages(campfireID, since) where the since parameter filters messages by TIMESTAMP (the message creation time), not ReceivedAt:
  conditions = []string{"timestamp > ?"}
  args := []any{afterTimestamp}

ReceivedAt is the local store insertion time; Timestamp is the message creation time set by the sender. These are different values. The cursor returned is a ReceivedAt nanosecond timestamp, but it is used as a filter against Timestamp.

Impact: 
1. If a message arrives late (sender clock behind server clock), its Timestamp could be earlier than the cursor, causing the message to be missed on poll even though it arrived after the cursor was set.
2. If the sender has a clock ahead of the server, the cursor could skip messages.
3. The computeInitialCursor function in read.go (line 344) correctly uses ReceivedAt for computing the cursor, but the server filters by Timestamp — so the cursor computed from the local store and the server-side filter are measuring different things.

This is a significant semantic mismatch that can cause message loss in the NAT poll path.
close_reason: Fixed: handlePoll now uses MessageFilter{AfterReceivedAt: since} so cursor and filter both use received_at. Messages from clock-skewed senders are no longer permanently missed. Regression test TestHandlePollFiltersByReceivedAt added.

title: SWEEP-SEC: resolveEndpoint always generates http:// URLs — TLS not enforced for inter-node communication
description: resolveEndpoint() in cmd/cf/cmd/create.go line 419 always constructs 'http://' URLs regardless of whether TLS is configured. When a node starts with --listen :9001, its self-advertised endpoint stored in peer_endpoints and shared with peers is 'http://localhost:9001'. All inter-node communication (Deliver, Sync, Poll, membership events, rekey, sign rounds) uses this plain HTTP endpoint. The campfire private key and FROST DKG shares are encrypted at the application layer (via ECDH + HKDF + AES-GCM during join/rekey), but all other traffic — message payloads, membership events, signing round messages — flows in plaintext over HTTP. An on-path attacker can read all campfire messages. The serve command does support TLS (SetTLSConfig), but the endpoint advertised to peers is constructed by resolveEndpoint() which always uses http://. There is no mechanism to advertise an https:// endpoint when TLS is configured. Impact: network-level confidentiality of message content is not provided even when TLS certs are available.

title: SWEEP-BUG: fs.atomicWriteCBOR ignores rand.Read error, producing potentially empty temp file name
description:
In pkg/transport/fs/fs.go atomicWriteCBOR(), line 174 calls rand.Read(randBytes[:]) and ignores the return error: 'rand.Read(randBytes[:])'. If crypto/rand.Read fails (e.g., /dev/urandom is unavailable — possible in sandboxed environments), randBytes will be all zeros. This causes all concurrent atomicWriteCBOR calls to generate the same temp filename (path.tmp.0000000000000000), creating a race condition where concurrent writes clobber each other's temp file before the rename.

Trigger: concurrent writes to the same campfire's member or message directory when crypto/rand is unavailable.
Impact: P2 - concurrent atomicWriteCBOR calls can clobber each other's temp file, causing one write to silently lose data; on most platforms rand.Read never fails, but the error is still silently swallowed.
Fix: check the error from rand.Read and fall back to a timestamp-based suffix or return an error.

title: SWEEP-BUG: Transport.Stop() uses context.Background() for Shutdown — hangs indefinitely with active long-poll connections
description:
File: pkg/transport/http/transport.go, line 162

    func (t *Transport) Stop() error {
        return t.server.Shutdown(context.Background())
    }

net/http Server.Shutdown() with context.Background() (no timeout) will wait indefinitely for all active connections to close before returning. Long-poll handlers (handlePoll) block in a select for up to 120 seconds waiting for messages or timeout. While blocked, they hold an active HTTP connection.

When Stop() is called (e.g., SIGTERM in the serve command), it will:
1. Stop accepting new connections.
2. Wait for all active connections to finish.

Since long-poll handlers can block for up to 120 seconds, a SIGTERM will not cleanly shut down the server for up to 120 seconds. If any poll is stuck (e.g., a misbehaving client), the shutdown hangs forever.

The serve command (cmd/cf/cmd/serve.go, line 75) calls tr.Stop() directly after receiving a signal. This means "cf serve" cannot be cleanly stopped without waiting up to 120s or killing the process.

Conditions: Active long-poll connections when Stop() is called.

Fix: Use a timeout context:
    ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 5*time.Second)
    defer cancel()
    return t.server.Shutdown(ctx)

title: SWEEP-SEC: GitHub transport campfire:key-delivery polling has no sender identity check — key from any sender accepted
description: In cmd/cf/cmd/join.go, pollForKeyDelivery() (line 442) polls GitHub issue comments and looks for a message tagged campfire:key-delivery. When found, it decrypts the payload using DecryptWithEd25519Key and returns the result as the campfire private key (line 470). The function verifies the message Ed25519 signature (via transport.Poll -> msg.VerifySignature()) but does NOT verify that the message sender is the campfire creator or any known campfire member. Any GitHub user who can post a comment to the issue can craft a campfire:key-delivery message signed with their own key, containing arbitrary content. The joiner will attempt to decrypt it; if decryption fails (wrong key), it silently continues to the next message. But if an attacker pre-generates a keypair X, encrypts a malicious private key K to the joiner's public key, and posts it as a comment signed with X, the joiner will accept K as the campfire private key. The joiner has no way to verify that K is the legitimate campfire private key — there is no proof from the campfire's public key that K is correct. Impact: a malicious party with write access to the GitHub issue can hijack the join of any new member on open campfires.

title: SWEEP-SEC: role enforcement is client-side only — any member can bypass observer/writer restrictions via direct HTTP
description:
Role enforcement (observer cannot send, writer cannot send campfire:* system messages) is enforced ONLY in the cf CLI client (cmd/cf/cmd/role.go). The HTTP transport server has no role checks.

The code comment in role.go explicitly confirms this: "Phase 1: client enforces only. Transport-level enforcement is future work."

Attack scenario: A member admitted as RoleObserver or RoleWriter crafts a raw HTTP POST directly to /campfire/{id}/deliver:
1. Constructs a valid Ed25519-signed HTTP request (authMiddleware passes — they are a member)
2. Sends a CBOR message with campfire:compact, campfire:rekey, or campfire:member-joined system tags
3. The server stores it without checking the senders role

Impact: An observer-role member can send arbitrary messages including system messages. A writer-role member can send campfire:* system messages. Combined with the Sender spoofing vulnerability (workspace-sdgd), an observer can inject system messages attributed to the campfire key.

This is particularly dangerous for campfire:compact (can purge history), campfire:rekey (triggers key rotation), and campfire:member-joined (can fabricate membership records).

Severity assessment: High (requires member access, leads to privilege escalation — observers can act as full members)

Fix: Server-side role checking in authMiddleware or individual handlers. Store each members role in the peer_endpoints table and verify against it. The current role field exists in campfire_memberships on the members own node but is not propagated to or enforced on other nodes.
close_reason: Implemented: server-side role enforcement in handleDeliver — observer 403, writer blocked from campfire:* system messages. Schema migration adds role column to peer_endpoints. 11 tests.

title: SWEEP-SEC: Beacon files world-readable (0755 dir, 0644 files) and beacon signature not verified on load
description:
Severity: MEDIUM

Two issues with the beacon system:

1. Beacon directory published with 0755 permissions (beacon.go Publish() line 111), beacon files at 0644. Beacon files contain campfire IDs, join protocols, reception requirements, transport endpoints, and descriptions. These are world-readable on multi-user systems, leaking campfire topology to any local user.

2. Beacon.Verify() method exists (beacon.go line 76) but is NOT called anywhere in the codebase during beacon loading/scanning. Scan() (lines 148-173) reads and deserializes beacons without calling Verify(). This means:
   - A local attacker can overwrite beacon files with forged beacons pointing to attacker-controlled endpoints
   - lookupBeaconDescription() (join.go line 534) trusts beacon metadata without signature verification
   - joinFilesystem() trusts beacon descriptions from unverified beacon files

Attack: Replace a legitimate beacon file with a forged one pointing to an attacker-controlled p2p-http endpoint. Joining agents connect to the attacker endpoint.

Relevant code:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/beacon/beacon.go Publish() line 111 (0755 dir), line 127 (0644 file)
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/beacon/beacon.go Scan() — no Verify() call
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/join.go lookupBeaconDescription() — uses unverified beacon data

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.
close_reason: Fixed: beacon dir permissions tightened to 0700, file permissions to 0600, and Scan() now calls b.Verify() to reject beacons with invalid signatures

title: TEST-GAP: bead .9 tests not in worktree — rekey replay, threshold join, membership fail-closed tests absent from worktree HEAD
description:
The task spec for workspace-17qu.13 lists these test files to review:
- pkg/transport/http/join_pubkey_test.go (join identity)
- pkg/transport/http/sign_race_test.go (FROST race)  
- pkg/transport/http/security_test.go (isPrivateIP tests at bottom)

None of these files exist in the review worktree (agent-a1ccff05 at HEAD dfdc369). The worktree is missing commits from main that contain these files:
- dcddfba adds security_test.go isPrivateIP additions
- 49cffa1 adds handler_rekey_test.go, handler_join_test.go, handler_membership_test.go

The bead .9 close reason acknowledges: 'Agent completed but test files not persisted (no worktree).'

The tests DO exist in the main repo but were not merged into the worktree. The review was therefore conducted on an incomplete state.

**Done condition:** Reviewer verifies via main branch that the three new test files from bead .9 and .10 exist, compile, and are included in CI.

**Source:** workspace-17qu.13 review finding.
close_reason: Tests added via workspace-e18n, workspace-nqlv, workspace-fn8 — rekey, threshold, membership coverage now present.

title: SWEEP-TEST: store.UpdateCampfireID — zero test coverage on rekey rename logic
description:
store.UpdateCampfireID is the critical atomic rename operation executed during every eviction+rekey. It renames campfire_id across 7 tables (campfire_memberships, messages, read_cursors, filters, peer_endpoints, threshold_shares, pending_threshold_shares) in a single transaction.

Currently zero tests exist for this function in pkg/store/store_test.go. The rekey integration tests (rekey_test.go) exercise it indirectly, but no unit-level tests exist.

Missing tests:
1. Basic rename: old-id memberships, peers, messages, and shares all appear under new-id after rename.
2. Non-existent old-id: call succeeds with zero rows affected (not an error).
3. New-id already exists (conflict): transaction rolls back, old-id unchanged.
4. Partial state: only some tables have rows — only those tables are renamed, others are silently skipped.

Why it matters: If UpdateCampfireID is broken, rekey silently fails — the store claims success (handler returns 200) while the node continues to reference the old campfire ID. This is a silent data integrity failure on a critical security operation (post-eviction key rotation).
close_reason: Implemented: 4 unit tests for store.UpdateCampfireID covering basic rename, non-existent ID, conflict rollback, and partial state. Pushed work/workspace-e18n.

title: SWEEP-AP: read.go is a 903-line multi-responsibility file
description:
Pattern: SRP violation / oversized file.

Location: cmd/cf/cmd/read.go — 903 lines.

The file handles:
1. Command flag definitions and RunE logic
2. Field projection parsing (parseFieldSet, validFieldNames)
3. Human-readable message formatting (printSingleMessage, printMessagesWithFields — two variants with duplicated field extraction)
4. JSON output formatting (encodeMessagesJSONWithFields)
5. Follow mode polling loop (runFollowMode)
6. One-shot sync mode (runOneShotMode)
7. NAT poll configuration and loop (natPollConfig, runNATPoll, filterNATMessages)
8. Sync helpers for all three transports (syncFromGitHub, syncFromFilesystem, syncFromHTTPPeers)
9. Cursor management helpers (computeInitialCursor)
10. Payload sanitization (sanitizePayload)

Additionally, printMessagesWithFields has two internally duplicated paths (nil fields vs non-nil fields) that both do json.Unmarshal([]byte(m.Tags)) and json.Unmarshal([]byte(m.Antecedents)) — the same deserialization in two loops.

Why it's problematic: Any change to output format, sync logic, or follow behavior means hunting through 900 lines. The two branches in printMessagesWithFields share no extraction logic despite doing identical JSON parsing.

Suggested direction: Extract printMessagesWithFields into a formatter.go with a MessageFormatter type. Extract runNATPoll + natPollConfig into nat_poll.go. Extract syncFrom* helpers into sync.go. The command RunE itself then becomes a thin orchestrator.

title: SWEEP-AP: Two independent crypto stacks for key delivery (identity/x25519.go vs http/crypto.go)
description:
Pattern: Implicit coupling / duplicated crypto plumbing.

Locations:
- pkg/identity/x25519.go: EncryptToEd25519Key / DecryptWithEd25519Key — uses Ed25519→X25519 conversion + HKDF-SHA256 (with nonce as salt) + AES-256-GCM. Info string: 'campfire-key-delivery'.
- pkg/transport/http/crypto.go: aesGCMEncrypt / aesGCMDecrypt + hkdfSHA256 — used for join/rekey key exchange. Uses different HKDF invocation (zero salt, info as context string). Info strings: 'campfire-join-v1', 'campfire-rekey-v1'.

Both implement AES-256-GCM encryption using HKDF-SHA256, but with different HKDF parameters (salt handling differs), different wire formats (identity/x25519.go prefixes ephemeral pub + nonce inline; http/crypto.go prepends nonce only), and different key derivation contexts.

Why it's problematic:
1. A future auditor must understand two separate crypto implementations in two packages to reason about key delivery security.
2. The HKDF implementations differ in salt handling: identity/x25519.go uses the nonce as the HKDF salt (which is unusual and changes the KDF behavior), while http/crypto.go uses a zero-length salt per RFC 5869 default. This difference is not documented at either call site.
3. If a bug is found in the AES-GCM or HKDF implementation, both files must be patched.

Suggested direction: Consolidate into a single pkg/crypto package with a documented interface for hybrid encryption. The two use cases (Ed25519 long-term key delivery vs ephemeral X25519 key exchange) are legitimately different protocols, but the AES-GCM and HKDF primitives should be shared.

title: SWEEP-BUG: Transport.Stop() uses context.Background() — shutdown never times out
description:
In transport.go, Transport.Stop() calls t.server.Shutdown(context.Background()). http.Server.Shutdown() waits for all active connections to close before returning. With context.Background(), this wait is unbounded — if a long-polling goroutine is blocked in a select waiting for ch or a 120s timer, Stop() will block for up to 120 seconds waiting for that goroutine.

The PollBroker does not implement a stop mechanism (NotifyEviction is a no-op stub), so there is no way to wake all pending pollers before shutdown.

Trigger: Transport.Stop() is called (e.g., after SIGTERM in cf serve) while any client has an active long-poll connection.
Impact: P3 - graceful shutdown takes up to 120 seconds (the max poll timeout); any server with active pollers appears to hang on SIGTERM.
Fix: use a context with a 5-10 second deadline in Stop(), and add a shutdown signal to PollBroker to wake all pending pollers.

title: SWEEP-SEC: handleSign exposes FROST signing oracle to any campfire member for arbitrary payloads
description: handleSign() in handler_sign.go verifies that the caller is a campfire member, then co-signs any MessageToSign bytes provided in the request body. There is no validation of what MessageToSign contains. A campfire member can request the node to co-sign: (1) arbitrary data on behalf of the campfire group key (forging provenance hops for other campfires), (2) malicious rekey payloads, (3) data that looks like a valid campfire:rekey message to trick other nodes. The FROST signature produced is a valid Ed25519-compatible threshold signature under the campfire's group key. Any other node that receives content signed with the campfire group key will accept it as legitimate campfire-signed content. The only mitigation would be to validate that MessageToSign has an expected structure (e.g., is a CBOR-encoded HopSignInput or MessageSignInput), but no such validation exists. Impact: a single malicious campfire member can forge campfire-level signatures on arbitrary content, enabling provenance fraud across the network. This is particularly severe because campfire group signatures are used to bootstrap trust for message relay.

title: TEST-WEAK: TestHandleSignRound2WithoutRound1 discards error body before reading it
description:
In pkg/transport/http/handler_sign_test.go (added in bead .8), TestHandleSignRound2WithoutRound1 does:

  if resp.StatusCode != http.StatusBadRequest {
      b, _ := io.ReadAll(resp.Body)
      t.Errorf(...)
  }
  // Verify the error message mentions session not found.
  respBody, _ := io.ReadAll(resp.Body)
  _ = respBody // already consumed above after status check

The comment 'already consumed above after status check' is wrong — resp.Body was only read inside the if-block (when status != 400). If the status IS 400, the body is never read. The test claims to verify the error message mentions 'session not found' but it reads only an empty body (body was never consumed in the 400 path). This means the 'verify error message' assertion is dead code.

**Impact:** The second assertion (message text contains 'signing session not found') is never exercised. A refactor that changes the error message would go undetected.

**Done condition:** Fix the test to read the response body unconditionally and assert the 'signing session not found' substring when status == 400.

**Source:** workspace-17qu.13 review finding.

title: SWEEP-SEC: ECDH key exchange in join/rekey lacks KDF — raw X25519 shared secret used directly as AES-256 key
description:
Severity: HIGH

The aesGCMEncrypt/aesGCMDecrypt functions in crypto.go use the raw X25519 shared secret (32 bytes) directly as the AES-256-GCM key, with no key derivation function (KDF).

X25519 shared secrets are NOT uniformly random — they are points on a curve and may have structure. NIST and RFC 7748 explicitly require that X25519 outputs be passed through a KDF before use as symmetric keys.

Contrast: EncryptToEd25519Key (identity/x25519.go line 173) correctly uses HKDF-SHA256 with salt and info string.

The join and rekey handlers use the raw shared secret:
- handleJoin: aesGCMEncrypt(sharedSecret, privKey) — encrypts campfire private key
- handleRekey: aesGCMDecrypt(sharedSecret, req.EncryptedPrivKey) — decrypts campfire private key
- evictAESGCMEncrypt in evict.go also uses raw shared secret

This weakens the cryptographic security of the key exchange and may be exploitable via small-subgroup attacks or non-uniform key material.

Relevant code:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/crypto.go aesGCMEncrypt/aesGCMDecrypt — no KDF
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go handleJoin() line 523 (raw shared secret)
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/evict.go evictAESGCMEncrypt() line 639 — no KDF

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.
close_reason: Applied HKDF-SHA256 (RFC 5869) to all X25519 shared secrets before use as AES-256-GCM keys. Added hkdfSHA256() in crypto.go, updated handleJoin, handleRekey, and peer.go Join client. Updated rekey_test.go to use matching derivation. Wire format break is intentional — documented in commit.

title: SWEEP-BUG: handleRekey writes new campfire state file before UpdateCampfireID, leaving inconsistent state on DB failure
description:
In handler_rekey.go, phase 2 of the rekey protocol writes the new campfire state file to disk (os.WriteFile newStateFile, line 223) and removes the old one (os.Remove stateFile, line 225) BEFORE calling h.store.UpdateCampfireID() (line 229). If UpdateCampfireID fails (e.g., SQLite write error), the node is left in an inconsistent state: the old .cbor file is gone and the DB still has the old campfire_id. The campfire is unrecoverable on the receiving peer.

Trigger: disk full, SQLite error, or OS crash between os.Remove and tx.Commit in UpdateCampfireID.
Impact: P1 - permanent loss of campfire membership for the receiving peer; they can no longer read, write, or participate in the campfire.
Fix: perform the DB update before removing the old state file. Only remove the old file after both the new file write and the DB update succeed atomically.
close_reason: Verified: production fix already in place (commit 7d4d1b1). Added regression test TestRekeyDBFailureLeavesStateFileIntact. Commit 923e0ba.

title: SWEEP-BUG: GitHub transport Stop() does not wait for pollLoop goroutine to exit
description:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/github/transport.go, Stop() (line 111-120)

Stop() closes stopCh and sets running=false but does NOT wait for the pollLoop goroutine to exit:

func (t *Transport) Stop() error {
    t.mu.Lock()
    if !t.running { t.mu.Unlock(); return nil }
    close(t.stopCh)
    t.running = false
    t.mu.Unlock()
    return nil
}

pollLoop runs in a goroutine started by Start(). After Stop() returns, the goroutine may still be mid-poll (blocked on a GitHub API HTTP call up to 30 seconds). If the caller then discards the Transport and the store/db gets closed, the goroutine could access a closed db causing a panic or use-after-free.

The goroutine also checks stopCh only at the top of the for loop (case <-t.stopCh: return). If it is in the middle of a Poll call (which itself does multiple API calls), it will not exit until that poll completes.

There is no WaitGroup or done channel to synchronize shutdown. This is a goroutine leak in tests and a potential crash if the Store is closed before the goroutine finishes.
close_reason: Fixed: GitHub transport Stop() now waits for pollLoop exit (befb820)

title: SWEEP-BUG: compact double-stores compaction event - sendP2PHTTP already stores locally
description:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/compact.go, execCompact (line 211-228)

The comment on line 212 says:
  // (sendP2PHTTP already stores locally; sendFilesystem and sendGitHub do not -- store here for all paths.)

But then the code unconditionally calls s.AddMessage for ALL transport types including P2P HTTP. Since sendP2PHTTP also calls s.AddMessage internally (send.go line 357), the compaction event is stored TWICE for P2P HTTP campfires.

The AddMessage uses INSERT OR IGNORE so the second insert is silently dropped (returns rows=0, inserted=false). This is not a data corruption issue, but it is a logic error and wastes a DB write on every compact operation for P2P HTTP campfires.

Additionally: the comment says sendFilesystem does not store locally -- but execCompact then stores it. However, sendFilesystem writes to the filesystem transport directory only (not to the SQLite store). The SQLite store is populated during syncFromFilesystem. So compact.go correctly stores to SQLite for filesystem campfires on the same call.

The actual bug: for P2P HTTP, the store write is redundant. Low severity (INSERT OR IGNORE makes it safe) but indicates the comment is misleading and the code does unnecessary work.
close_reason: Fixed: compact double-store eliminated (befb820)

title: SWEEP-TEST: store.UpdateCampfireID — messages table update not tested in isolation
description: pkg/store/store.go: UpdateCampfireID runs a transaction renaming a campfire across 7 tables including messages. The rekey integration test (TestRekeyProtocolThreshold1) exercises this indirectly by verifying membership is renamed, but does not verify: (a) messages table is also renamed (messages sent before rekey are still accessible under the new campfire ID); (b) the transaction is atomic — if one UPDATE fails, all changes roll back; (c) concurrent calls with the same oldID are safe. Done condition: a store unit test that directly calls UpdateCampfireID and verifies all tables are correctly renamed, including the messages table.

title: SWEEP-AP: member join flow copy-pasted across joinFilesystem and autoJoinRootCampfire
description:
The logic for joining a filesystem campfire is nearly identical in two places:
- cmd/cf/cmd/join.go joinFilesystem() (lines 73-184)
- cmd/cf/cmd/autojoin.go autoJoinRootCampfire() (lines 18-107)

Both functions:
1. Call transport.ReadState, transport.ListMembers
2. Check if agent already on disk
3. Conditionally write member record (WriteMember)
4. Conditionally write campfire:member-joined system message (identical code block, same format string, same logic)
5. Call campfireFromState + AddHop (identical)
6. Call transport.WriteMessage (identical)
7. Call s.AddMembership (near-identical)

The only differences are: autoJoin handles open-only protocol, skips invite-only silently; joinFilesystem handles both and also has lookupBeaconDescription. The common core is ~50 lines duplicated verbatim.

Severity: MEDIUM - a bug fix in either path must be applied to both.
close_reason: Extracted admitFSMemberIfNew() shared helper eliminating ~50 lines of duplication between joinFilesystem and autoJoinRootCampfire. Commit a6acf22.

title: SWEEP-TEST: handleRekey — missing campfire_id (empty string or non-hex) in newCampfireID rejected correctly but not tested
description:
handleRekey in pkg/transport/http/handler_rekey.go validates newCampfireID on lines 70-78:

  if req.NewCampfireID == '' {
      http.Error(w, 'missing new_campfire_id', http.StatusBadRequest)
      return
  }
  newPubKeyBytes, err := hex.DecodeString(req.NewCampfireID)
  if err != nil || len(newPubKeyBytes) != ed25519.PublicKeySize {
      http.Error(w, 'new_campfire_id must be a valid Ed25519 public key hex', http.StatusBadRequest)
      return
  }

No tests cover these validation paths:
1. Empty new_campfire_id → 400
2. Non-hex new_campfire_id (e.g., 'not-hex') → 400
3. Hex string with wrong length (e.g., 16 bytes / 32 hex chars instead of 32 bytes / 64 hex chars) → 400

These checks matter because a successfully accepted newCampfireID with wrong length would silently corrupt the store's campfire_id primary key. The existing rekey_test.go only exercises the success path with a valid 64-char hex key.

title: SWEEP-TEST: handleSync — recordToMessage conversion errors silently drop messages
SWEEP-SEC: Filesystem transport messages not signature-verified on sync; no provenance hop verification on deliver
description:
Severity: HIGH

Two separate verification gaps:

1. syncFromFilesystem() (read.go line 742): reads messages from the filesystem transport and stores them WITHOUT verifying the Ed25519 sender signature. Any agent with write access to the shared /tmp/campfire directory can inject unsigned/forged messages that will be stored and displayed as legitimate.

2. handleDeliver (handler.go line 121): stores received CBOR messages WITHOUT verifying:
   - The inner message.Signature (sender Ed25519 sig over payload/tags/antecedents/timestamp)
   - The provenance hop signatures
   
The HTTP request signature (X-Campfire-Sender + X-Campfire-Signature) only authenticates the transport-layer envelope, not the message content. A member can deliver a message claiming to be from a different sender (crafted msg.Sender ≠ request senderHex) and it will be stored and displayed with the spoofed sender.

Contrast: github transport Poll() correctly calls msg.VerifySignature() before storing (transport.go line 211).

Relevant code:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/read.go syncFromFilesystem() lines 742-780
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go handleDeliver() lines 141-176 (no VerifySignature call)

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.
close_reason: Fixed: FS transport signature verification added (befb820)

title: SWEEP-BUG: RunDKG goroutines may deadlock if routeDKG fails during broadcast
close_reason: Fixed: RunDKG goroutines now have 30s context timeout (worktree-agent-a699bc1e)

title: SWEEP-TEST: RunFROSTSign end-to-end has no test — HTTP sign handler exercised in isolation only
description: RunFROSTSign() in pkg/transport/http/frost_sign.go orchestrates the two-round FROST signing protocol over HTTP between live transports. There is no test that exercises this function end-to-end: starting two transports, having one call RunFROSTSign pointing at the other's /sign endpoint, and verifying the returned signature passes ed25519.Verify. The existing TestSignNonMemberForbidden only tests the 403 auth rejection path; rekey_test.go uses threshold.Sign (in-process) for the threshold signing assertion. If RunFROSTSign's round-1/round-2 message routing, session state management, or the signing timeout broke, no test would catch it. This is a P1 gap on a security-critical path — threshold signature production is the whole point of FROST, and a silent breakage here means campfires could fail to sign or produce incorrect signatures without detection. Done condition: a test calls RunFROSTSign with two live transports and verifies the returned signature.

title: SWEEP-SEC: Campfire private key stored in plaintext in CBOR files; identity private key stored in plaintext JSON
description:
Severity: MEDIUM

Two sensitive key material storage gaps:

1. Agent identity (Ed25519 private key): stored as JSON at ~/.campfire/identity.json with mode 0600. The JSON encoding is human-readable and unencrypted. Anyone with filesystem access (or backup access) gets the agent private key. No passphrase protection.

2. Campfire private key (threshold=1): stored as CBOR at $CF_HOME/campfires/{campfireID}.cbor and in filesystem transport at {transportDir}/campfire.cbor, with mode 0600. Same issue — plaintext private key on disk.

Additionally: the filesystem transport directory uses mode 0755 for subdirectories (members/, messages/) — readable by any user on the system (fs.go lines 47-48). Message payloads, member public keys, and campfire state (including private key) are world-readable in multi-user environments.

Relevant code:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/identity/identity.go Save() line 43 (0600 JSON, no encryption)
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/fs/fs.go Init() line 47 (0755 directory)
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/fs/fs.go atomicWriteCBOR() line 178 (0644 file mode)

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.
close_reason: Documented as known limitation: private keys stored in plaintext is by design for the filesystem transport trust model; the identity file already uses 0600 permissions (Save() uses os.WriteFile with 0600)

title: compact before flag does exact ID match not prefix
description:
FINDING from workspace-o3l.3.1 review.

In compact.go execCompact(), beforeMsgID is used as an exact ID match (msg.ID == beforeMsgID). The flag description in init() says "message ID prefix" but the implementation requires the full ID.

Inconsistency: cf read --pull accepts prefixes via GetMessageByPrefix(), but cf compact --before requires exact IDs.

Done condition: Either (1) compact resolves beforeMsgID via GetMessageByPrefix() for consistency, or (2) flag help text corrected to say exact ID required. Prefer (1).

Parent: workspace-o3l.3
close_reason: Deferred: minor UX.

title: SWEEP-SEC: handleRekey accepts campfire ID rename from any valid signer (no membership or authorization check)
description:
Severity: CRITICAL

POST /campfire/{old-id}/rekey renames the campfire in ALL local tables and replaces the campfire state file. The handler only checks:
1. That the request body has a valid Ed25519 signature
2. That the node is a member of old-id (GetMembership check at line 847)

But it does NOT verify:
- That the sender has authority to initiate a rekey (only the creator/evicting member should)
- That the RekeyMessageCBOR contains a valid campfire:rekey message signed by the OLD campfire key
- That the EvictedMemberPubkey is actually a member

Attack:
1. Any valid signer who knows an old campfire ID can initiate phase 1 (get an ephemeral key)
2. If they then send phase 2 with arbitrary EncryptedPrivKey, the node replaces its campfire private key
3. Even without phase 2 succeeding, phase 1 stores an ephemeral key in rekeySessions map

More critically: The RekeyMessageCBOR field is stored directly as a message without verifying that it is signed by the OLD campfire key. An attacker can inject arbitrary rekey messages.

Relevant code: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go
- handleRekey: lines 812-1012
- RekeyMessageCBOR stored without verification: lines 988-1009

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.
close_reason: Fixed: handleRekey now (1) verifies sender is a campfire member before processing, (2) verifies RekeyMessageCBOR.VerifySignature() before storing the rekey message. Tests: TestRekeyNonMemberForbidden, TestRekeyRejectsUnsignedRekeyMessage.

title: SWEEP-BUG: handleDeliver stores messages without verifying message signature
description:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go, handleDeliver (line 121-177)

handleDeliver verifies the HTTP request signature (the transport-level signature that the sender signs over the request body), but it does NOT verify the inner message signature (msg.Signature which covers the message content).

The transport-level check on line 135-139 proves the HTTP request came from the keyholder of senderHex. However, the CBOR-decoded message (msg) has its own Ed25519 signature (msg.Signature) over the message ID, payload, tags, antecedents, and timestamp. This signature is computed by the message author (msg.Sender) and is the message's authenticity proof.

A peer could craft a message with:
- msg.Sender = someOtherAgent.PublicKey (forged sender field)  
- msg.Signature = anything (invalid signature)
- Sign the HTTP request with their own key

The message would be stored with a spoofed Sender field. Recipients reading this message would see it appearing to come from someOtherAgent even though someOtherAgent never sent it.

msg.VerifySignature() exists and is called in the GitHub transport Poll() (transport.go line 211). It should also be called here.

Impact: message sender spoofing is possible in the P2P HTTP transport. Any member can make messages appear to come from any other member.
close_reason: Fixed by workspace-0fh — handleDeliver now verifies message signatures and membership

title: SWEEP-SEC: open-protocol campfire join leaks full peer list and campfire private key to unauthenticated public key
description: handleJoin() allows any agent with a valid Ed25519 keypair to join an open-protocol campfire. For threshold=1 campfires, the handler returns the full encrypted campfire private key (EncryptedPrivKey) and the complete peer list to any caller who sends a valid signature over any body. The signature only proves the caller controls the private key corresponding to the public key in X-Campfire-Sender — it does NOT require that key to be pre-registered anywhere. Combined with the JoinerPubkey mismatch (workspace-t24), this creates a compounding risk: any actor who knows a campfire ID can obtain the full peer list by simply generating a keypair and sending a join request. For an open campfire this is by design, but the peer list exposure should be considered: all existing member endpoints (IP addresses / hostnames) are disclosed. Additionally, the campfire private key for threshold=1 is transmitted in the first response — there is no challenge-response to verify liveness of the joiner's key. If the admitting node's ephemeral X25519 keypair can be observed (e.g., via a MitM), the private key is compromised.

title: SWEEP-SEC: GitHub transport token stored in plaintext credential file readable by any process
description: resolveGitHubToken() in cmd/cf/cmd/github.go reads the GitHub PAT from ~/.campfire/github-token (a plaintext file). The file permissions are not checked or enforced before reading. If the file is world-readable (e.g., due to umask misconfiguration or accidental chmod), any local process can read the GitHub token. The directory is created with 0700 (store.go line 118: os.MkdirAll), but the token file itself is not created by the campfire code — it is written by the user. There is no advisory or enforcement of 0600 permissions on this file. Compare with identity.Save() which explicitly writes with 0600. Also: the token is passed to the GitHub API client as a Bearer token in HTTP headers. Go's http.Client does not implement certificate pinning, so a local proxy (or --github-base-url override) could intercept the token. Impact: GitHub token exposure leading to repository access under the token's permissions. Mitigation: document or enforce 0600 on the credential file; warn if permissions are too open.

title: SWEEP-TEST: store.ClaimPendingThresholdShare has no tests
description:
File: pkg/store/store.go

ClaimPendingThresholdShare() uses SELECT+DELETE transaction to atomically claim a pending DKG share. StorePendingThresholdShare() stores one. Neither has any tests.

Missing: normal claim, no-share returns nil, multiple shares claim in order, second claim returns nil.

This is called during p2p-http join for threshold>1. If a share is not atomically removed, a second joiner gets the same share (participant ID collision in FROST DKG).

Done: store_test.go adds coverage for both functions.
close_reason: Fixed: ClaimPendingThresholdShare tests added (befb820)

title: SWEEP-SEC: campfire state files have mode 0644 in fs transport — private key world-readable
description: In pkg/transport/fs/fs.go, atomicWriteCBOR() writes files with mode 0644 (line 178: os.WriteFile(tmp, data, 0644)). This affects ALL campfire state files written via the fs transport, including campfire.cbor (which contains the campfire private key in CampfireState.PrivateKey). The campfire private key is the Ed25519 private key that signs provenance hops and authorizes campfire operations. With 0644, the file is readable by all users on the system. Compare with: identity.Save() uses 0600; handler_rekey.go line 223 uses os.WriteFile(newStateFile, newStateData, 0600). The join handler in cmd/cf/cmd/join.go line 173 also uses 0600. Only the fs transport path uses 0644. On shared systems (e.g., CI servers, multi-user workstations), any local user can read the campfire private key from /tmp/campfire/<id>/campfire.cbor. Impact: complete campfire impersonation — attacker can sign messages as the campfire, admit/evict members, and forge provenance hops. Mitigation: change 0644 to 0600 in atomicWriteCBOR.

title: Bug: ListReferencingMessages uses %q formatting in LIKE pattern — antecedent lookup never matches
description:
BUG FINDING from workspace-o3l.7

In pkg/store/store.go ListReferencingMessages():
  pattern := fmt.Sprintf("%%%q%%", messageID)

%q in Go quotes a string with Go string escaping: it wraps in double quotes and escapes special chars. The result is:
  %"some-message-id"%

But the antecedents column contains JSON like: ["some-message-id", "other-id"]

So the LIKE pattern should be: %"some-message-id"% which actually would work for exact UUID-style IDs (no special chars). HOWEVER: %q also escapes chars like \ with \\, and importantly, the % wildcard appears correctly.

Actually on closer inspection: for normal UUIDs with no special chars, %q adds enclosing double-quotes, making the pattern %"uuid"%. A JSON array ["uuid"] contains the substring "uuid" so this WOULD match. But it would also spuriously match messages whose payload (not antecedents) happened to contain the quoted string.

The more important issue: this uses LIKE without ESCAPE clause. If messageID contains %, _, or \, the pattern breaks. Furthermore the outer % wildcards are added after %q, which means for messageID="abc", pattern becomes %"abc"%, which works for JSON ["abc"] but NOT for ["abc","def"] because the contains check is correct.

Actually: re-reading more carefully, this works for finding exact antecedents — the %q adds quotes around the ID, and JSON serializes string IDs with quotes. The pattern %"id"% will match any array containing "id" as an element. This is subtly correct but fragile.

The real bug: no ESCAPE clause means if messageID contains % or _, the query returns wrong results.

File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/store/store.go:479

Fix: Use json_each for proper array contains semantics (consistent with how tags filtering works):
  SELECT ... FROM messages WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM json_each(antecedents) WHERE value = ?)

Done condition: ListReferencingMessages uses json_each for correct array membership check. Tests added for antecedent IDs containing special characters.
close_reason: Fixed: json_each replaces LIKE for antecedent lookup. Commit e64a658.

title: SWEEP-SEC: validateJoinerEndpoint vulnerable to DNS rebinding — SSRF bypass via TTL-0 DNS
description:
validateJoinerEndpoint in pkg/transport/http/handler.go resolves the hostname at validation time using net.LookupHost, then checks all resolved IPs against a private-range blocklist. However, if a DNS response has a very short TTL, the resolution at validation time may return a public IP (passes the check), but subsequent connections made by the server to that endpoint may resolve to a private/internal IP.

Attack path:
1. Attacker controls a domain attacker.example.com with DNS TTL=0
2. Initially the domain resolves to a public IP (e.g., 1.2.3.4) — passes validateJoinerEndpoint
3. Attacker sends a join request with JoinerEndpoint = "http://attacker.example.com:80/"
4. Server stores the endpoint and may later contact it (e.g., when delivering membership notifications or during eviction rekey)
5. Before the outbound connection, attacker flips DNS to resolve to 169.254.169.254 (AWS metadata) or 192.168.1.1 (internal router)
6. Server connects to the internal address, leaking internal service responses or enabling SSRF

Additionally, validateJoinerEndpoint does not block:
- IPv6 literals like http://[::ffff:192.168.1.1]/ (IPv4-mapped IPv6, not checked by isPrivateIP which only checks IPv6 addresses directly)
- The "0.0.0.0" address as a hostname (resolves to all interfaces on some systems)
- Redirects to internal addresses (if the endpoint returns 301/302 to an internal URL)

The endpoint is stored in the database and used later in peer-to-peer deliveries via cfhttp.DeliverToAll and membership notifications.

Severity: High — a member with issue-write access on a GitHub campfire, or any member of a p2p-http campfire with an open join protocol, can trigger SSRF against internal services reachable from the campfire server host.

Fix: Use a custom http.Transport with a DialContext that re-validates the resolved IP at connection time (time-of-use validation), not just at join time. Also expand the IPv4-mapped IPv6 check in isPrivateIP.

title: SWEEP-AP: Transport struct is a God Object mixing server, session, and peer state
description:
Pattern: God Object / SRP violation.

Location: pkg/transport/http/transport.go — the Transport struct (lines 38-67).

What it does: Transport owns the HTTP server lifecycle (Start/Stop/server), per-campfire peer routing (peers map), signing session ephemeral state (signSessions), rekey session ephemeral state (rekeySessions), self-identity info (selfPubKeyHex/selfEndpoint), provider callbacks (keyProvider/thresholdShareProvider), poll infrastructure (pollBroker), and TLS config. That is six distinct concerns in one type.

Why it's problematic: Any change to signing session logic, rekey state, or poll infrastructure requires touching the same lock (mu) and the same struct. The getOrCreateSignSession, pruneSignSessions, storeRekeySession, claimRekeySession, pruneRekeySessions methods all compete for the same write lock. Adding a new concern (e.g. metrics, connection pooling) requires extending this already-overloaded struct. Testing session expiry in isolation requires constructing a full Transport.

Suggested direction: Extract ephemeral session management into a separate SessionManager type. Extract peer routing into a PeerRegistry. Transport retains only server lifecycle + wiring.

title: SWEEP-SEC: SSRF via JoinerEndpoint field — server-side requests initiated to attacker-controlled URLs
description:
Severity: HIGH

The handleJoin handler stores the JoinerEndpoint field from the join request directly into peer_endpoints (line 591-597) and calls transport.AddPeer() (line 597) without validating the URL. Subsequently:

1. The stored endpoint is used in DeliverToAll() to make outbound HTTP POST requests
2. The endpoint is used in NotifyMembership() for membership notifications
3. The endpoint is used in SendSignRound() for threshold signing

An attacker-supplied JoinerEndpoint could be:
- An internal network address (SSRF to metadata services, internal APIs)
- A loopback address targeting the campfire server itself
- A file:// URL (behavior depends on Go\s http.Client handling)
- A non-HTTP protocol URL

The endpoint is also echoed back to other joining members in the Peers list of JoinResponse, which may propagate the malicious URL to other nodes.

No URL scheme validation, host validation, or SSRF protection is present.

Relevant code:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go handleJoin() lines 589-597
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/peer.go DeliverToAll(), NotifyMembership(), SendSignRound()

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.
close_reason: Fixed: validateJoinerEndpoint() added to handleJoin; rejects non-http/https schemes, private IPs (RFC1918, loopback, link-local, CGNAT, reserved), and unresolvable hosts. Tests TestJoinSSRFPrivateIPRejected, TestJoinFileSchemeRejected, TestJoinValidEndpointAccepted, TestValidateJoinerEndpointUnit verify the fix.

title: Test coverage: ListReferencingMessages has zero tests
description:
TEST COVERAGE FINDING from workspace-o3l.10

pkg/store/store.go ListReferencingMessages() has no tests. It is called by the read path (read.go printMessagesWithFields) to resolve "future"/"fulfills" tag status. The fragile %q LIKE pattern bug (workspace-kw9) was found precisely because there are no tests exercising this function.

Done condition: At minimum, tests covering:
1. Basic case: message m2 with antecedents=["m1"] is returned by ListReferencingMessages("m1")
2. Empty case: returns empty slice when no messages reference the given ID
3. Multi-antecedent case: message with antecedents=["m1","m3"] is found by both ListReferencingMessages("m1") and ListReferencingMessages("m3")

Tests should be in pkg/store/store_test.go (internal package).
close_reason: Deferred: test coverage gap, P2.

title: SWEEP-SEC: eviction fallback creates unsigned rekey message that passes signature verification
description: In cmd/cf/cmd/evict.go, evictThresholdN() at lines 302-321, when FROST signing fails, a fallback creates an 'unsigned' message (no Signature field) and still sends it to peers. The handler_rekey.go at lines 260-265 accepts this message if rekeyMsg.VerifySignature() returns true. Looking at message.VerifySignature(): if the Signature field is empty/nil, the verification will fail (ed25519.Verify returns false for a nil signature) and the message will be dropped. However the fallback message has Sender=ed25519.PublicKey(oldCFState.PublicKey) but no Signature. If VerifySignature() were to short-circuit on empty signatures (treating empty as 'no signature = unsigned = not verified'), the message could be accepted. More critically: the fallback is sent in RekeyMessageCBOR, which means even if the CBOR-encoded message is rejected at the server, the handler has already updated the campfire state (renamed the campfire ID, stored the new share, removed the evicted peer) BEFORE checking the rekey message at line 260. The rekey message storage is a post-condition, not a gate. The main danger is informational: the eviction proceeds without a signed audit trail. Impact: evictions in threshold>1 campfires may not produce verifiable audit records.

title: BUG: DB schema DEFAULT 'member' inconsistent with new role constants — stale legacy value in schema
description:
Severity: minor

The campfire_memberships table schema (pkg/store/store.go:18) defines:
  role TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'member'

'member' is a legacy value that EffectiveRole() maps to 'full'. The new canonical roles are observer/writer/full. The schema default should be DEFAULT 'full' to match the canonical constants and avoid storing legacy strings that only work by accident through EffectiveRole's fallback.

This doesn't cause a functional bug today (EffectiveRole handles it), but storing legacy values by default is a maintenance hazard — anyone querying the DB directly will see 'member' and not understand what role it means.

Additionally, join.go, autojoin.go all hardcode Role: "member" and create.go hardcodes Role: "creator" — these should use campfire.RoleFull.

File: pkg/store/store.go:18, cmd/cf/cmd/join.go:158,222,453, cmd/cf/cmd/autojoin.go:95, cmd/cf/cmd/create.go:147,248,391.

Done condition: schema DEFAULT is 'full', all AddMembership call sites use campfire.RoleFull constant (not the "member"/"creator" legacy strings).
close_reason: Replaced 'member' string literals with campfire.RoleFull constant at all AddMembership call sites (join.go x3, autojoin.go, cf-mcp/main.go). Updated DB schema DEFAULT from 'member' to 'full'. Added role constants, EffectiveRole(), Role field to MemberRecord/Member. Commit: 329703c

title: Test coverage: predicate evaluator has no depth/recursion limit tests
description:
TEST COVERAGE FINDING from workspace-o3l.10

The predicate evaluator in pkg/predicate/predicate.go has no tests for deep nesting. This is especially important given the security finding (workspace-x4v) about stack overflow from deeply nested predicates.

Once the depth limit fix (workspace-x4v) is implemented, tests must verify:
1. Predicates at exactly the depth limit parse successfully
2. Predicates exceeding the depth limit return an error from Parse()
3. Error message is clear about the depth limit

This bead blocks workspace-x4v (the fix) — tests should be added as part of implementing the fix.
close_reason: Tests shipped with workspace-x4v fix.

title: Security: non-constant-time sender comparison in key delivery
close_reason: Fixed: constant-time compare for key delivery. Commit 45329cc.

title: TEST-INCOMPLETE: TestRekeyProtocolThreshold2 signs locally rather than over HTTP — doesn't test the rekey sign path
description:
In rekey_test.go, TestRekeyProtocolThreshold2's post-rekey signing assertion (lines ~547-553) calls threshold.Sign() directly on the DKG results in memory, not via the HTTP sign handler:

  sig, err := threshold.Sign(newDKGResults, []uint32{1, 2}, signMsg)
  if !ed25519.Verify(newGroupKey, signMsg, sig) { ... }

This does not validate that B's newly-received FROST share (stored via the rekey protocol in B's SQLite store) works correctly when loaded by the HTTP sign handler. The critical path — B loading its share from the store, then using it in an HTTP-mediated FROST signing session — is not exercised.

By contrast, TestFROSTSign2of3OverHTTP correctly uses the HTTP transport for the full round-trip.

**Done condition:** Either extend TestRekeyProtocolThreshold2 to verify that B's rekey'd share works via the HTTP sign handler (start a sign session over HTTP after rekey), or create a new test for this flow.

**Source:** workspace-17qu.13 review finding.

title: SWEEP-TEST: pruneSignSessions / pruneRekeySessions — session expiry has zero test coverage
description:
pkg/transport/http/transport.go contains two session-pruning functions:
- pruneSignSessions(): removes signing sessions older than 5 minutes (called on every new session creation)
- pruneRekeySessions(): removes rekey sessions older than 5 minutes (called on every storeRekeySession)

Neither function has any test coverage. The removeSignSession function (which deletes a single session post-round-2) is also untested directly.

Missing tests:
1. pruneSignSessions: sessions created < 5 min ago survive; sessions created > 5 min ago are removed.
2. pruneRekeySessions: same contract.
3. removeSignSession: verify the session map entry is deleted after round 2.
4. Concurrent create/prune: ensure no panic or map corruption under concurrent access (mu is held but the test would document the invariant).

Why it matters: Without tests, a regression that causes premature pruning would silently drop in-progress signing sessions mid-protocol, causing threshold signing to fail with an opaque 'signing session not found' error. Conversely, a failure to prune would allow unbounded memory growth — a DoS vector in long-running servers.
close_reason: Implemented: 9 tests for pruneSignSessions/pruneRekeySessions/removeSignSession including concurrent stress test. No flakiness.

title: Tag-filtered reads at store level
description:
