title: Observer/writer/full membership roles with enforcement
description:
P0 — Implement three membership roles (observer, writer, full) with client-side enforcement.

## Current State
- `pkg/store/store.go` — Membership struct has `Role string` field but only stores "creator"/"member" locally
- `pkg/campfire/campfire.go` — Member struct has no role field
- `pkg/transport/fs/fs.go` — MemberRecord is written as CBOR, no role enforcement
- No protocol-level role concept — all members can read and write equally

## Target State
Three roles:
- **observer**: Can read messages only. Cannot send. Client-side enforcement: `cf send` refuses if local membership role is observer.
- **writer**: Can read and send messages. Cannot sign campfire:* system messages (join, compact, view). Client-side enforcement.
- **full**: Existing behavior. Can read, write, and sign system messages. Default for backward compatibility.

Implementation:
1. Add `Role` field to fs transport MemberRecord (CBOR): `Role string` with values "observer", "writer", "full". Default empty = "full" (backward compat).
2. Add `Role` field to `pkg/campfire/campfire.go` Member struct.
3. `cf admit` gets `--role` flag (default "full").
4. `cf send` checks local role before sending — error if observer.
5. `cf send` with campfire:* tags checks role — error if not full.
6. Store membership role tracks the protocol role (update Membership struct).

## Files to Modify
- `pkg/campfire/campfire.go` — add Role to Member
- `pkg/transport/fs/fs.go` — add Role to MemberRecord, WriteMember, ListMembers
- `cmd/cf/cmd/admit.go` — add --role flag
- `cmd/cf/cmd/send.go` — role enforcement before sending
- `pkg/store/store.go` — update Membership struct, schema if needed
- Tests for each change

## Constraints
- Backward compatible: existing campfires without roles default to "full"
- Phase 1 is client-side only (signing proxy enforcement is out of scope for campfire)
- Transport-enforced roles (server rejects observer writes) are P2/future
- Do not break existing CBOR deserialization — empty/missing Role = "full"

## Test-First Sequence
1. Write tests: observer cannot send, writer can send but not campfire:* tags, full can do everything, empty role defaults to full
2. Implement role in campfire.go and fs transport
3. Implement enforcement in send.go
4. Add --role to admit.go
5. Run full test suite

title: Compaction events (campfire:compact)
description:
P1 — Implement campfire:compact message type for local compaction.

## Design Reference
docs/automaton-substrate-design.md §P1.3

## Target State
A new `cf compact` command that:
1. Takes a campfire ID and optional message range (default: all messages before a threshold)
2. Creates a CompactionEvent message:
   - tag: "campfire:compact"
   - payload: JSON with `supersedes` (list of message IDs), `summary` (compacted content as bytes), `retention` ("archive"|"discard"), `checkpoint_hash` (SHA-256 Merkle root of superseded message IDs+signatures)
   - antecedents: [ID of last superseded message]
   - Signed by campfire key (requires "full" role)
3. Writes the compaction event as a regular campfire message
4. Store.ListMessages respects compaction: when a compaction event exists, messages in `supersedes` list are excluded from default reads (but still stored)
5. New members joining start from the latest compaction snapshot

Store changes:
- New method: `Store.ListCompactionEvents(campfireID)` — returns compaction messages
- `Store.ListMessages` gains optional `respectCompaction bool` — when true, excludes superseded messages
- Compaction events themselves always appear in message lists

CLI:
- `cf compact <campfire-id>` — creates compaction event (full role required)
- `cf compact <campfire-id> --before <msg-id>` — compact messages before the given message
- `cf read --all` shows all messages including compacted; default read respects compaction

## Files to Create
- `cmd/cf/cmd/compact.go` — compact command
- `cmd/cf/cmd/compact_test.go` — tests

## Files to Modify
- `pkg/store/store.go` — compaction-aware ListMessages, ListCompactionEvents
- `pkg/store/store_test.go` — tests for compaction filtering
- `cmd/cf/cmd/read.go` — wire compaction awareness into default read path
- `cmd/cf/cmd/root.go` — register compact command

## Constraints
- Compaction is append-only — never deletes messages from store
- Compaction is a message like any other (signed, timestamped, tagged)
- Only "full" role members can compact (role check from roles bead)
- Distributed compaction with quorum is out of scope — local only
- checkpoint_hash is SHA-256 of sorted(superseded_id + "|" + signature_hex) for verifiability

## Test-First Sequence
1. Write tests: compact creates valid compaction event; ListMessages with respectCompaction excludes superseded; compaction events always visible; --all ignores compaction; role enforcement
2. Implement store methods
3. Implement compact command
4. Wire into read path
5. Full test suite green

## Dependencies
- Depends on: membership roles bead (needs "full" role enforcement)

## Agent Type

title: Named views (campfire:view) with predicate grammar
description:
P1 — Implement campfire:view message type and view materializer.

## Design Reference
docs/automaton-substrate-design.md §P1.4

## Overview
This is the largest feature. It has two parts:
1. View definition — a campfire:view message that defines a named view (predicate, projection, ordering, limit, refresh strategy)
2. View materializer — reads messages, applies the predicate, computes projections, returns results

## Predicate Grammar
The predicate language must support:
- Tag match: `tag = "memory:standing"`, `tag IN ("memory:standing", "memory:proposed")`
- Sender match: `sender = "<hex>"`
- Time predicates: `timestamp > <unix_nano>`, `timestamp BETWEEN <a> AND <b>`
- Payload field predicates: `payload.confidence = "high"`, `payload.category = "identity"`
- Boolean composition: `AND`, `OR`, `NOT`, parentheses
- Numeric computation: `payload.confidence * pow(0.9, sessions_since_reference)` for weight expressions

Implementation approach: Define predicate as a simple expression AST. Parse from a string syntax. Evaluate against message records. Store as JSON in the view definition payload.

Grammar (S-expression style for simplicity and unambiguous parsing):
```
(and (tag "memory:standing") (gt (field "payload.confidence") 0.5))
(or (tag "memory:standing") (tag "memory:anchor"))
(gt (mul (field "payload.confidence") (pow 0.9 (field "payload.sessions_since"))) 0.10)
```

## Target State
- `cf view create <campfire-id> <name> --predicate "<expr>" [--projection "id,tags,payload"] [--ordering "timestamp desc"] [--limit N] [--refresh on-read]`
  - Creates a campfire:view message (requires "full" role)
- `cf view read <campfire-id> <name>`
  - Materializes the view: finds latest campfire:view with that name, applies predicate to all messages, returns filtered/projected/ordered results
- `cf view list <campfire-id>` — lists defined views

## Files to Create
- `pkg/predicate/predicate.go` — AST types, parser, evaluator
- `pkg/predicate/predicate_test.go` — extensive tests for grammar
- `cmd/cf/cmd/view.go` — view create/read/list commands
- `cmd/cf/cmd/view_test.go` — integration tests

## Files to Modify
- `pkg/store/store.go` — helper to list messages matching a predicate (or reuse ListMessages + Go-level predicate eval)
- `cmd/cf/cmd/root.go` — register view command

## Constraints
- View definitions are messages — they live in the campfire like everything else
- Views are campfire:* system messages — require "full" role
- Predicates evaluate in Go (not SQL) — keeps the predicate language portable across transports
- Projection is Go-level field selection on the output
- Ordering: "timestamp asc" (default) or "timestamp desc"
- Refresh strategy stored in view definition but only "on-read" implemented in P1
- Weight computation predicates work on payload JSON fields (messages must have JSON payloads for field access)
- Do not implement "on-write" or "periodic" refresh strategies — store in view definition but error if selected

## Test-First Sequence
1. Write predicate parser tests: tag match, sender match, time predicates, payload field access, boolean composition, numeric computation (mul, pow, gt, lt)
2. Implement predicate AST, parser, evaluator
3. Write view command tests: create stores view message, read materializes, list shows views
4. Implement view commands
5. Full test suite green

## Dependencies
- Depends on: tag-filtered reads (for efficient initial filtering before predicate eval)
- Depends on: membership roles (for "full" role enforcement on view creation)

## Agent Type

model-tier: opus (novel predicate grammar design + implementation)

title: Projection reads (cf read --fields)
description:
P2 — Implement field projection on read output.

## Design Reference
docs/automaton-substrate-design.md §P2.5

## Target State
- `cf read --fields id,sender,tags,payload` returns only requested fields
- Reduces output size by 60-80% for bulk reads
- Works with both human-readable and --json output
- Works with --tag, --sender, --all, --follow flags

## Implementation
- Add `--fields` flag to read command (comma-separated string)
- Parse into field set: valid fields are id, sender, payload, tags, antecedents, timestamp, signature, provenance, instance, campfire_id
- In human-readable output: only print requested fields
- In JSON output: only include requested fields in JSON objects
- Default (no --fields): all fields (backward compatible)

## Files to Modify
- `cmd/cf/cmd/read.go` — add --fields flag, field projection in output formatting
- `cmd/cf/cmd/read_test.go` or new test file — test projection output

## Constraints
- Pure output-level filtering — no store changes needed
- Must work with all existing read modes (one-shot, --follow, --pull)
- Invalid field names produce an error before reading

## Test-First Sequence
1. Write tests: --fields with valid fields returns only those; invalid field errors; default returns all; works with --json
2. Implement flag parsing and output projection
3. Full test suite green

## Dependencies
- No hard dependencies (can run in parallel with P0/P1 work)

## Agent Type
agent-type: implementer
model-tier: sonnet
close_reason: Implemented --fields flag on cf read. parseFieldSet validates field names, printMessagesWithFields does projection for human-readable output, encodeMessagesJSONWithFields for JSON output. nil fields = all fields (backward compat). 9 tests added covering valid/invalid fields, human output projection, JSON projection, and command-level error. All tests pass. Commit 8287e33.

title: SWEEP-TEST: store.UpdateMembershipRole / GetReadCursor / SetReadCursor / HasMessage — zero test coverage
description:
Four exported store functions have zero test coverage in pkg/store/store_test.go:

1. store.UpdateMembershipRole — updates role for an existing membership. Returns an error ('membership not found') if the campfire_id does not exist. The not-found error path is entirely untested.
2. store.GetReadCursor — returns the last-read timestamp for a campfire, or 0 if no cursor exists. The 'no cursor exists' path is untested, as is the 'cursor exists' path.
3. store.SetReadCursor — upserts a read cursor. No test verifies persistence or the UPSERT behavior (overwriting an existing cursor).
4. store.HasMessage — checks if a message ID exists. No test exercises this function.

Why it matters: UpdateMembershipRole has an error path that returns a misleading 'rows affected = 0' error instead of a wrapped sentinel. Missing tests here mean a regression in the error message or logic goes undetected. GetReadCursor / SetReadCursor are used by the read command and cf-mcp server to manage read position — a silent regression could cause duplicate message delivery or missed messages.
close_reason: Implemented: 7 unit tests covering UpdateMembershipRole, GetReadCursor, SetReadCursor, HasMessage — all happy path and error/edge cases.

title: SWEEP-AP: handler.go mixes SSRF validation, path sanitization, event types, and routing
description:
Pattern: SRP violation.

Location: pkg/transport/http/handler.go — 185 lines containing:
1. validateJoinerEndpoint (SSRF prevention, with DNS resolution)
2. isPrivateIP (builds and iterates private CIDR ranges on every call)
3. sanitizeTransportDir (path traversal prevention)
4. MembershipEvent type definition
5. CampfireKeyProvider type definition
6. handler struct definition
7. checkMembership method
8. route method (the actual dispatcher)

The isPrivateIP function rebuilds the same []string of CIDR strings and calls net.ParseCIDR on each one on every invocation — it has no cache. Called on every join request.

Why it's problematic:
- Concerns span security validation (SSRF, path traversal), type definitions, and HTTP routing in one file.
- isPrivateIP allocates and re-parses 11 CIDR blocks on every call. A package-level var []net.IPNet would allocate once at init time.

Suggested direction: Move MembershipEvent and CampfireKeyProvider type definitions to a types.go. Move SSRF/path validation to a validate.go. Parse private CIDRs into a package-level var at init time. Keep handler struct, checkMembership, and route in handler.go.

title: SWEEP-BUG: handleSign Round 1 ss.Start() and ss.Deliver() operate on session after mutex released
description:
In handler_sign.go handleSign(), for Round 1 the code does:
1. Lock t.mu (write)
2. getOrCreateSignSession (creates SigningSession, stores in t.signSessions map)
3. Unlock t.mu
4. ss.Start() — on the session WITHOUT holding the lock
5. ss.Deliver() for each inbound message — on the session WITHOUT holding the lock

Meanwhile, pruneSignSessions() (called inside getOrCreateSignSession while locked) can delete sessions older than 5 minutes. If two concurrent round-1 requests arrive for the same session_id: both call getOrCreateSignSession — the first creates the session, the second returns the existing one. Then both call ss.Start() and ss.Deliver() concurrently on the same SigningSession without any synchronization.

The FROST library's state.State uses internal channels but is not documented as safe for concurrent method calls. Concurrent ss.Start() calls on the same session could double-initialize the commitment phase, producing duplicate round-1 messages or corrupting the state machine.

Trigger: two concurrent POST /campfire/{id}/sign requests with the same session_id and round=1.
Impact: P1 - possible FROST state machine corruption; could produce invalid signatures or panic.
close_reason: Fixed by workspace-uwf4: per-session lock now protects Start/Deliver in round 1.

title: SWEEP-TEST: store.ClaimPendingThresholdShare race — concurrent joiners claim same share
description: store.go: ClaimPendingThresholdShare uses a transaction to SELECT + DELETE a pending share atomically. If two joiner requests arrive concurrently, only one should receive a share — the other should get nil. No test verifies this: (a) concurrent calls to ClaimPendingThresholdShare with two goroutines race to claim — exactly one succeeds with a non-nil share, the other gets nil; (b) the transaction correctly rolls back if the DELETE fails; (c) after all shares are claimed, subsequent calls return nil without error. This is a P1 because a race condition here could result in two joiners receiving the same FROST share — catastrophic for threshold security. Done condition: a concurrent test of ClaimPendingThresholdShare with 2 goroutines claiming from a pool of 1 share.

title: Security/Bug: isCompactionEvent uses substring match — false positive on tag like 'not-campfire:compact'
description:
SECURITY + BUG FINDING from workspace-o3l.6

Both isCompactionEvent (store.go:375) and isCompactionMsg (compact.go:239) use:
  strings.Contains(m.Tags, `"campfire:compact"`)

This is a JSON string contains check. A tag like "not-campfire:compact" or "xycampfire:compact" would match because the JSON encoding of the tags array would contain the substring "campfire:compact".

While the attack surface is limited (only affects compaction filtering, not auth), a message with tag "bad-campfire:compact-extension" would be incorrectly treated as a compaction event and always shown even when RespectCompaction=true.

Files:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/store/store.go:375 (isCompactionEvent)
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/compact.go:239 (isCompactionMsg)

Fix: Use proper JSON array parsing to check for exact tag match, not substring. OR use json_each SQL approach (already done in ListCompactionEvents query — copy that pattern).

Done condition: Both functions use exact array element comparison. Tests verify a tag "xycampfire:compact" does not cause a message to be treated as a compaction event.
close_reason: Fixed: HasTag with JSON parse replaces strings.Contains. Commit e64a658.

title: SWEEP-BUG: handleJoin persists joiner endpoint before membership check completes for invite-only campfires
description:
In handler_join.go handleJoin(), the joiner's endpoint is persisted via h.store.UpsertPeerEndpoint() and h.transport.AddPeer() unconditionally at line 264-270, after the join response is fully built. The invite-only check at line 128-147 correctly rejects unauthorized joiners — but only after the signature is verified. If the campfire is open protocol (no invite-only check), an unauthenticated joiner's pubkey+endpoint is stored in peer_endpoints immediately after signature verification passes, before any other membership validation.

The deeper problem: for invite-only campfires, the membership check at line 130 checks ListPeerEndpoints — but this is the same table that is about to be written. A concurrent join attempt from the same pubkey could race: one request reads the peer list (not yet present, so not admitted), while a concurrent request writes the peer endpoint. Both would be rejected, but the written record would remain in peer_endpoints even for a rejected join.

Trigger: concurrent join attempts from the same pubkey to an invite-only campfire.
Impact: P2 - rejected joiners can leave stale peer endpoint records in the store, polluting the peer list.

title: TEST-GAP: no negative test for sign handler membership check — non-member can trigger FROST round 1
description:
The FROST sign handler (handleSign in handler.go) performs auth header verification (Ed25519 signature check) but there is NO membership check — any authenticated peer can initiate a signing session for any campfire, even if they are not a member.

The e2e test TestFROSTSign2of3OverHTTP only tests the happy path where all three participants are legitimate members. There is no test that:
1. A non-member agent (no peer_endpoint record) sends a POST /campfire/{id}/sign → expects 403.
2. An authenticated agent for a DIFFERENT campfire cannot create sessions in this campfire.

This is a gap in the security test matrix. The poll handler has a membership check (handlePoll lines 267-283); sign does not. Whether this is intentional (signing is always between known members who share DKG state) or a missing check is unclear from the tests alone.

**Done condition:** Either add a test confirming non-members are rejected from /sign, or create a note in the spec that /sign intentionally omits membership checks with justification (the FROST share lookup fails for non-participants anyway).

**Source:** workspace-17qu.13 review finding.

File: pkg/store/store.go UpdateMembershipRole()

- Normal update: role changes and is readable

title: SWEEP-SEC: handleMembership accepts peer list manipulation from any valid signer (no membership check)
description:
Severity: HIGH

POST /campfire/{id}/membership updates the node's in-memory peer list and persistent peer_endpoints table based on an inbound MembershipEvent. The handler verifies the Ed25519 signature but does NOT verify that the sender is a current member of the campfire.

Attack:
- Any actor with a valid keypair can send a "join" event adding themselves or a malicious endpoint
- Any actor can send a "leave" or "evict" event removing legitimate members from the local peer list
- This enables routing manipulation: messages stop being delivered to evicted peers, or are re-routed to attacker-controlled endpoints

This is a confusion between HTTP-level authentication (valid keypair) and authorization (is this agent a campfire member with authority to announce membership changes?).

Relevant code: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go lines 379-421

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.
close_reason: Fixed: handleMembership now verifies sender is a campfire member before updating peer list. Non-members get 403. Tests: TestMembershipNonMemberForbidden, TestMembershipMemberAllowed.

title: SWEEP-TEST: handleRekey - no test for phase-1-skipped attack (phase 2 without prior phase 1)
description:
File: pkg/transport/http/handler.go handleRekey()

The rekey handler has a two-phase protocol. Phase 2 requires a cached receiver ephemeral key (stored by phase 1). If an attacker sends a phase-2 request without a prior phase-1, the handler returns 400 "no pending rekey session". This is tested in TestRekeyProtocolThreshold1 as a happy-path test, but the following cases are not tested:

Missing tests:
- Phase 2 with no prior phase 1 (storeRekeySession was never called): returns 400 (no test)
- Phase 2 with wrong SenderX25519Pub (key not in cache): returns 400 (no test)
- Phase 2 with invalid encrypted payload: decryption failure path returns 400 (no test)
- Phase 1 with invalid SenderX25519Pub hex: bad hex decode should return 400 (not tested - actually missing validation; currently just stored as-is)
- Rekey to non-member campfire (membership not found): returns 404 (not tested)
- Missing new_campfire_id or sender_x25519_pub fields: returns 400 (not tested)

The phase-1-skipped case and wrong-key case are security-critical: they test that the key cache cannot be polluted or bypassed.

Done: unit tests cover all missing error paths in handleRekey.
close_reason: Fixed by workspace-j4j — test added for phase-1-skipped attack (TestRekeyNonMemberForbidden)

title: Antipattern: campfire:* system tag strings are magic literals scattered across 6+ files
description:
ANTIPATTERN FINDING from workspace-o3l.9

System tag strings are hardcoded as magic literals across multiple files with no central constants:
- "campfire:compact" — compact.go:241, store.go:377, store.go:423, read.go (comment)
- "campfire:view" — view.go (multiple places)
- "campfire:member-joined" — join.go, autojoin.go, admit.go
- "campfire:join-request" — join.go
- "campfire:key-delivery" — join.go

This is an antipattern because:
1. A typo in any file silently creates a non-functional tag
2. Renaming a tag requires a grep+replace across all files
3. Tests that check these tags may use different capitalization

The role constants (RoleObserver, RoleWriter, RoleFull) in pkg/campfire/campfire.go are the correct pattern — follow that.

Fix: Add tag constants to pkg/campfire/campfire.go:
  const (
      TagCompact      = "campfire:compact"
      TagView         = "campfire:view"
      TagMemberJoined = "campfire:member-joined"
      TagJoinRequest  = "campfire:join-request"
      TagKeyDelivery  = "campfire:key-delivery"
  )

Replace all magic string literals with these constants.

Done condition: All campfire:* tag values use exported constants. Tests pass. No raw "campfire:" string literals in non-test Go files except for HasPrefix checks in hasSystemTag().

title: SWEEP-AP: Campfire and CampfireState are near-duplicate types without a clear conversion contract
description:
Pattern: Leaky abstraction / implicit coupling.

Location: pkg/campfire/campfire.go — Campfire (lines 37-44) and CampfireState (lines 53-62), plus MemberRecord (lines 65-69) which parallels Member (lines 47-51).

Campfire: has Identity *identity.Identity, Members []Member (with Role)
CampfireState: has PublicKey/PrivateKey []byte (flattened from Identity), no Members field
MemberRecord: same fields as Member

The relationship is:
- Campfire is the live/in-memory form
- CampfireState is the on-disk serialization form
- Member and MemberRecord are identical except for the type name

Issues:
1. The State() method produces CampfireState but discards the Members slice entirely — callers that need members must also write separate MemberRecord files. This implicit split contract is not documented on the type.
2. MemberRecord and Member have the same fields (PublicKey []byte, JoinedAt int64, Role string) but are separate types. Any new field added to Member must be manually mirrored to MemberRecord and vice versa, with no compile-time enforcement.
3. campfireFromState() in join.go reconstructs a Campfire from CampfireState + []MemberRecord, duplicating the construction logic.

Suggested direction: Alias MemberRecord = Member or embed one in the other with a conversion method. Document explicitly on CampfireState that it does not carry Members (filesystem transport stores those separately). Consider whether the Campfire/CampfireState split belongs in the campfire package or in the fs transport package.

title: SWEEP-SEC: handleDeliver accepts messages with spoofed Sender field — any member can impersonate another
description:
handleDeliver in pkg/transport/http/handler_message.go accepts a CBOR-encoded message and stores it directly without verifying that the message inner Sender field matches the authenticated senderHex from the request signature.

Attack scenario: A campfire member M1 constructs an HTTP POST to /campfire/{id}/deliver where:
- The X-Campfire-Sender header is M1s own public key (passes auth)
- The X-Campfire-Signature is M1s valid Ed25519 signature over the body (passes auth)
- The CBOR body contains a Message where msg.Sender = M2s public key bytes

Result: The message is stored in SQLite attributed to M2 (the spoofed member). All peers who sync or poll this message will see it as if authored by M2. The msg.Signature field (if present) will fail verification against M2s key, but handleDeliver does NOT call VerifySignature() — it just calls store.AddMessage() directly.

Compare: The GitHub transport (pkg/transport/github/transport.go line 218) explicitly calls msg.VerifySignature() before storing. The HTTP transport has no equivalent check.

Impact: Any member can forge messages appearing to come from any other member (or from the campfire identity itself). This breaks the core trust model — provenance chains become meaningless. An attacker member can inject false campfire:rekey, campfire:compact, or campfire:member-joined system messages attributed to the campfire key.

Fix: In handleDeliver, verify msg.VerifySignature() before storing. Also verify that msg.Sender matches senderHex (the authenticated sender from the request headers).
close_reason: Implemented: verify msg.Sender matches authenticated sender and call msg.VerifySignature() in handleDeliver. Commit b795708, pushed.

title: SWEEP-SEC: handleJoin admits unauthenticated pubkey — JoinerPubkey not verified against X-Campfire-Sender
description: In handleJoin (pkg/transport/http/handler_join.go:263-270), after verifying the Ed25519 signature against X-Campfire-Sender, the server persists req.JoinerPubkey (from the JSON body) to the peer_endpoints table — NOT senderHex (the verified sender from the header). These can differ: a hostile joiner can set JoinerPubkey to any arbitrary hex string while signing the request as a different key. Effect: the attacker injects a fake pubkey into the peer list. For invite-only campfires, the membership check at line 130 compares senderHex against the peer list. But after joining, future membership checks for the injected pubkey will incorrectly pass. Also: AddPeer(campfireID, req.JoinerPubkey, req.JoinerEndpoint) at line 270 registers the unverified pubkey in the in-memory peer map. The fix is to use senderHex (the verified key from the header) instead of req.JoinerPubkey when persisting/registering the peer. Attack scenario: Alice joins campfire X. She sets JoinerPubkey=<Bob's pubkey> while signing with her own key. Bob (who was never admitted) now has a peer entry that passes membership checks and can send messages or participate in threshold signing under Bob's identity.
close_reason: Already fixed in workspace-17qu.4 (commit c8e68ba). handler_join.go lines 244-250 correctly use senderHex (verified header) instead of req.JoinerPubkey (unverified body) for both UpsertPeerEndpoint and AddPeer. Regression tests in pkg/transport/http/join_pubkey_test.go cover pubkey injection attack (TestJoinPubkeyInjectionPrevented) and normal join path (TestJoinMatchingPubkeyWorks) — all pass.

title: SWEEP-AP: Duplicated auth preamble in every HTTP handler
description:
Pattern: Copy-paste / SRP violation.

Location: pkg/transport/http/handler_message.go, handler_join.go, handler_sign.go, handler_rekey.go — every handler repeats the same ~15-line block.

The repeated block:
  r.Body = http.MaxBytesReader(w, r.Body, maxRequestBodySize)
  body, err := io.ReadAll(r.Body)   // (for POST handlers)
  senderHex := r.Header.Get("X-Campfire-Sender")
  sigB64 := r.Header.Get("X-Campfire-Signature")
  if senderHex == "" || sigB64 == "" { http.Error(401) }
  if err := verifyRequestSignature(...); err != nil { http.Error(401) }

handleSync and handlePoll also repeat a variant without body reading. handleJoin additionally re-does the membership check inside its invite-only block after checkMembership already ran.

Why it's problematic: Any change to auth (new header name, different error code, rate-limit hook) requires editing 5+ files. The current duplication makes it easy for one handler to accidentally omit a step; handler_join currently does NOT call checkMembership before the invite-only check, which means an unadmitted agent can hit the key provider path before the membership gate fires.

Suggested direction: Extract an authenticateRequest(w, r, campfireID) middleware function that returns (senderHex, body, ok). Wire it as a first step in route() or as an http.Handler wrapper. This centralizes auth and eliminates copy-paste.
close_reason: Already fixed: authMiddleware centralizes auth preamble. No per-handler duplication remains.

title: SWEEP-TEST: store.UpdateCampfireID - no test for transaction atomicity
description:
File: pkg/store/store.go UpdateCampfireID()

UpdateCampfireID() runs a transaction that updates campfire_id across 7 tables (campfire_memberships, read_cursors, filters, peer_endpoints, threshold_shares, pending_threshold_shares, messages). There is no test for this function.

Missing tests:
- Basic rename: old ID disappears, new ID has all records
- Rename of non-existent ID: succeeds silently (no rows updated, no error)
- Messages table FK update (messages has a FK on campfire_memberships)
- Atomicity: if one table update fails mid-transaction, none should apply

This function is called during eviction/rekey. If it partially succeeds and rolls back, the campfire is in an inconsistent state (old membership still present, new state file written). No test validates the transaction behavior.

Done: store_test.go adds TestUpdateCampfireID covering normal case, missing ID case, and verifying all 7 tables are updated.
close_reason: Fixed: UpdateCampfireID atomicity test added (befb820)

title: SWEEP-AP: minInt and min16 utility functions defined in multiple files
description:
Small string/int truncation utilities are redefined in multiple files:
- cmd/cf/cmd/send.go: minInt(a, b int) int (line 504)
- cmd/cf/cmd/compact.go: uses minInt (imported from send.go via same package)
- cmd/cf/cmd/join.go: min16(s string) int (line 525) - returns min(len(s), 16)
- cmd/cf/cmd/role.go: minInt(12, len(campfireID)) also used

These are micro-utilities scattered in implementation files. Go 1.21+ provides built-in min(). If targeting older Go, a single shared util.go in the cmd package should hold these. Currently min16 and minInt serve slightly different purposes (string len cap vs. int min) but both exist for the same pattern of "safe truncate a hex string for display".

Severity: LOW - minor noise, but each is a definition that could be a single shared helper.
close_reason: Fixed: replaced minInt/min16 helper functions with built-in min() (Go 1.21+); removed duplicate definitions

title: Dead code: baseDir variable in compact.go execCompact is assigned but never used
description:
DEAD CODE FINDING from workspace-o3l.8

In cmd/cf/cmd/compact.go execCompact() lines 193-196:
  transportDir := m.TransportDir
  baseDir := transportDir
  if baseDir != "" {
      baseDir = filepath.Dir(transportDir)
  }

The variable baseDir is assigned but never used after this block. Only transportDir is passed to sendFilesystem(). The filepath import in compact.go is only used here — removing baseDir would allow removing the filepath import too.

File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/compact.go:193-196

Fix: Remove the baseDir variable and the filepath.Dir computation. Remove the filepath import.

Done condition: baseDir and filepath import removed. Tests pass. go vet clean.

title: SWEEP-TEST: identity.EncryptToEd25519Key — empty plaintext and minimum-size ciphertext rejection not tested
description: pkg/identity/x25519.go: EncryptToEd25519Key and DecryptWithEd25519Key are tested for round-trip, wrong key, tampered, and truncated cases. Missing tests: (a) encrypting empty plaintext (len=0) — valid operation, but the wire format minimum size check in DecryptWithEd25519Key requires >=60 bytes (32+12+16), so encrypting empty results in exactly 60 bytes; (b) ciphertext of exactly 59 bytes (one byte short of minimum) should be rejected with 'ciphertext too short'; (c) Ed25519ToX25519Pub with a 32-byte all-zeros key (the identity point) — should return an error or produce a low-order point; (d) Ed25519ToX25519Priv with a 32-byte key (wrong length — Go Ed25519 private keys are 64 bytes) should return an error. Done condition: tests for edge cases (b), (c), (d) above.

title: SWEEP-TEST: handlePoll membership check can be bypassed with unknown sender
description:
File: pkg/transport/http/handler.go handlePoll()

handlePoll() has a membership check: the sender must either equal transport.SelfInfo() pubkey or appear in store.ListPeerEndpoints(campfireID). This check is NOT tested.

A sender who is not in peer_endpoints and is not self can be blocked (403 Forbidden). But:
1. If store.ListPeerEndpoints() returns an error, isMember stays false and the request is rejected - this is correct behavior but untested.
2. There is no test verifying that an unknown sender gets 403.
3. There is no test verifying that a known peer (in peer_endpoints) can poll.
4. The poll handler accepts any agent whose pubkey matches the self key, even for campfires the self is not a member of - untested.

Missing tests:
- Unknown sender: 403 Forbidden (P1 - membership enforcement)  
- Known peer: 200 with messages or 204 (no test for member-passes-check path)
- Self-sender on own campfire: 200 (not explicitly tested)

Done: poll_handler_test.go (exists but may not cover membership check) should be audited and extended.
notes: CORRECTION: TestHandlePollNonMember (poll_handler_test.go:273) explicitly tests the 403 Forbidden case for a non-member sender. The core 403 path IS tested. Remaining gaps: (1) no test that a known peer (in peer_endpoints but not self) passes the membership check with 200/204; (2) no test for self-sender on a campfire the self is not a member of (boundaryless self-pass). Bead title is inaccurate — 403 membership enforcement is tested.
close_reason: Fixed: handlePoll refactored to use shared checkMembership() helper. Added TestPollKnownPeerAllowed verifying that a peer in peer_endpoints (but not self key) can poll successfully. Core 403 path already tested by TestHandlePollNonMember.

title: SWEEP-BUG: handleSign round 2 holds global transport write lock during FROST ProcessAll/Deliver — blocks all other HTTP operations
description:
File: pkg/transport/http/handler_sign.go, lines 101-128

In the round-2 branch of handleSign, the code acquires h.transport.mu.Lock() at line 101 and does NOT release it until line 128, after calling:
- ss.ProcessAll() (line 112)
- ss.Deliver() in a loop (line 120)
- ss.ProcessAll() again (line 124)
- delete(h.transport.signSessions, req.SessionID) (line 127)

h.transport.mu is the global RWMutex used for all transport state (peers, signSessions, rekeySessions, selfInfo, keyProvider, thresholdShareProvider). Holding it as a write lock during FROST cryptographic operations means every concurrent HTTP handler that acquires h.transport.mu (any lock acquisition in handleSign, handleJoin, handleRekey, AddPeer, RemovePeer, SelfInfo, etc.) is blocked until round 2 completes.

For high-latency FROST operations (the state machine processes elliptic curve arithmetic), this can block for tens to hundreds of milliseconds.

Conditions: Any concurrent HTTP request to the same Transport while a FROST round-2 is in progress.

Impact:
- All concurrent handlers (message delivery, peer polling, other sign requests) are serialized behind round-2 processing.
- For a node with multiple campfires and many peers, this becomes a significant throughput bottleneck.
- Particularly bad if a round-2 request hangs (e.g., FROST state machine stuck) — the transport is deadlocked until the request is cancelled.

The stated reason for holding the lock is to prevent pruneSignSessions from deleting the session. A narrower fix would be: acquire the lock only to extract the session pointer and release, then operate on the session under the per-session mu, then re-acquire briefly to delete the session entry.
close_reason: Narrowed global transport write lock in handleSign round 2: lock now held only briefly to extract session pointer, FROST crypto runs under per-session lock. Added TestSignRound2DoesNotBlockPeers. Committed 6f23047 on work/workspace-dq5g.

title: SWEEP-AP: isPeerHTTPCampfire in cmd/send.go duplicates isPeerHTTPTransportDir in pkg/transport/transport.go
description:
Pattern: Copy-paste / implicit coupling.

Location: cmd/cf/cmd/send.go lines 154-159 (isPeerHTTPCampfire) and pkg/transport/transport.go lines 60-68 (isPeerHTTPTransportDir).

Both functions perform the identical filesystem-stat heuristic to detect p2p-HTTP campfires: join transportDir + campfireID + ".cbor" and os.Stat the result. The pkg/transport/transport.go file even has a comment saying "isPeerHTTPTransportDir mirrors the cmd-layer isPeerHTTPCampfire check" — acknowledging the duplication explicitly but not fixing it.

This means the detection logic is in two places. If the heuristic ever changes (e.g., the state file gets renamed or the detection criterion changes), both must be updated.

Why it matters: The pkg/transport layer has the correct abstraction (ResolveType returns a typed enum). The cmd layer should call transport.ResolveType() instead of reimplementing the same stat check. The duplication also means the cmd layer bypasses the ResolveType abstraction entirely in the send path, making it inconsistent with the read/view paths.

Better pattern: Remove isPeerHTTPCampfire from send.go and replace calls with transport.ResolveType(m) == transport.TypePeerHTTP, using the Membership struct already loaded from the store.

title: SWEEP-TEST: handleMembership evict — silent auth bypass when GetMembership errors
description:
In pkg/transport/http/handler_message.go, the evict case of handleMembership has a conditional creator check:

  membership, err := h.store.GetMembership(campfireID)
  if err == nil && membership != nil && membership.CreatorPubkey != '' && senderHex != membership.CreatorPubkey {
      http.Error(w, 'only the campfire creator may evict members', http.StatusForbidden)
      return
  }

The check only fires when ALL four conditions are true. If GetMembership returns an error (e.g., DB failure), the evict proceeds without any creator verification. Similarly, if membership.CreatorPubkey is empty (legacy records), the check is skipped entirely.

Missing tests:
1. GetMembership returns an error → evict should be REJECTED (not silently allowed). Currently the evict proceeds.
2. CreatorPubkey is empty string → evict by any member proceeds. This is the intended backward-compat behavior, but it has no test asserting it.
3. Non-creator with a set CreatorPubkey → evict rejected with 403. (This case IS covered by existing auth tests, so only the error path is new.)

Why it matters: This is a security-critical path. If GetMembership fails transiently (DB locked, etc.), any authenticated member can evict any other member, bypassing creator-only access control. The test should confirm the handler rejects the evict on GetMembership failure rather than silently allowing it.
close_reason: Implemented: fail-closed creator check in handleMembership evict — 500 on DB error, 403 for non-creator. Added CreatorPubkey to Membership struct with migration. 4 tests.

title: SWEEP-AP: handler.go violates single responsibility - one file handles 7 HTTP endpoints plus crypto plus store logic
description:
pkg/transport/http/handler.go is 1057 lines and handles all of:
- Message delivery (handleDeliver)
- Sync (handleSync)
- Long-poll (handlePoll) 
- Membership notifications (handleMembership)
- Join handshake with ECDH crypto (handleJoin)
- FROST signing rounds (handleSign) 
- Two-phase rekey protocol with ECDH crypto (handleRekey)
- AES-GCM encryption/decryption helpers
- campfireStateForRekey local type
- recordToMessage conversion
- verifyRequestSignature
- HTTP request routing (route)

Each endpoint has distinct concerns: message storage, crypto key exchange, FROST signing protocol, membership state. This is a textbook god-object violation. The handler should be split into at minimum: message_handler.go, join_handler.go, sign_handler.go, rekey_handler.go.

Severity: MEDIUM - the current size makes it hard to audit individual protocol paths, and tests for one handler are intermixed with others.
close_reason: Split handler.go (1020 lines) into handler_message.go, handler_join.go, handler_sign.go, handler_rekey.go; handler.go is now 61 lines with only routing and shared types. Commit a6acf22.

title: Bug: outputViewProjected silently drops campfire_id, antecedents, provenance, signature projection fields
description:
BUG FINDING from workspace-o3l.7

In cmd/cf/cmd/view.go, outputViewProjected() has a switch statement that handles: id, sender, tags, payload, timestamp, instance. The following valid field names are silently dropped if specified in a view projection:
- campfire_id
- antecedents
- provenance
- signature

This means cf view read with a projection including these fields will silently omit them from output with no error or warning.

File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/view.go:513-539 (outputViewProjected)

Fix: Add cases for campfire_id, antecedents, provenance, signature to the switch statement. OR validate projection fields at view creation time against a known-valid set (relates to workspace-rqt).

Done condition: All valid MessageRecord field names produce output in outputViewProjected. Tests verify campfire_id and antecedents appear in projected output.
close_reason: Deferred: view projection field coverage is P2.

title: SWEEP-AP: Every CLI command copy-pastes identity-load and store-open boilerplate
description:
Pattern: Copy-paste / violated DRY.

Location: cmd/cf/cmd/*.go — every non-trivial command (admit, compact, create, dag, disband, evict, join, leave, member, members, read, send, serve, view) contains the same 8-10 line preamble:

  agentID, err := identity.Load(IdentityPath())
  if err != nil { return fmt.Errorf("loading identity: %w", err) }
  s, err := store.Open(store.StorePath(CFHome()))
  if err != nil { return fmt.Errorf("opening store: %w", err) }
  defer s.Close()

This pattern appears ~14 times across the command files. The only variation is the error message prefix ("loading identity (run cf init first)" vs "loading identity").

Why it matters: Any change to initialization order (e.g., adding a migration, changing the store path logic) must be applied to every command. A command that forgets defer s.Close() leaks a DB connection — a bug that has already occurred and been fixed manually.

Better pattern: Extract a helper such as:
  func requireAgentAndStore() (*identity.Identity, *store.Store, error)

called at the top of each RunE. The defer s.Close() would need to remain in the caller but a single wrapper reduces copy-paste surface by 80%.

title: SWEEP-AP: isPeerHTTPCampfire duplicated across cmd and pkg/transport layers
description:
Pattern: Copy-paste / implicit coupling.

Locations:
- cmd/cf/cmd/send.go:154 — isPeerHTTPCampfire(transportDir, campfireID) checks os.Stat for <id>.cbor
- pkg/transport/transport.go:59 — isPeerHTTPTransportDir(transportDir, campfireID) does the same os.Stat check

The comment in transport.go even says it 'mirrors the cmd-layer isPeerHTTPCampfire check', acknowledging the duplication.

Why it's problematic: The detection heuristic (existence of <id>.cbor in transport dir) is duplicated. If the on-disk layout changes, both sites must be updated. The cmd layer calling os.Stat directly on transport internals is a leaky abstraction — the cmd layer knows the HTTP transport's storage convention.

Suggested direction: Remove isPeerHTTPCampfire from send.go. The cmd layer already calls transport.ResolveType() for routing decisions in send.go:120 and read.go. The os.Stat call in send.go:154 is dead code — it's defined but only used to check if a campfire is p2p-http, which transport.ResolveType already handles. Verify and delete the cmd-layer duplicate.
close_reason: Duplicate of workspace-v5h1 (same finding)

title: SWEEP-TEST: pkg/transport/fs/fs.go has zero test coverage
description:
File: pkg/transport/fs/fs.go

The filesystem transport package has no test file. It is the primary transport used in init/join/leave/send/admit/disband commands and is exercised only indirectly through the cmd-layer integration tests. No unit tests exist for any of the following behaviors:

- Init(): creates directory structure, writes campfire.cbor
- WriteMember() / ListMembers() / RemoveMember(): member lifecycle
- ReadState(): decodes CampfireState from disk; error path on corrupt CBOR
- WriteMessage() / ListMessages(): message ordering (timestamp sort), skip non-.cbor files
- Remove(): removes entire transport directory
- atomicWriteCBOR(): atomic write via temp file + rename — no test for partial write or rename failure
- DefaultBaseDir(): env override via CF_TRANSPORT_DIR

ListMessages silently skips files it cannot read or decode (lines 149-155). If a file is corrupted, it is silently dropped. This is a silent breakage risk: data loss would not cause test failures.

Done condition: a test file pkg/transport/fs/fs_test.go covers the full CRUD lifecycle, ListMessages ordering, error paths, and the CF_TRANSPORT_DIR env var.
close_reason: Fixed: 14 FS transport tests added (befb820)

title: BUG: send.go role check runs before final tags are built — --future/--fulfills flags bypass writer check
description:
Severity: major

In send.go RunE (lines 92-103), checkRoleCanSend(m.Role, sendTags) is called BEFORE the final tags slice is assembled. The --future flag appends "future" and --fulfills appends "fulfills" to tags after the check. While these two tags are not campfire:* system tags (so no security bypass today), the role check is operating on an incomplete tag list. This is a structural bug: the enforcement point does not see the same tags that will be sent.

If any future non-campfire: tag were to be restricted by role, this ordering would silently fail to block it.

Fix: perform checkRoleCanSend on the final assembled tags slice, not on sendTags.

File: cmd/cf/cmd/send.go, lines 92-103.

Done condition: role check in send.go uses the same tags slice passed to the transport functions.
close_reason: Moved checkRoleCanSend() call in send.go to run after final tags slice is assembled (after future/fulfills appended). Regression test: TestSendRoleCheckSeesAssembledTags. Commit: 329703c

title: isCompactionEvent and isCompactionMsg are duplicate functions
description:
FINDING from workspace-o3l.3.3 review. Code quality issue.

pkg/store/store.go defines isCompactionEvent(m MessageRecord) bool
cmd/cf/cmd/compact.go defines isCompactionMsg(m store.MessageRecord) bool

Both do strings.Contains(m.Tags, "campfire:compact"). The store-level function should be used by compact.go, or both should be consolidated into a single exported function on the store package.

Done condition: One function remains. Either isCompactionEvent is exported from store package and compact.go uses it, or compact.go calls a helper it owns and store.go is refactored to not duplicate it.
close_reason: Deferred: cosmetic.

title: SWEEP-AP: campfire.equal() reinvents bytes.Equal
description:
Pattern: Reinventing stdlib.

Location: pkg/campfire/campfire.go:164-174 — equal(a, b []byte) manually loops over bytes comparing them.

The standard library provides bytes.Equal which is equivalent, more readable, and potentially SIMD-optimized.

Why it's problematic: Minor — adds a function that every future reader has to understand before realizing it's just bytes.Equal. Mildly misleading because it suggests there's something special about the comparison.

Suggested direction: Replace equal(a, b) calls with bytes.Equal(a, b) directly. Remove the equal() function.

title: SWEEP-TEST: handleMembership unknown event type returns 400 but is untested
description: handler_message.go lines 301-311: the handleMembership switch has a default case that returns 400 for unknown event types. No test sends an unknown event type (e.g., 'kick', 'ban') to verify the 400 response. Additionally: (a) no test verifies that a 'join' event with an empty endpoint is silently ignored (the code checks event.Endpoint \!= '' before calling AddPeer — behavior is correct but untested); (b) no test verifies that an 'evict' event correctly calls RemovePeer (only 'leave' is tested in TestMembershipNotification). Done condition: tests for unknown event type → 400, join with empty endpoint → 200 with no peer added, evict event → peer removed.

title: SWEEP-SEC: Message payload treated as trusted UTF-8 and printed directly — potential terminal injection
description:
Severity: LOW

Message payloads are printed directly to stdout without sanitization:
- read.go line 147: fmt.Printf("  %s\n\n", string(m.Payload))
- read.go line 214: fmt.Printf("  %s\n", string(m.Payload))
- printNATMessages: fmt.Fprintf(w, "  %s\n\n", string(m.Payload))

Payloads can contain:
1. ANSI terminal escape sequences — terminal control sequences injected via message payload can overwrite screen content, move cursor, change colors, or in some terminals execute code
2. Null bytes — may truncate output in some contexts

This is a classic "terminal injection" / "ANSI injection" vulnerability. An attacker who can send a message to a campfire (even one the victim reads) can inject escape sequences that appear in the victim\s terminal.

Example: payload "\x1b[2J\x1b[H" clears the victim\s terminal. More sophisticated sequences can overwrite previously displayed text, hiding malicious activity.

Relevant code:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/read.go lines 147, 214, 843, 858

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.
close_reason: Fixed: added sanitizePayload() function that strips ESC and other control characters; applied at all three human-readable print sites

title: Bug: --pull output missing instance display in both human and JSON modes
description:
In cmd/cf/cmd/read.go runPull() function:

1. Human-readable output (line 732): senderDisplay is set to "agent:" + senderShort but does NOT append " (" + m.Instance + ")" when instance is non-empty. Compare with printMessages() lines 188-190 which correctly shows instance.

2. JSON output (lines 680-689): The jsonMsg struct does not include an Instance field, while the one-shot read JSON struct (line 429) does include Instance string with json:"instance,omitempty".

Fix: Add instance display to both human-readable and JSON output paths in runPull().

title: SWEEP-TEST: handleSync recordToMessage errors silently drop messages
description: handleSync in handler_message.go silently skips records where recordToMessage fails (malformed sender hex). Caller receives 200 with partial data and no indication of data loss. Same issue in handlePoll respondWithRecords helper. Missing tests: (1) malformed Sender hex record is silently omitted from sync response, (2) valid record appears in response. Source: pkg/transport/http/handler_message.go lines 68-74.

title: SWEEP-SEC: rekey handler allows any member to trigger campfire ID rename and evict arbitrary peers
description: handleRekey() in pkg/transport/http/handler_rekey.go verifies the sender is a current member of the campfire, but does not verify that the sender is the creator/owner. Any member can call POST /campfire/{id}/rekey with a phase-2 payload containing: (1) a new_campfire_id of their choosing, (2) an evicted_member_pubkey to remove any peer, (3) arbitrary encrypted key material (or a phase-1 token obtained beforehand). The handler at lines 229-241 will rename all records in the database (UpdateCampfireID) and at lines 244-246 delete the specified peer from the peer list. An evicted member who still has network access could re-evict the person who evicted them, or any other member. Combined with the fact that the handler does not verify the RekeyMessageCBOR signature against the OLD campfire key (line 263 uses rekeyMsg.VerifySignature() which validates against rekeyMsg.Sender which is attacker-controlled), a member can forge a rekey announcement and disrupt the campfire. Impact: member-level privilege escalation — any full member can evict any other member, including the creator, by sending a crafted rekey request.
close_reason: Already fixed: handleRekey has creator-only check — non-creator gets 403.

title: Test gap: runViewRead negation predicate leaks system messages (no test)
description:
## Gap

There is no test verifying that campfire:view definition messages do NOT appear in view results when using negation predicates or predicates that incidentally match system messages.

The existing TestViewRead_PredicateFilters test manually filters isViewMsg in its own evaluation loop — but it does not call runViewRead directly, so it doesn't catch the bug in the actual command implementation.

## Fix
Add a test that:
1. Creates a view with a negation predicate like (not (tag "foo"))
2. Adds normal messages and a campfire:view message
3. Calls runViewRead (or the underlying materialization logic directly)
4. Asserts that the output does not include any campfire:view messages

File: cmd/cf/cmd/view_test.go

Done condition: A failing test is added that demonstrates campfire:view messages leaking into view results, which then passes once the fix in workspace-316 is applied.
close_reason: Fixed: TestViewRead_NegationPredicateExcludesSystemMessages added to view_test.go, verifies negation predicate (not (tag "foo")) doesn't leak campfire:view system messages. Commit 413cd15.

title: SWEEP-AP: message printing logic split across printMessagesWithFields and printNATMessages
description:
Two nearly identical message printing functions exist in cmd/cf/cmd/read.go:
- printMessagesWithFields() (lines 75-218): renders store.MessageRecord slices
- printNATMessages() (lines 826-860): renders message.Message slices

Both produce identical human-readable output (campfire prefix, timestamp, sender display with instance, tags line, antecedents truncated to 8 chars, payload). The duplication exists because NAT-mode messages arrive as message.Message (not yet persisted to store), but the display logic is the same.

This creates an SRP violation: the human-readable format is defined in two places. Any display change (format, field ordering, new field like campfire_id color coding) must be applied to both. 

A shared printMessageFields(cfID, sender, instance, ts, tags, antecedents, payload string) helper would centralize the format. Severity: MEDIUM - display bugs will be inconsistent across read modes.
close_reason: Extracted printSingleMessage() kernel shared by printMessagesWithFields and printNATMessages. Commit a6acf22.

title: SWEEP-BUG: evictThresholdN counts remainingPeers incorrectly for new DKG participant count
description:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/cmd/cf/cmd/evict.go, evictThresholdN (line 257)

n := uint(len(remainingPeers))

remainingPeers is built from store peer_endpoints excluding the evicted member (line 99-104). However, the creator/self is included in remainingPeers if they have a stored peer endpoint, but excluded from delivery (line 326: skips agentID.PublicKeyHex()). 

The DKG is run with n participants and participant IDs [1..n]. The creator is assigned participant 1 (line 314). The remaining non-self peers get IDs starting at 2 (line 316-321).

Problem: if the creator also has a stored peer_endpoint entry (which is stored during join/serve), then len(remainingPeers) includes the creator. The DKG runs for n participants. But then peerToParticipantID skips agentID.PublicKeyHex() when incrementing nextPID (line 317-321), so peers get IDs 2..n. The creator gets ID 1. But the DKG was run with n participants — and if the creator IS in remainingPeers, those n slots are correctly filled.

If the creator is NOT in remainingPeers (no self peer_endpoint), n would be the count of other peers only. The creator is not counted, so the DKG runs for n+1 participants (n other peers + 1 creator) but n was set to just the count of other peers. In this case the DKG runs with fewer participants than expected: participantIDs = [1..n] but there are actually n+1 members.

Actually: n = len(remainingPeers) where remainingPeers includes all non-evicted peers from peer_endpoints. The creator may or may not be in peer_endpoints. If the creator has no endpoint stored (did not call serve or join with --listen), they would not appear in remainingPeers. The DKG would then run with n participants (only the other members), assigning IDs 1..n. Creator gets ID 1, others get 2..n. But the creator is the n+1-th participant that was NOT counted in n.

This means the DKG is run with n participants instead of n+1, producing shares for n people, but the creator is also using participant ID 1. The creator share (dkgResults[1]) is valid for a DKG of n participants, but the distributed shares go to n-1 other peers (IDs 2..n), leaving 1 uncounted.

Net effect: with t-of-n threshold and creator not in peer_endpoints, the DKG produces a (t-1)-of-(n-1) system missing the creator from the participant count. The subsequent signing would fail because the group public key does not match the expected members.
close_reason: Fixed: evictThresholdN now includes self in participant count (worktree-agent-a699bc1e)

title: Security: predicate evaluator has no recursion depth limit — stack overflow possible
description:
SECURITY FINDING from workspace-o3l.6

The predicate evaluator in pkg/predicate/predicate.go uses mutual recursion (eval → eval) with no depth limit. A malformed predicate like (and (and (and ... 10000 deep ...))) will overflow the Go stack and crash the process.

Affected: pkg/predicate/predicate.go eval() function
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/predicate/predicate.go

Fix: Add a depth counter to parseExpr and eval. Return an error at Parse time if depth > 50 (reasonable max for real predicates).

Done condition: Parse() returns an error for predicates nested deeper than a configurable limit. Tests verify the limit is enforced.
close_reason: Fixed: MaxDepth=64 with ErrDepthExceeded in predicate evaluator. Commit e64a658.

title: SWEEP-BUG: ListMessages RespectCompaction filters by afterTimestamp but compaction events may predate cursor
description:
File: /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/store/store.go, ListMessages (line 314-388)

When RespectCompaction=true, ListMessages:
1. Fetches messages WHERE timestamp > afterTimestamp (line 321-323)
2. Fetches ALL compaction events via collectSupersededIDs (line 371-377) which calls ListCompactionEvents

ListCompactionEvents has NO afterTimestamp filter -- it fetches ALL campfire:compact events regardless of when they were created (line 431-459).

This creates an asymmetry: if a compaction event was created BEFORE the afterTimestamp cursor, it will not appear in the filtered msgs list (correct -- its timestamp is too old). But it WILL appear in the superseded set (via collectSupersededIDs), so messages it supersedes would be filtered out of the result.

Scenario: 
- Messages M1, M2 created at T=100, T=200
- Compaction event C1 created at T=300, superseding M1 and M2
- User reads with afterTimestamp=400 (has already seen C1 but wants any NEW messages)
- New message M3 created at T=500

ListMessages(campfireID, 400, RespectCompaction=true) would:
- Fetch msgs where timestamp > 400: only M3
- Collect superseded from ALL compaction events: {M1.ID, M2.ID} from C1
- Filter: M3 is not in superseded, so M3 is returned. Correct.

This scenario is fine. But consider:
- User reads with afterTimestamp=250 (cursor between M2 and C1)
- Compaction C1 exists at T=300

ListMessages(campfireID, 250, RespectCompaction=true):
- Fetch msgs where timestamp > 250: C1 (at T=300) and M3 (at T=500)
- collectSupersededIDs includes M1.ID and M2.ID (from C1)
- C1 is included (isCompactionEvent), M3 is included
- M1 and M2 were already before the cursor so not in result anyway

Still fine. The bug scenario:
- Cursor at T=350 (past C1)
- NEW compaction C2 at T=450 superseding M3 (which was at T=500... wait M3 would not be superseded yet)

Let me reconsider: the real edge case is when multiple compaction events exist and the cursor is between them. The superseded set from ALL compaction events includes messages superseded by OLDER compactions that were before the cursor. When the user fetches messages AFTER the cursor (expecting to see new messages), the compaction events before the cursor still affect the filter.

This is actually CORRECT behavior -- you want to see the current non-superseded state even for new messages. The bug is different: collectSupersededIDs could be very expensive for campfires with many compaction events because it fetches and parses ALL compaction payloads on every ListMessages call with RespectCompaction. There is no indexing or caching of the superseded set. For a campfire with many compaction events and thousands of superseded IDs, this is an O(events * ids) operation on every read.
close_reason: Fixed: collectSupersededIDs now caches the superseded-ID set per campfire keyed by max compaction timestamp. Cache miss only on new compaction events. Cross-campfire queries fall through uncached. Committed in befb820.

title: SWEEP-TEST: handleDeliver lacks server-side message signature verification
description:
File: pkg/transport/http/handler.go handleDeliver()

handleDeliver authenticates the HTTP request (X-Campfire-Sender header) but does NOT verify the inner message-level Ed25519 signature in the CBOR body. A valid member can deliver a message with a forged payload and the server stores it unconditionally.

title: SWEEP-BUG: evictThreshold1 removes old state file before UpdateCampfireID — inconsistent state if DB update fails
description:
File: cmd/cf/cmd/evict.go, evictThreshold1(), lines 219-227

The ordering in evictThreshold1 is:
1. Write new state file (newCampfireID.cbor) — line 219
2. Remove old state file (oldCampfireID.cbor) — line 222
3. s.UpdateCampfireID(oldCampfireID, newCampfireID) — line 225

If step 3 (UpdateCampfireID) fails after step 2 completes:
- The old .cbor file is gone
- The DB still contains campfire_id = oldCampfireID
- The new .cbor file exists with the new key

The creator's local store is now permanently inconsistent:
- GetMembership(oldCampfireID) still works (DB has old ID)
- The state file referenced for crypto operations now has newCampfireID as its name but the DB key is oldCampfireID
- Any subsequent operation that joins these (e.g., reading campfire state by membership TransportDir) will fail because TransportDir still references oldCampfireID

Note: handleRekey (the receiver side) already fixed this ordering (workspace-fn8), but evictThreshold1 (the creator side) has the same bug in the opposite direction.

Conditions: SQLite write failure (disk full, journal mode error, locked DB) after the state file swap.

Fix: Reorder to:
1. Write new state file (newCampfireID.cbor)
2. UpdateCampfireID in DB — fail fast before removing old file
3. Only on DB success: remove old state file

This matches the fix already applied to handleRekey.
close_reason: Implemented: reorder evictThreshold1/evictThresholdN to call UpdateCampfireID before os.Remove. Commit fcaa842, pushed.

title: SWEEP-SEC: HTTP server has no request size limits, timeouts, or rate limiting — DoS surface
description:
Severity: HIGH

The HTTP transport server (New() in transport.go, listen.go) creates an http.Server with no:
- ReadTimeout / WriteTimeout / IdleTimeout
- MaxHeaderBytes limit
- Request body size limit (io.ReadAll used on all endpoints without MaxBytesReader)

All five request handlers call io.ReadAll(r.Body) without limit:
- handleDeliver: line 122 — attacker sends 10GB CBOR body, exhausts memory
- handleMembership: line 382
- handleJoin: line 442
- handleSign: line 624
- handleRekey: line 813

Additionally, no rate limiting on:
- /join: each call triggers DKG computation or key lookup
- /sign: each call creates signing sessions (stored in-memory map)
- /poll: limited to 64 per campfire by PollBroker, but campfire IDs are attacker-controlled — attacker creates 1000 campfire IDs and sends 64 poll requests each

Relevant code:
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/transport.go lines 72-93 (no timeouts on http.Server)
- /home/baron/projects/campfire/pkg/transport/http/handler.go all io.ReadAll calls

Done condition: A bead exists and is tracked. No fix in this sweep.
close_reason: Fixed: http.Server configured with ReadTimeout=30s, WriteTimeout=60s, IdleTimeout=120s in New(). http.MaxBytesReader(4MiB) applied to all handler body reads (deliver, membership, join, sign, rekey). HTTPServer() exported for testability. Tests TestServerTimeoutsConfigured and TestRequestBodySizeLimit verify.

title: SWEEP-AP: isPrivateIP re-parses all CIDR networks on every invocation
description:
Pattern: Unnecessary repeated work / inefficient implementation.

Location: pkg/transport/http/handler.go lines 72-99 (isPrivateIP).

On every call, isPrivateIP allocates a 12-element string slice, then calls net.ParseCIDR 12 times (each allocates a *net.IPNet), iterates them, and discards all results. Called once per join request during SSRF validation.

The CIDR list is a static constant — it never changes. All 12 *net.IPNet values could be computed once at package init using sync.Once or a package-level var initialized in an init() function.

Why it matters: Moderate inefficiency in the join path. More importantly, it is an antipattern that obscures the static nature of the data. Any reader who sees the function may assume the list is dynamic and fail to notice the allocation cost.

Better pattern: Parse the CIDR list into a package-level var privateNets []*net.IPNet at init time and iterate over it in isPrivateIP with no per-call parsing.

title: SWEEP-SEC: no replay protection on HTTP request signatures — captured requests can be re-submitted
description: The Ed25519 request signature scheme (verifyRequestSignature in handler_message.go line 318) signs only the request body bytes. There is no nonce, timestamp, or request ID in the signed payload. Once a valid signed request is intercepted (e.g., by a passive network observer, or a compromised peer), it can be replayed indefinitely to any node that accepts it. Concretely: a captured POST /campfire/{id}/deliver with a valid signature can be re-sent to any other peer who is a member of the same campfire. The server has no way to detect this is a replay rather than a fresh message. For the deliver endpoint, the AddMessage store function uses INSERT OR IGNORE, so duplicate message IDs are deduplicated. But for other endpoints (membership events, sign rounds, rekey), replaying can cause side effects: re-replaying a 'leave' membership event removes a member from the peer list, a replayed sign round 1 creates a new ghost signing session, a replayed rekey phase-1 floods the rekeySessions cache. Mitigation: include a per-request nonce and timestamp in the signed payload and reject requests with stale timestamps (e.g., >60 seconds old).

title: SWEEP-TEST: identity.Load - invalid key size paths not fully tested
description:
File: pkg/identity/identity.go Load()

Load() validates key sizes after JSON unmarshal: checks public key is 32 bytes and private key is 64 bytes. TestLoadInvalidFile only tests non-JSON input. It does NOT test:

- Valid JSON but wrong PublicKey size (e.g., 31 bytes): should return "invalid public key size"
- Valid JSON but wrong PrivateKey size (e.g., 63 bytes): should return "invalid private key size"
- Valid JSON with correct sizes but the private key seed does not correspond to the public key: Load() does NOT detect this (silent security gap - wrong keypair loaded without error)

The last point is a security gap: a corrupted identity file where private and public keys are mismatched would load successfully. The first sign/verify would produce an incorrect result but no error.

Done: identity_test.go adds tests for malformed key sizes and documents (with a test comment) that key consistency is not verified on load.
close_reason: Fixed: added tests for Load with short public key (not 32 bytes) and short private key (not 64 bytes) in identity_test.go

title: SWEEP-SEC: rekey phase-2 skips HKDF — raw X25519 shared secret used as AES key in evict path
description: In cmd/cf/cmd/evict.go, deliverRekey() computes the shared secret via ECDH at line 577 (senderPriv.ECDH(receiverPub)) and passes it directly to evictAESGCMEncrypt() at lines 592-603. The raw 32-byte X25519 output is used as the AES-256-GCM key directly, without HKDF. Compare with the join/rekey handler-side code in pkg/transport/http/handler_rekey.go which correctly calls hkdfSHA256(rawShared, 'campfire-rekey-v1') before using the key. The mismatch means the evict-side encryption uses raw X25519 output while the handler-side decryption applies HKDF before decrypting — they would produce different keys and decryption would fail. More importantly, using raw X25519 output directly violates NIST SP 800-56C: raw DH output is not a uniformly random key suitable for direct use in AES. Impact: during eviction, key material (new campfire private key or FROST share) encrypted by deliverRekey() cannot be decrypted by handleRekey() (they use different key derivations). This also means eviction silently fails to deliver key material to remaining peers, effectively bricking the campfire after any eviction.
close_reason: Already fixed: evict.go applies HKDF to raw ECDH output. Same fix as workspace-a4j.

title: follow mode JSON output with --fields not implemented

In read.go --follow loop: new messages are printed via printMessagesWithFields(filteredMessages, s, fieldSet). However, the --json output path (jsonOutput=true) is never triggered in the follow loop. There is no JSON output in follow mode at all, with or without --fields.
